// ISSUE TITLE: Add shader `#include` support
// ISSUE REPORT:
I've got lots of random functions that I keep copying from one shader to another. `#include` would be a great help.

Since this is not necessarily supported by all drivers, I would need to implement it myself.
This shouldn't be too hard, since it's essentially just a basic search for `"#include ...\n"`

A problem might be that there is less autocomplete support. But that's likely not too big of a deal. I could for example give each file the same name as the function they contain.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Since the removal of voxel models this is not really a big issue anymore. Furthermore it would conflict with #303.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We already have a lot of duplicate buffers in the code which is difficult to maintain.
This is going to get worse with further Vulkan progress #3335.

// ISSUE TITLE: simple_vegetation places single blocks on ceilings weirdly // ISSUE REPORT: ### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior Using the following, the block will be placed one block down from the actual ceiling. This doesn't happen for longer simple vegetations (ex. the glimmer worms in glimmergill caves) ``` .{ .id = "cubyz:simple_vegetation", .block = "cubyz:cave_sprouts:1", .generationMode = .ceiling, .chance = 0.5, .height = 0, .height_variation = 0, }, ``` // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Does it work when you set `.height = 1`? I think the value 0 may be the problem. [USER COMMENT] > Does it work when you set `.height = 1`? I think the value 0 may be the problem. At height 1 i get this. Which is too long and still not on the ceiling.

As discussed here: https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/pull/3218#discussion_r3485817241

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Make them drop Rupees sometimes too XD

[USER COMMENT]
How do the tags `.cuttable` for sickles and `.sliceable` for swords sound before I add it to all foliage?

[USER COMMENT]
Also, woody blocks like mushroom blocks and cactus are special. Both sickles and axes are effective against them, and they drop items for both. Should using swords also be effective against them and let them drop items? I'd say yes for both

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

As for cactus/giant mushroom I'm not sure. It should definitely drop the item, since it also drops it when broken by hand.

// ISSUE TITLE: Items are lost if the game closes unexpectedly and there are items in the procedural crafting grid
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior

If the game closes unexpectedly (crash, user kills the process, etc) and any items are in the procedural crafting window, since those items are no longer in the players' inventory, they are effectively lost.

My suggestion to fix this would be that placing items in the procedural crafting window's inventory shouldn't affect the stored data of the player's inventory until after an item has been crafted. Then the items used should be subtracted from the player's inventory data.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This is really difficult to fix.

> My suggestion to fix this would be that placing items in the procedural crafting window's inventory shouldn't affect the stored data of the player's inventory until after an item has been crafted.

What if a friend drops you some diamonds in the mean-time? With your approach those would now get lost instead.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Well, that is not that hard to fix if workbench behaved like a chest. I think there was idea like this before?

[USER COMMENT]
> This is really difficult to fix.
> 
> > My suggestion to fix this would be that placing items in the procedural crafting window's inventory shouldn't affect the stored data of the player's inventory until after an item has been crafted.
> 
> What if a friend drops you some diamonds in the mean-time? With your approach those would now get lost instead.

I think #2052 can not be easily fixed for the same reason. Maybe the items could not leave the inventory at all, until the item being crafted is actually crafted, or until the item in cursor is dropped? Maybe the item in cursor/crafting grid can be just a "pointer" into inventory? I would love trying to implement that, @IntegratedQuantum  if you think that would be a good idea.

[USER COMMENT]
> What if a friend drops you some diamonds in the mean-time? With your approach those would now get lost instead.

Maybe there's a separate per-player procedural crafting inventory that is moved back into that player's primary inventory when they reconnect after an unexpected disconnect. They'd just drop the items they can't fit on the ground, if that situation arose.
When they close the crafting window without crafting anything, those items return to their inventory as they currently do. But during the use of the crafting window those items are really stored in a more persistent way via this secondary crafting inventory.

This does have the quirk that people can load an alternate profile with potentially up to 25 slots of item stacks on top of the usual inventory and have them disconnect mid-craft to keep it stored in that invisible inventory, but since we can create infinite profiles anyway, I don't think it's an issue of fairness or whatever.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> I would love trying to implement that, @IntegratedQuantum if you think that would be a good idea.

I think this, dragged items and items in the grid being pointers in the original inventory, could negatively impact the UI, as it would leave behind slots that can't be interacted with because they are already occupied by an item stored elsewhere.

[USER COMMENT]
> I think this, dragged items and items in the grid being pointers in the original inventory, could negatively impact the UI, as it would leave behind slots that can't be interacted with because they are already occupied by an item stored elsewhere.

Maybe there could be semitransparent placeholder shown where the item actually exists if the item slot is occupied by item that is being pointed to from cursor or from crafting grid to make everything perfectly clear. I think the clearness would make that a non-issue. 

It might actually be better UX, because currently if you exit crafting grid with items inside it, the items will just be put arbitrarily where game decides new items go, instead of the items going back to their original intentory slot.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Well I've never seen a game that does this. So at the very least it will be confusing for new players.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Fixes of #3018 will likely also end up fixing this.

// ISSUE TITLE: Unable to build and run vulkan window MacOS
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior

I would like to be able to work on the Vulkan migration steps. But am running into issues where I can't get to opening the vulkan window

When running `zig build run` with 
```zig
pub var vulkanTestingWindow: bool = true;
```
Results in this output:
```
zig build run 
[info]: Starting game client with version 0.1.0-dev
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Couldn't rename thread: Unsupported
[error]: Vulkan is not supported. Please update your drivers if you want to keep playing Cubyz in the future.
[error]: GLFW Error(65543): Requested OpenGL version 4.6, got version 2.1
thread 37502695 panic: Failed to create GLFW window
```

I suspect that the issue is due to this line in `build.zig`
```zig
exe.addRPath(.{.cwd_relative = "/usr/local/GL/lib"});
```
Of which, the `/usr/local/GL/lib` path doesnt exist for me.

Is there a library that is expected to be installed here?

If so. I would be happy to install and test - then add some error handling to the build script for macos, with instructions for users to install `xyz` library.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Yes, currently it's missing MoltenVK and some other stuff to build on macos. @blackedout01 is currently trying to figure that part out.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think this was fixed in #2351

// ISSUE TITLE: Anistropic Filtering Errors // ISSUE REPORT: ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d2fe911a-940a-4865-a858-39c88ebe248a) // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] This has nothing to do with anisotropic filtering. It's just a result of not allowing partial transparency for these faces, so the game will just use a dithering pattern instead, which is bad for low resolutions obviously. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Should be easy to fix by just simply making pixels transparent in mip maps if any of their parent pixels is transparent. Note that this should only be done for flowers/grass, but not leaves which we want to turn opaque in the distance anyways. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] This was fixed a while ago, With anisotropic filtering enabled it looks correct now.

// ISSUE TITLE: Block Breaking
// ISSUE REPORT:
Blocks are broken when it takes enough damage. The amount of damage saves for the last few blocks you interact with, but will be overridden when you break other blocks.
Damage dealt to the block depends on a few factors:
- The block's health
- The block's hardness
- The tool's power
- The tool's type

Breaking should be accomplished via auto-swing rather than block progress. In theory this method might sync better in servers, and it could allow you to track durability per-hit rather than per-block broken. 

Having durability drain per-hit would discourage the player from swinging their tools all willy-nilly. Nothing gets into the players head more than hitting a block that's too hard and it just wastes away their durability. Also, this would prevent having to make block-health reset when switching tools.

### **Related issues:**
- #620
- #232

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
While this could open up some really interesting possibilities, I'm not sure if it's worth the cost. I fear that this will cause too many mesh updates on the client due to the random tick regeneration.

I think many of the advantages you have here could also be achieved by just storing the health of just one block per entity/player.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Now that I think of it, it would just be a good idea to store it per player. Could also potentially sync that with the server so two people can work on the same block

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I believe that's what Terraria does. It just stores the last few blocks you've mined

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Updated this post to be about Block Breaking in general

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
all points are already implemented or tracked by other issues

// ISSUE TITLE: Authentication without a central authentication server
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Design Goals
- Clients should not be able to impersonate other clients (and gain access to their character/inventory).
- Servers should not be able to silently relay the login process to gain a session on another server

### Preliminaries
- Each player has a public/private key pair. Ideally these are presented to the user as a seed phrase, so we always have the option to switch seamlessly to a new algorithm in case the old algorithm got compromised.
- The protocol should use some form of encryption past the initial key exchange to avoid a man in the middle from tampering with the packets once the session is established

### Login Process
- TLS initialization (without certificates)
- the server asks the client to sign the TLS packets in various key-pair configurations (to provide migration options)
- The client signs the TLS packets and sends the signatures and related public keys back to the server
- The server uses the public key of the preferred method to find the player they belong to
- If no player is found, the public keys of the old methods are used to look for player

<details>

<summary>Obsolete public key encryption proposal</summary>

- The client sends its public key to the server
- The servers uses the clients public key to encrypt a challenge, the encryption type and a key for the remaining encryption process.
- The client decrypts the message and sends the challenge data back to the server (using the now encrypted channel)
- The server decrypts the data and uses it to verify the client's ownership of the public key
- The server then uses the public key to look up the player data, if it doesn't exist yet it creates a new file

</details>

### Limitations
- Without server certificates a man in the middle could still hook into your connection, however it would be impossible to do so without providing a different public key, so the original account is not under attack. Of course if an attacker were to do this over a long period of time, then they could take over your account eventually, but it seems unreasonable to do this in a block game, so I don't think we need to worry about it.

<details>

<summary>Old proposal</summary>
To my surprise it seems like this is actually possible

### Design Goals
- The client should be able to log in with a single password and username(or another identifier, to be decided) to all servers
- A malicious server must not be able to steal your credentials

### Registration
1. The client sends its username/id to the server
2. The server creates a new private/public key pair for the username/id and stores them in the player file.
3. The server sends the public key to the client
4. The client generates a new password for this particular server
5. The client stores its password alongside the server public key and encrypts them using the player's password
6. The client sends the encrypted password+public key back to the server
7. The server stores the encrypted password+public key in the player file
8. The client logs in using the method below

### Login
1. The client sends its username/id to the server
2. The server looks up the encrypted password+public key using the username/id and sends it to the client
3. The client decrypts the per-server password and public key using the player's private password
4. The client now encrypts the per-server password with the server's public key, note that only the real server can decrypt the password.
5. The server decrypts the password computes a hash and compares it with the stored hash, if no hash is stored yet (because we are in the registration process) it stores it now.

### Remaining problems
- The server knows the public, the per server password and knows the player-password encrypted version of these.
Together this may be enough to brute-force the player's password.
→ A sufficiently secure encrpytion algorithms needs to be used to prevent this.
- If a malicious server relays this handshake to a target server, then it can steal your account for a session there.
→ A solution could be to send the IP the client connected to alongside the per server password in step 4. This would allow the server to identify if the client directly connected to it or chose an indirect path.
- If the world is shared with other players, then the per player private keys would be public. This would allow a malicious server to decrypt your per-server password, allowing them to join the original server.
→ It would make sense to store the private key somewhere separate from the world itself to still allow world downloads. Furthermore a mechanism to re-register a player on the world copy is needed.
- Anyone could create a new account under your username/id on a server and you wouldn't be able to claim it back
→ I don't think this is even solvable. It would be rather annoying to the player if this happens, but they could just change their name in that case.

### Sources
I took some inspiration from http://www.peerson.net/papers/passwordsP2P.pdf

</details>

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Anyone could create a new account under your username/id on a server and you wouldn't be able to claim it back
→ I don't think this is even solvable. It would be rather annoying to the player if this happens, but they could just change their name in that case.

This is an advantage and disadvantage at once. On one hand it means someone can claim your username (but you can always choose a different one), but on the other hand it means that you can't globally claim rare usernames and sell them. Additionally as practice shows usernames most likely to get stolen (streamers, internet celebrities) are also ones which are most likely to change their usernames often to NOT get recognized.

The problem I see is that they can't just change their username if they are playing with this one on different servers, or they will lose all their stuff, they even may run into more conflicts, which means they may claim even more names before they find one that is not used on any of the servers they play on. That sounds like a very bad cycle.

To solve the inconvenience part of the problem we could just add server list with username configurable on server basis with global username being the default.

However problem of claiming multiple usernames is much harder to solve, rather impossible without external authority like phone numbers or emails

[USER COMMENT]
We could have display names separate from usernames, but that might be over complicating things.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Why does the public private key pair have to be per player?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> We could have display names separate from usernames, but that might be over complicating things.

That unfortunately has some other implications. To avoid situations where players on the servers are pretending to be someone else then they are you would have to display both and this creates UI challenges both for chat and for user name labels. For PvP servers it's a difference between life and death.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Why does the public private key pair have to be per player?

It might not be strictly necessary, but if it is global then it could be brute-forced, allowing you to decrypt the per-server player password of every player, if you get access to the encrypted version. However if it's per player, then you cannot brute force it, since you only know it if you intercept the initial registration.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
What if we used Sha 256 hashed nanosecond timestamps of when the game was first run as player names? Ofc we would communicate to the player "hey, it's your identity, save it or you will lose all your shit". Then we would display only first few hex digits, as many as necessary for it to be unique on that server (like git) + display name. Basically a tag. How hard is it to have same nanosecond timestamp or Sha 256 as someone else?
This is only for the display name problem ofc. If someone steals it it kind of sucks, but there is still password to cover your existing accounts.
It's just a tiny bit better than copying whole server list.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Actually I think we should just keep it at names. Sure renaming is a bit of a pain on servers you are already active on (you need to log on with both and swap over your inventory and fix up all the totem permissions), however I think this is better than using random ids since it easy to forget about them. Especially since it is kind of unusual for games to use a random id like this.

Furthermore if someone does steal your ID then it is rather difficult to change it and remember which one to use for which server.

It is much easier to remember names, and name variants, in the worst case you may just need to go through your aliases in order and try them all until one gets accepted by the server. Nowadays people do seem to have many names already, since on many platforms you cannot choose the one you like.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
As we already discussed in discord, there is no way to change master password after it's set. Since the name is claimed you are no longer able to re-claim it. Sure, there are plenty of good practices regarding passwords which would reduce the likelihood of this problem, but the whole problem with passwords comes from the fact that people don't follow those guidelines.

What about clean installation/ hardware change? Will it be possible to re-claim your accounts only knowing your password?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> What about clean installation/ hardware change? Will it be possible to re-claim your accounts only knowing your password?

Yes of course, that's why the server password is stored on the server.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> The server knows the public, the per server password and knows the player-password encrypted version of these.
Together this may be enough to brute-force the player's password.

Maybe instead of hashing the player's server password we can use another private/public key pair, this would mean the server only knows the public keys (which could be encrypted again with the private key) and not the full contents of the encrypted data, and thus cannot perform a plaintext attack.

[USER COMMENT]
Quick interrogation, I can see in the registration process, at step 4, that in addition to the username/password, the client should keep a per-server password.
In a case where I'm playing on another computer, with the same username/password credentials, and the server has already an entry for my credentials, but I don't have the server password on disk, what would happen ?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
the per-server password is stored encrypted on the server.

[USER COMMENT]
I'm interested in the reason for pushing against a central auth server. It would undoubtedly be the simplest and likely most secure approach. 

The immediate idea to come to mind if you yourself don't want to host a central auth server would be to have a federated auth server that multiple individuals can spin up themselves, then allow server hosts to select which auth server they want to abide by. As more servers join the federation the username pool is synced and hopefully an organic auth network is formed. Then in the event someone doesn't want to opt into that network comes along they can either spin up their own without federating or default back to username authentication.

Not a perfect solution, but I have to imagine there are already existing tools that could facilitate this approach.

[USER COMMENT]
I think it would make sense to do about the opposite:

1. The client has a public/private key pair (in separate files, like ssh keys).
2. The client sends its public key to the server. This functions as the unique identifier for the player (instead of the username).
3. The server sends some string to encrypt to the client
4. The client encrypts it using its private key and sends that back
5. The server verifies it with the public key that was sent
6. The server now knows for certain that the client has access to the full keypair

This also lets us make allowlists. Simply put the public keys or hashes of them in a file on the server, and only those people are let in! Along with that, it has the advantage of being cryptographically secure and not dependent on a likely insecure password. The player would, to use their account across devices, have to simply copy the keypair from one device to the other. We could have files for that that the game lets you easily export and import ("Export account", "Import account").

- Username/password pairs are easy to understand.
- It gets confusing if they are unique per-server.
- It gets more confusing if there's also a server password.
- Connection details are (also) easy to forget / lose.

See Luanti as an example. This could be solved with good UX, but the complexity is still there.

Proposed UX for a public/private key oriented solution:

- Launch the game for the first time.
- "Multiplayer" button is grayed out.
- Hovering over it shows a tooltip that an "identity" must be created.
- There is an "Identity" button in one of the corners.
- Next to it, player model/head could be displayed.
- Clicking it leads to another screen / opens a popup.
- Here you can "Create New Identity" and "Import" as well as "Open Folder".
- Optionally, identities should be able to be protected by a password.
- Once an identity is created / selected, you can also customize your name / appearance.
- You can switch between identities, "Export" and "Delete" (with warning).

With the right information on screen, such as displaying a filename, this should make it straight-forward enough for people to understand that their identities are tied to the files. Servers could (by default) lock in the first seen username (minus color codes) so changing them is only possible with admin permission. Similarly, lost identities could be recovered by letting the admin move player data between identities easily – could be as easy as renaming a file storing the per-player information.

RE: Recovery methods for asymmetric keypairs: 

Many password managers/Crypto wallets/etc utilize recovery phrases. Usually comprised of a series of seemingly random words.

When generating a new identity, we can prompt the user to save the recovery key and disable the acknowledge button until they confirm with a checkbox or something that they've saved it somewhere secure. (Like a password manager, or just an external drive, or something.)

I think this would give us a great balance between usability (enter the recovery key/phrase/whatever to regenerate that identity on a new machine) and security (high-entropy asymmetric keys rather than low-entropy user-provided passwords).

We should also encourage the user to set a password to encrypt the key with, and perhaps give the option to _not_ encrypt it but make it very clear that it's not recommended. (I hope it's obvious why we should guide users against unencrypted storage of a private key, but I don't expect the users to understand intuitively).

// ISSUE TITLE: Item Migration doesn't work for player inventories
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior

Player inventories use a human-readable format and store the clear-text item ids.
Player data must remain in a human-readable format, and inventories need to be too, so I can easily look for and patch cheated items.

We have two options here:
1. We can change the format (which requires bumping the world save format version and adding a migration path, which can be removed in the future) to store palette indices instead of ids. This would still allow looking for cheated items by checking the ids. This makes it slightly more difficult to do since I'd need to lookup the palette indices first, but that is a compromise I'm willing to accept.
2. We can go through all player files every time there is an update to the migration list and update the ids.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Option `#2` would require you to migrate **all** inventories in **all** worlds **every** time you open a game, because you don't know  what migrations were applied to given save, what migrations were added since last time you opened the game etc. It would require some versioning to identify what was applied to keep world portability.

> Player inventories use a human-readable format and store the clear-text item ids.
> Player data must remain in a human-readable format, and inventories need to be too, so I can easily look for and patch cheated items.

IMO looking through tens or even hundreds of inventories manually is a dead end, especially considering that soon we will have chests and cheaters will be able to just hide cheated items in chests and you can easily have hundreds of chests. 

For purpose of checking few players we could just have a command or UI button (or both) allowing you to open someone's inventory  even if they are not in game based on username (please lets ignore color tags when using usernames as identifiers!) supported by way to get list of all players that have saved inventories.

Temporarily we could use option `#1` as a workaround, but long term I see no benefit, because just scale of the problem will obfuscate inventory content even if we don't make them binary.

// ISSUE TITLE: Modding
// ISSUE REPORT:
There has been a lot of discussion about modding again lately in the context of entities, so I think it is time to make a final decision here soon.

The current state, designing for all future possibilities, is not pleasant, so we should decide on an approach soon and commit to it.

Available options are:
- no native modding
   - easy to maintain for us, since we don't have to worry about maintaining a modding interface in any form
   - addons can already accomplish many things, maybe that is enough
   - fork modding is still possible
   - third party modding would still be possible (minecraft is a great example of a project where this worked out)
     - moves work from me to third party maintainers
- comptime modding
  - still easy to maintain, since mods can use existing functions, instead of needing a dedicated modding API
  - would require a launcher to select and recompile the game with a selected set of mods
  - insecure, mods can do anything, but it is possible to moderate the source code
  - fast, mods are optimized for the native platform like everything else in the game
  - long compile times every time the mods change
- runtime (DLL) modding
  - requires maintaining a modding API with C ABI compatibility
  - still relatively fast, but slower than comptime modding
  - insecure, mods can do anything
  - fast first load times
- runtime (WASM/JS/lua) modding
  - requires maintaining a wasm/JS/lua-compatible modding API, which I think is harder than just a C API
  - requires an interpreter or JIT, which makes it slower than the other options
  - secure, we can control what memory and functions mods can access
    - would allow the server to send mods to the client while joining, making it effortless to play with mods

---

I personally would like to avoid maintaining a stable modding API, since I know it's going to be a lot of work, and I don't like restricting myself to use the modding API either (which would be required to ensure that it actually works).

But I would like to hear some more thoughts on this before I go ahead and make a final decision on this.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
`comptime modding`: it is common to establish a "mod pack" with predefined set of mods and stick to it for compatibility reasons, changing mod set after it was used in a world would break that world. Effectively this means that for average user game with mods would be compiled once. Only the process of establishing mod collection would require recompilation, which may be a fair compromise.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Yeah, averaging over multiple runs it will eventually even be faster than JITing the wasm.
Furthermore for modpacks it might even be possible to just distribute precompiled executables. It's going to be insecure either way anyways.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
`runtime (DLL)`: having stable ABI ~is~ can be **very, very** hard and very restricting. At the moment it is impossible, in the future it will likely force us to tick the major version (assuming we will semantic versioning) quite often. That may be really annoying to maintain and can push us into bad design decisions just to not break ABI. This may also make the API harder to use than we would want, but that is purely speculative.

[USER COMMENT]
I'm a fan of either comptime modding or wasm modding. Wasm modding is one of my favourites because it is very secure and still can be relatively fast, while comptime modding is also good because you can look at the source code to see if anything is funny, the main downside being the compilation times. For me it can take a couple minutes to build Cubyz in release safe.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Right, DLL modding allows for closed source modding with no way to actually check if the code is not malicious, so IMO it should be out of the question. Making the addon code open source doesn't automatically make it secure, but at least you can check it, that is a huge difference.

[USER COMMENT]

Also the mods should have no reason to access the file system. we could probably do a regex check to see if there are any imports we dont like but that wouldn't stop people from doing .fs or anything. if anything we could delete some files and the lines they are imported from in the std.zig when compiling the mods, but that is probably not something we need right now.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Who knows, maybe there is a way to disable parts of the std library in zig that do things like read and write to the internet

Keep in mind that everything is in one compilation unit, so there is no way to disable things for part of the game.
Also in Zig you can just bypass the std and call e.g. a windows API function directly.
In general there are just too many possible attack vectors to be able to prevent them all. If you are creative you probably even just look through the entire executable memory, use a stack buffer to overwrite the function return address, and then just call a forbidden API function that way.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I'm a big fan of the lua modding. From a player's POV, it looks amazing.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
`no native modding`: The major drawback of modding with forking is that you can't easily mix and match mods without good programming knowledge and even if you have that advanced programming knowledge some of the conflicts are not easy to resolve. This is basically worse version of comptime modding.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
`no native modding`: The difference between m word and Cubyz is that Cubyz does not have multi-milion player base and likely would have to wait a long time to get to a point where somebody does make a 3rd party modding framework that would acually keep up with base game changes. Remember that every API change has to be somehow reflected in the framework and likely in the mods so with no stability guarantees it is a maintenance hell. In minecraft mods are born and die every day, there are insane amounts of abstraction libraries, just a total mess. This consumes a large amounts of software development work. Right now best way to programmatic features to Cubyz is just come and contribute to the core game, but this is huge amount of work for @IntegratedQuantum and not a sustainable model.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
`WASM/JS/LUA`: We have no/very little expertise in that domain, we have no idea what roadblocks we will face and we don't have extra development team to maintain a separate API, mixing dynamically typed languages with statically typed languages is not easy, mixing garbage collected with manually managed languages is not easy either.
JS has awkward semantics of the language and runtimes are often huge and take very long to compile (we have Bun tho, its written in zig)
LUA has disgusting syntax.
Any of those solutions will impose performance penalty in two ways: 
- as an interaction overhead, all conversions from dynamic types into static types are expensive OR requires copying back and forth a lot of stuff.
- Those technologies are not native, even if JITed they will have performance penalty, JITing is not magic, it won't just fix all the slow constructions you can make in the code, interpreting is even slower.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
`comptime modding`: We can reduce overhead of joining servers with similar approach to package managers usually do: In your launcher you would be able to add a 3rd party registry with modifications (eg. a git repository with submodules) following certain protocol defined by core game and the game would just show you the list of mods you need for specific server and would ask you if you want to download and install them. When you are installing node or python modules you can't be sure their dependencies are not malicious, but that is the cost you pay for having easy to use interface. We could define metadata format based on zon that every mod would have to use to be able to work as mod.
Similar registry approach could be used to provide eg. list of active servers. We could have our own 1st party registry with very limited and selective mods and servers of our own, but with disabled issues and pull requests so it is not flooded with 3rd party stuff.
That would allow us to present how registry works, how you can implement it and have some of our more controversial opinions implemented there.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Honestly, after seeing how easy it was to do comptime modding, if the alternative would be to have no first party modding interface att al, we should just go for comptime modding instead. WASM modding can be added by community (and likely they won't do that because noone cares really). It should still be possible by some codegen magic, whatever, not our problem 😄
I might be biased tho cause I co-authored the comptime modding demo PR 😅

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
`wasm`: I have reviewed this list of wasm runtimes: https://github.com/appcypher/awesome-wasm-runtimes

Out of listed runtimes 4 seem to offer what we need - either JIT or AOT and C/Zig interoperability:

- https://github.com/extism/extism
- https://github.com/WasmEdge/WasmEdge
- https://github.com/wasmerio/wasmer
- https://github.com/bytecodealliance/wasm-micro-runtime

I will research them further to identify what they offer and how we could use them.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
[Extism](https://github.com/extism/extism?tab=readme-ov-file):
- BSD-3-Clause
- Zig SDK and PDK
- JIT
- Written in Rust
- One core developer 
- The project seems mature, has docs, including one for Zig SDK: 
  - https://github.com/extism/zig-sdk
  - https://extism.org/docs/quickstart/host-quickstart
- Supports calling host functions from web assembly
- Supports calling web assembly functions from host
- Designed with plug-ins in mind

In general a very compelling option, with a lot of examples, both for code and build configuration.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
[WasmEdge](https://github.com/WasmEdge):
- Apache-2.0
- No official Zig SDK advertised, we would have to use C SDK
- JIT and **AOT**
- Written in C++
- Multiple core developers
- Mature, with good docs, just not for zig:
  - https://wasmedge.org/docs/category/c-sdk-for-embedding-wasmedge
  - https://wasmedge.org/docs/embed/c/intro#quick-start-guide-for-the-wasmedge-aot-compiler
- Supports calling host functions from wasm
- I am not sure if supports opposite, couldn't find it in the docs 
- there is documentation about how to do plug-ins

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
[Wasmer](https://github.com/wasmerio/wasmer?tab=readme-ov-file):
- MIT
- Zig SDK
- Written in Rust

Seems to be more oriented for standalone applications, not plug-in systems, documentation for SDKs is rather poor or I just couldn't find it. Official docs focus on running standalone WASM applications with CLI.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
[WARM](https://github.com/bytecodealliance/wasm-micro-runtime?tab=readme-ov-file):
- Apatche-2.0
- C API
- Interpreter, JIT and AOT
- Written in C
- Multiple core developers 
- Project seems mature, has good documentation with examples:
  - https://wamr.gitbook.io/document/basics/introduction
  - https://wamr.gitbook.io/document/wamr-in-practice/features/export_native_api
- Supports calling host functions from WASM
- Couldn't find code examples for the opposite


Seems to have most steep learning curve, but it's simplicity seems compelling. Only problem is that I could find easy recipe how to incorporate it into nom-C project, that's incoventien. It is worth mentioning that this project has biggest core team out of mentiones so it is least likely to just die one day. Also, building C is always harder than building eg Rust if one day we have to build it ourselves.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Alright, I think we should go for comptime modding then.
It seems that with a registry as described by @Argmaster, the only substantial downside are the first-time compilation times when attempting to load a mod(pack) for the first time. Right now release builds of the entire game take a few minutes, of course that number will grow over time, but I do hope we'll also see some decreases there as Zig improves. I think this is acceptable, and given that the alternatives require significantly more development time, I think this is the best choice.

I'll open a separate issue to track the implementation of comptime modding.

// ISSUE TITLE: `CircularBufferQueue` method renames
// ISSUE REPORT:
- [ ] Rename `enqueue` to `pushFront`
- [ ] Rename `enqueue_back` to `pushBack`
- [ ] Rename `dequeue` to `popBack`
- [ ] Rename `dequeue_front` to `popFront`
- [ ] Rename `peek` to `peekBack`

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
According to [wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Double-ended_queue#Operations) seems to prefer push/pop instead of enqueue/dequeue and it's also shorter and easier to write, so I suggest we also use that.
So it should be `pushBack`/`pushFront`, `popBack`/`popFront` and `peekBack`/`peekFront`

Honestly after seeing it for a while, I personally think that managed lists are still nice to have, with unmanaged the code is often riddled with explicit allocators everywhere and especially when used across function interfaces, there is a bigger chance of making mistakes (see #3128), and these mistakes are also difficult to spot in a review.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Edit: I assume we are considering change from

```zig
pub fn init(zon: ZonElement) ?*@This() {
```

To

```zig
pub fn init(zon: ZonElement, owner: *something) ?*@This() {
```

Since use case is this simple, I don't think we have a reason to produce more complicated solution.
Callbacks are inherently bound to the context of their owner, they can't be exchanged between different owners (an owner would be a block, an item etc.) so I don't think it reduces the usefulness of the solution. This is a reasonable extension of passing zon object to init.
There should be no separate info object. It should be just an reference to incomplete (as in not fully initialized) owner object.
Tbd how exactly the state of the object should be defined. You cannot guarantee the order of the callbacks, maybe you can guarantee that callbacks are initialized last. This is quite important, because callbacks can do basically anything if they have a pointer to the object.
Implementation should be flexible enough to handle typical path of evolution - **use without an owner** and use with modded owner (eg. Custom item type). I am not sure if implementation can be done though comp time generics or anytype pointers or union enums.

// ISSUE TITLE: Automatic code style violation detection
// ISSUE REPORT:
## Description

Contribution guidelines impose certain code style regulations that must be followed by all pull requests. Some of those restrictions are guarded by formatter, but there are also others that are not checked automatically even though they are technically checkable (eg. variable name style, import sorting, unnecessary type names where `.function(...)` is enough) and introduce additional burden when reviewing code. 

We could automate process of checking those problems with some tool:
- [zlint](https://github.com/DonIsaac/zlint) - recently stopped being actively worked on, some maintenance still happening. Describes itself as opinionated, we would have to see if it does provide reliable set of rules and if their opinions align with ours.
- [ziglint](https://github.com/nektro/ziglint) - appears dead and incomplete.
- [zls](https://github.com/zigtools/zls) - zls by itself is not a linter but a language server, however it does contain some error checking logic (possibly reliant on a compiler) and what's more important it implements Language Server Protocol with `Semantic token highlighting` feature. This means that not only it handles parsing, but it also provides semantic information about the code it parses. Now, we could take LSP-client-like library (eg. I find [pytest-lsp](https://pypi.org/project/pytest-lsp/) quite useful in some of my other projects) and try using it to extract some information about files in repository to catch some trivial errors. That is a bit hacky, but with Python it should be quick and ~~simple~~ not that hard to craft, so we can just throw it away if it breaks (or just fix it, that works too). 

Introducing some automated code checking hopefully would take some of the burden of code analysis off IntegratedQuantum  shoulders, same way formatter did.

*As you can guess I am most keen on working on least reliable out of the options*

*Technically there is also copilot, but I am not sure we want AI producing garbage anywhere near Cubyz*

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I would very much approve of this!

Some relevant things are:
- unused declarations/imports - often someone imports something, removes it later and then forgets to remove the import
- Functions/Constants that are public but not used anywhere outside the file (together with the above this also has the neat effect of enforcing at least one test for each function)
- naming convention - this might be difficult to enforce, since there are sometimes valid usecases for `_` in variable names.
- import/alias sorting: imports/aliases at the top, no imports/aliases afterwards, sorted by `std`/`main`/`@cImport`, in each category hierarchically, and on the same hierarchy alphabetically
- needless casting, needless `@as`

zlint looks good, but it doesn't cover everything, and we'd have to check if we can disable some of it's rules.
ziglint looks dead, and after two years I don't think it would even be usable for us.

However one big problem of either of these, is that we rely on another third party tool that may not update to whatever master zig version we might want to use. I think it would be best to make our own thing, I think the Zig tokenizer and parser are available in the standard library, so maybe we could make use of those.

> Now, we could take LSP-client-like library (eg. I find [pytest-lsp](https://pypi.org/project/pytest-lsp/) quite useful in some of my other projects) and try using it to extract some information about files in repository to catch some trivial errors.

I don't think it's as easy as you think. Knowing how something is highlighted may not be enough to know what something really is. Furthermore I think sometimes the highlighting is not quite reliable, so it may not be the best source of information.

> Technically there is also copilot, but I am not sure we want AI producing garbage anywhere near Cubyz

It might be interesting to check if it can help find some of the logic errors in a PR, however I do think it is too unreliable to check syntax highlighting, if I start contributing somewhere only to get an AI response hallucinating a formatting mistake, I would quit immediately.

Also let me add one last option for the fun of it:

*Technically there is also* you, *but as you already said, you don't want to work on the reliable solutions ;D*

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Also, so you don't get your hopes too high that this is going to change a lot, while it certainly does slow things down, having to check for all these simple mistakes, the main bottleneck for me is mostly the fact that I have to read, understand, and look for mistakes and bad design choices in the code. And I do think it is a lot harder to speed this up in most cases.

// ISSUE TITLE: Indicator for being on a block
// ISSUE REPORT:
I think one of the reasons for the dislike of low air mobility in games (compared to real life), is because a crucial information is missing.
In real life we can see our feet in our peripheral vision, this allows our brain (unconsciously) to sync up our feet with a target line or ledge on the ground. To confirm this I did an experiement: I blocked my downwards peripherals with my hands, and it made me much less accurate at hitting and jumping from a line on the ground.

So to solve this I propose an indicator for whether or not the player is currently on the ground. Together with coyote time and coyote frictions (see https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/1969#issuecomment-3403227249), this should be enough to give the player some time to react when stepping of a block.
As for how that would look, I propose just a small horizontal bar below the crosshair that gets toggled on or off.

// DISCUSSION:

// ISSUE TITLE: Procedural Crafting Changes (For After Pre-Alpha)
// ISSUE REPORT:
- [x] Remove Elasticity, as it's a confusing stat for players.
- [ ] Make swing speed constant, only changable via modifiers and accessories. Alternatively, keep this as an averaged stat.
- [ ] Make other stats additive or use some other method than averaging. This is to reduce player confusion and prevent them from having to use a wiki.
- [x] #1570 

Making procedural crafting work has been proving to be very difficult. I wonder if there's a different way of doing it... 

Anyways, don't think too hard about it. Use your brain for more important things!

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
- I am pretty sure weight should be additive, as this is exactly what players would expect.
- At the same time I think most mean is the most intuitive choice for durability. Although we could verify what would be the impact of geometric or harmonic mean or some other way to reduce impact of outliers. I am not a math guy so the second part may be completely pointless idea.
- Some of the modifiers could stack additively or exponentially (eg. fragile could be exponential to prevent all diamond pickaxes, heavy modifier should be additive so it is intuitive)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I want to point out that making swing time constant and using modifiers / effects to change it doesn't really improve our situation. We still have 3 variables, only the means we use to alter those variables change. It doesn't solve the problem, it only moves it to a different place. I still don't understand what exactly are our goals, this is the same situation as last time when we opened rebalancing issue #1430, since then we didn't meet to brainstorm, we were basically shutting down each others ideas in text without moving forward. Since #1430 we started working on #1466 but it doesn't talk much about our goals for balance. We need a document that would specify which materials in what way should be better or worse than others and it should give rough numbers what "good" or "bad" means, what values of stats should be common, what values rare and what values impossible to get. That document should also include rough specification of accessories, possibly armor and weapons, without that everything we change stuff in one place it will break in few other places. For now we are just clashing our unspoken imaginations of how tools should be balanced and apparently these imaginations are nowhere close to each other.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I just want a system that makes intuitive sense to the player. That is it.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Here's what I would consider good and bad:

Good:
- System that lets the player experiment with different combinations of materials
- Different materials have different values and modifiers that have a clear advantage or disadvantage over another

Bad:
- Arbitrary stats that the player can't possibly know about unless they look at a wiki (aka elasticity)
- Players being confused on how to craft the optimal tool
- Unclear stats

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
So, using modifiers is the best play here, as it allows the player to see how and why their tool is different.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I respect what you consider good and bad but I must point out that different behaviors will be intuitive for the player depending on their gaming background / experience, knowledge about irl physics and engineering and their imagination, so that is not a precise specification. 

We need to establish what we think is intuitive / what out current community considers intuitive and stick with it. You can't please everyone and if we don't set clear rules we will re-balancing the system over and over again every time someone says that they are confused about something. 

This is why following irl physics is beneficial, as it already sets a framework of how stuff should work and interact, but that would be quite boring IMO, so we need to set our own rules. And those rules need to specify how we want different materials to behave on their own and how they should interact with other materials. 

I our case there are too many possible combinations of materials in all possible positions, so we can't just check all of them every time. But we still can take some common / important designs and set rules for them, then interpolate that for other designs. We need strict rules for what stats we consider min, mean, max for tools so we can design stuff within those boundaries. Goals first, Implementation later. 

Current approach of trial and error without precise planning already let us down multiple times, how many times do we have to redesign procedural crafting system before we finally sit down and design it as a whole, without hoping that new features will magically fix existing systems?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

I think we need a solution that directly shows you the big picture.

[USER COMMENT]
> different package formats all claiming they are very important to implement and very universal

We support any linux system that has the following: 

* a shell in `/bin/sh`

* a writable `/tmp` directory (depends on the app, not all need this).

And that is it lol. We don't have dependency on userns (containers) or FUSE.

The biggest limitation is that certain libraries have a minimum kernel version, in practice this means the oldest system you can usually run our appimages on is ubuntu 14.04, anything older (specially kernel 2.6) starts to run into many issues.

See README [here](https://github.com/pkgforge-dev/Anylinux-AppImages#anylinux-appimages) for more details

// ISSUE TITLE: Inventory Enhancement: The Bag
// ISSUE REPORT:
Just like a normal bag, you can throw things in at the top, but if you want to search something in there, you have to start taking things out of it.

In the game the bag would be represented by a single item slot, where you can access the top-most item in the bag, or put new stuff into it.

Overall this allows expanding the inventory, while also encouraging some strategy when it comes to inventory management.
Which items do you want to access quickly? Which items can you just put into the bag?

Internally this can be represented by a Stack data structure.

In the future, there could also be capacity upgrades to the bag.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
- How should we indicate that a slot is a bag?
- Should we be able to view all items in the bag, even if we can take only last in?
- How should we indicate fullness when bag slot is part of structure eg. furnace

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> How should we indicate that a slot is a bag?

I'd imagine that it would probably have a symbol in the background, or a special color/outline.

> Should we be able to view all items in the bag, even if we can take only last in?

No, I think only seeing the top is part of the fun.
I think it would be nice if you could see the top two items behind each other (the one behind maybe slightly gray), to indicate visually how the bag works.

> How should we indicate fullness when bag slot is part of structure eg. furnace

I think a binary indication should be enough, maybe we can change the color of the frame or something.

[USER COMMENT]
Having adventured for ~5000 blocks, my inventory has gotten full of random shiny things that I picked up along the way (new flowers, plants, wood types, etc), leaving no room for anything I find at my destination or pick up on the way back.

The bag would be the perfect solution, I could throw all this stuff in a bag and sort it out when I got home

[USER COMMENT]
With #2774 removing the `Inventory.Type` I would like to know how this could be achieved. 
A dynamic sized Inventory sounds pretty unique to me but also shouldn't conflict with other inventories. 

Will it maybe be one inventory with size 1 and one backing type with the stack. 
Then on the update callback of the inventory we could move the top most item from the stack to the actual inventory. 
And with specifing the stack as the source we can make commands look if they first need to move the item in the inventory to the stack.
This would of course mean we may need new sync commands wich represent these two operations (moving from stack to inventory and from inventory to stack, if the stack is not itself an inventory).

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
It could also be not an inventory.
There is no reason to try to generalize an inventory into being able to fulfill the same things as the bag, especially given that it behaves much differently too.
There are basically just two operations: deposit and take, so we could just add sync commands for these and be done with it.

// ISSUE TITLE: Statically link release artifacts
// ISSUE REPORT:
Currently, the builds released by the `Release` action are built against glibc, which is statically linked by default.  
This means that these binaries cannot be used on systems like *NixOS*, which do not support running dynamically-linked binaries.

My proposal is to build against `musl`, which is fully statically-linked, resulting in a binary that works on all systems (even systems that use glibc).

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I am confused about your issue;

> built against glibc, which is statically linked by default.

> NixOS, which do not support running dynamically-linked binaries.

What's the logic here? If glibc is statically linked then everything should be alright, no?

[USER COMMENT]
You cannot make a fully static binary, because you will not be able to dlopen libraries if you produce a fully static binary. **That means no hardware acceleration.**

Even if you manage to statically link MESA, you will not able to use the proprietary nvidia driver, you will also not be able to use stuff like mangohud which preload a library, vulkan layers thru zink will also not be possible.

The solution to this is what I talked about [here](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/2975#issuecomment-4321516098)

* https://github.com/pkgforge-dev/Cubyz-AppImage

This works in alpine linux and NixOS. As long as your GPU supports OpenGL4.6 btw.

Edit: If you will test this on NixOS make sure to disable `appimage-run` btw, as it actually dies because it expects the appimage to be SquashFS which is not the case here. Just run the AppImage directly instead.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I tried linking musl and it didn't work, see fe5afde6d36acd37637d56746536cf3387b9eeed

// ISSUE TITLE: Ornate blocks
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

This is a proposal for a crafted ornate block.

## Player perspective

Ornate block would be crafted in dedicated ornate workbench.
Ornate workbench contains N predefined block patterns player can select from.
Ornate workbench contains one input slot which can be filled with any full block.
Colors of the resulting ornate block depend on the materials used. 
Resulting ornate block has a fixed orientation.

## Implementation details

An ornate block uses `cubyz:ornate` rotation mode which stores source block ID similarly to what `cubyz:ore` does.
Every full block that can be used to create ornate has to define a static 8 color `ornate_colors` table used to color ornate blocks.
Ornate "patterns" are just default textures for each of the ornate blocks.
Similarily to how tools work, ornates would create a colored version of ornate texture based on the source block it has.
Since normal items do not have data information field, ornate blocks would require a dedicated item type that remembers the type of source block used.

## Possible extension: procedural crafting

Idea presented above is cheap(er) performance-wise, but is also very limiting. A possible extension, that most likely would have much larger performance impact, would be to make ornate block procedural. 
Ornate blocks would  be crafted out of 8 arbitrary blocks and ornate texture would be generated on the fly.
Since each of the 8 source blocks defines 8 `ornate_colors`, index of color used would be tied to location in crafting grid. This means that order of block in crafting grid influences how the resulting ornate block looks.
Since there is a lot of data to store, we would have to make the ornate blocks block entities instead of just using 16 data bits (48 bits wouldn't help either, so orevolution is not a solution :P )

---

Idea comes from [here](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/pull/2796#issuecomment-4181353172)

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]

i.e. marble is smooth and shiny, sandstone is very rough

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

Either way it will be annoying in some scenario, as, when it drops ingredients, you will have to run back to craft it again into ornate block. Animated blocks are out of question, glass blocks are out of question - both are technically not viable.

But I do support the idea of patterns that can be applied to any block. // ISSUE TITLE: Chain block updates
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Currently we do not have chained block updates, meaning things like vines will not be able to break completely if the top supporting block is broken. There are two ways to fix this that I can see:
1. Schedule block updates on the next tick if the update function returns true.
2. Update recursively on the same tick if the update function returns true.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
Both might be useful. Vines might break all at the same time, and leafs when we cut down tree will decay gradually. I think, that there might be a peace of data in blocks that says, that this block is relaying on other blocks, and one saying that other blocks relay on it. that way we could cut down on checks. Block when placed will update all block around, and will revive list of block it need to update when updated. Those list might even refer to specific types of updates. Connected block will know what block they are connected to when moved, block will know if they support gravity block, red stone like system might work by having list of connections etc.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The most obvious problems with chained updates are long update chains and cycles.
ATM when you update block x, all its direct neighbors will be informed about that by calling appropriate rotation mode callbacks. However, if we propagate the update to neighbors of neighbors of block x, then block x itself is one of those blocks. We have to be careful with assumptions, because propagating only outwards from block x will not be enough, eg. neighbor of neighbor can be a support block for neighbor.

Right now we were able to perform all those updates atomically within single frame, but dependency chains between blocks can and will be long. Vine chain can easily have 5, 10 or 100 blocks if somebody decides they want to climb to their base with vines. This would easily mean updating 100 blocks and informing another ~402 (assuming no neighbors of neighbors meet to be informed as neighbors didn't change, which may not be necessarily true) modifying multiple chunks. 

Some time ago I suggested more generic system of scheduled tasks which could be used for purposes like this. Tasks like this would have to be stored as part of the save file to be stored and restored on chunk unload. However the idea did not meet with wide support.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Another difficult question is also what happens if the dependency chain leaves the radius of loaded chunks. Ideally we'd load, update and store these chunks, which is going to be even more costly.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Also a problem with scheduled tasks approach is that intermediate states of block updates may not be valid by themselves eg. A fence or branch doesn't have a side face when extended to another block.

That said scheduled tasks are most sane approach imo. They would prevent large lag spikes of instant updates, although still an exponentially growing dependency chain can cause lag.

For block updates happening outside of loaded chunks I am pretty sure we are discarding them ATM, but what happens when is just a neighbor update?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Are lag spikes even a concern? At least on the server side updating thousands of blocks should be fine.

[USER COMMENT]
> an exponentially growing dependency chain can cause lag.

I can't see this happening in normal gameplay, it would probably only crop up if a player intentionally causes lag.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
this is done, vines are tracked in #2486

// ISSUE TITLE: Consider greedy meshing or similar.
// ISSUE REPORT:
This is especially interesting for transparent blocks, like water or fog. This could bring down the number of triangles rendered.
Greedy meshing might be problematic however due to creating pixel errors at T junctions.

However greedy meshing might need special cases for lighting #61.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Alright, so it seems that overall the fragment shader cost of greedy meshing, at least with the variant I implemented in https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/compare/greedy_meshing, is quite high.
While I was able to offset some of that cost with a depth prepass, overall the speedup is only around 0-20% on my GPU, and it's actually slower on my laptop.
So ultimately it's not really worth it, which is why I decided to deprioritize this.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I have tried it, and I have no further ideas for other approaches.
Furthermore other solutions for performance and mesh memory usage have been found.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think we should reconsider this, but not for its rendering performance, but for allowing us to store meshes in a nicer way on the CPU: Right now a big bottleneck of block updates is resampling the light data for all the faces. With greedy meshing we could better cache samples in an area and then apply them to faces in bulk, instead of re-interpolating the same vertices 4+ times.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Right now a big bottleneck of block updates is resampling the light data for all the faces. With greedy meshing we could better cache samples in an area and then apply them to faces in bulk, instead of re-interpolating the same vertices 4+ times.

I ported the greedy meshing branch to master and tested how viable it is for this use case:
Overall I saw no large reduction in the number of samples needed. The main problem I guess is that greedy meshing is not effective enough at reducing the face count, it halves, which at best duplicates 2/6th of the corners, at the cost of significantly increasing the cost of meshing itself.

Furthermore, there were some more rendering improvements (internal leaf culling), making greedy meshing even less viable for the actual rendering. And since the mesh memory usage has been optimized, greedy meshing also results in more data per face.

// ISSUE TITLE: Get a little closer to Zig's preferred formatting
// ISSUE REPORT:
I think particularly the whitespace things are annoying since ZLS tends to autocomplete in the `zig fmt` style, I guess it's time I change some of my habbits with regards to whitespaces around structs, fields and ifs.

These should be changed one at a time, to reduce the impact.

I do still have a strong opinion about tabs and spaces around operators though.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Hmm, I'm not quite happy with how it handles nested constructs, like
```zig
.{ .attachments = &.{.{ // zig fmt
	.srcColorBlendFactor = .one,
	.dstColorBlendFactor = .one,
	.colorBlendOp = .add,
	.srcAlphaBlendFactor = .one,
	.dstAlphaBlendFactor = .one,
	.alphaBlendOp = .add,
}} }, // ← weird

.{.attachments = &.{.{ // Cubyz-fmt
	.srcColorBlendFactor = .one,
	.dstColorBlendFactor = .one,

}}},
```

// ISSUE TITLE: The game cannot save players with too long names
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior

The file name can exceed the limits of the file system. We need some different way of uniquely identifying which file belongs to which player.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We could have a zon file containing list of players and use indexes in that list to name directories.

[USER COMMENT]
why not use an UUID? like Minecraft does? (Minecraft uses an MD5 of the name hash for UUIDs)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
UUID is a cursed standard, that's the first reason. Second reason is that Version 3 and 5 UUID which we could use here use only 121/122 bits of randomness of a hash, so we would be better of just using the hash function and it definitely shouldn't be MD5, it's considered weak as of now, SHA-1 would be better. Third reason is that since players have infinitely many name options there might be collisions and it's simply much harder to resolve those than to have a mapping. To prevent situation where mapping gets corrupted and it's not possible to retrieve player data we could add an file to each player folder containing their player name, so there is a worse time complexity recovery mechanism (adding an entry to player data also works).

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
UUIDs are only guaranteed to be unique if you own all devices they are generated on.
Also this would imply an account system which we don't have.

Instead of maintaining a separate mapping file I think it would be easiest to just make a hashmap in the file system, where collisions are resolved with an incremental ID appended to the player name hash.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We have no guarantees about name distribution, are you sure betting your success on your hash function is a good idea? If our hash function turns out to be bad we would need to migrate a lot of files, with mapping file a hash function is completely irrelevant. Additionally it makes the storage almost opaque which may bite back when we least expect.

[USER COMMENT]
I thought I would post a comment with the approach as I see it:

- New structure table type that encapsulates:
  - id
  - biomeTags (cubyz:cold, cubyz:hot, etc)
  - structures (biomes.structures as we currently know it)
- Biomes no longer directly store a structure table in their zon file.
- Biomes now store a biomeTags element to associate structure tables to biomeTags

- During asset loading:
  - Structure Tables
    - Load each available .zon in assets/cubyz/structure_tables (or assets/<addons>/structure_tables
    - Builds a `[][] const u8` of the structure table IDs to lookup later
  - Biomes
    - Replace structure table load logic with:
      - Loop through all loaded structure tables (via the ID list from earlier, Structure Tables would need a getById or something)
      - If a structure table has a biometag that matches the biome, or the structure table has no biometag, concatenate the contents of that structure table to the biome's internal structure table
      - Add totalChance as we did before, normalize if > 1 as we did before.

// ISSUE TITLE: Flatpak package on Flathub
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Hi! Cool game :) 

Have you considered publishing Cubyz on as a [Flatpak](https://flatpak.org) on [Flathub](https://flathub.org)? This would make it easy to install and play on Linux systems, including the Steam Deck where the built-in Discover application automatically has Flathub set up out of the box.

Thanks in advance.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
There are a lot of package managers and package formats we could theoretically support, but we can't support every one of them so likely there won't be a first party support for any of them.

The only channel we consider to support, as it would allow us to appeal to a wider audience, are game stores like Steam, but with no promises as of now.

If you want to distribute a flatpack package with Cubyz inside feel free to do so (considering license)

If we notice people are dedicated to maintaining such distribution channels I guess we could consider listing them in documentation (non-existent as of now) and supporting them to the extent of not breaking them constantly. That is however something to discuss at a later date.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Sorry, but we do not have the time to maintain package managers for now. It would be cool to see Cubyz in package managers, but in my opinion adding more features is more important than distribution at the moment (and honestly on most devices with a web browser it only saves a few clicks).

[USER COMMENT]
Just to clarify, publishing Cubyz as on Flathub would not only be about distribution but also discoverability, compatibility, and security. Flathub is either the biggest or second biggest Linux app store (the other contender being the Snap Store which uses Snap packages) and it is set up by default in many Linux OS:es. In addition to that, Flatpak packages work on any Linux OS with Flatpak available, which is most of them. It also provides trust and security as Flatpak packages have to declare their permissions at build time (some can be requested at runtime) so users can be confident both that the program won't access their files, or in the case of security issues (which IMO is relevant given that this game has multiplayer), an attacker won't as easily be able to access them.

With that said I of course respect your decision. But, 

> If you want to distribute a flatpack package with Cubyz inside feel free to do so (considering license)

This part is very important :) [Flathub requires that you contact the upstream maintainers of a given program before you publish it there yourself](https://github.com/flathub/flathub/blob/0dbdbd27ab8444edb0a3afa721265cdf7732c1f9/.github/pull_request_template.md?plain=1#L15-L16).

Thanks again.

// ISSUE TITLE: Subbiomes should inherit music
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

If no song is specified, subbiomes should inherit the song of the parent biome

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
This is trickier than it sounds. 

Biomes can have multiple parent biomes, for example check `decorative/stone_pit.zig.zon`

You would need to determine which biome caused the subbiome to spawn, and use its music, not any other parent biome.

There's a decent chance all the parent biomes for a subbiome share music, but we'll be making an arbitrary choice about which parent to get the music from if we're not storing the subbiome's parent as part of the generated world data. So if that assumption they all match isn't true, maybe we're playing the wrong music now.
