// ISSUE TITLE: ID / asset file name limitations
// ISSUE REPORT:
Currently asset file names and hence IDs generated from them have very little limitation, only those directly implied by asset loading system and OS Cubyz is currently running on. There are reasons to explicitly restrict certain characters from appearing in asset file names and hence IDs (completely or partially) to either use them for special purpose or to encourage cross-platform compatibility of assets.

In particular:
- `:` - used to separate addon name from asset ID and to separate ID from block data
- `%` - used to separate weight from block ID in `Pattern` - #1237 
- `,` - used to separate `Pattern` sub-expressions - #1237
- `|` - used to logically or `EditMask` sub-expression - #1284 
- `&` - used to logically and `EditMask` sub-expression - #1284 
- `!` - used to inverse `EditMask` sub-expression - #1284 
- `$` - used to indicate a tag in `EditMask` - #1284 
- `@` - used to indicate block property query in `EditMask` - #1284
- `#` - used for adding colors to text in chat
- `.`, `{`, `}`, `=` - to allow cleaner notation for block data. For example, for branches: `cubyz:oak_branch:.{.posX = true, .negX = true}`
- `<`, `>`, `"`, `\`, `|`, `/`, `:`, `?`, `*` - characters not allowed in file names on Windows operating system
- control characters in range 0 - 31 - again, characters not allowed in file names on Windows operating system

We can either refuse to register assets with those characters in file names or automatically replace those characters with some placeholder character like `_`. Latter option creates a small problem with handling of name collisions in auto-generated names, but we could in such case refuse to load the asset completely. Either way file names that do not comply with limitations should be reported to the user as `std.log.err`.

There is also list of reserved file names from Windows:

CON, PRN, AUX, NUL, COM0, COM1, COM2, COM3, COM4, COM5, COM6, COM7, COM8, COM9, COM¹, COM², COM³, LPT0, LPT1, LPT2, LPT3, LPT4, LPT5, LPT6, LPT7, LPT8, LPT9, LPT, LPT, and LPT

We can also consider restricting file name lenght to some sane limit, eg. 255 chars to match limitations of most [file systems](https://doc.owncloud.com/server/next/admin_manual/troubleshooting/path_filename_length.html).

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I thought about it some more, and honestly these are id names, mainly intended for internal usage.
The player will see different names in the future anyways, so it could make sense to just restrict them to `a-z`, `A-Z` (converted to lower case internally) and `_`. This also has the nice side effect that all addons will be forced to use a similar naming style.

> There is also list of reserved file names from Windows

That's just stupid. We already have enough code that deals with stupid windows issues, so I would like to just skip it here, since it wouldn't be a big deal anyways.

> We can either refuse to register assets with those characters in file names or automatically replace those characters with some placeholder character like _.

Automatic replacement would make things difficult, since you'd need to add an asset migration after it has been added like this to a test world. Ignoring the file is probably the best decision.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> I thought about it some more, and honestly these are id names, mainly intended for internal usage. The player will see different names in the future anyways, so it could make sense to just restrict them to `a-z`, `A-Z` (converted to lower case internally) and `_`. This also has the nice side effect that all addons will be forced to use a similar naming style.

Cool.

> That's just stupid. We already have enough code that deals with stupid windows issues, so I would like to just skip it here, since it wouldn't be a big deal anyways.

Windows in a nutshell.

> Automatic replacement would make things difficult, since you'd need to add an asset migration after it has been added like this to a test world. Ignoring the file is probably the best decision.

Ok

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
What about numbers? `0-9`? We do have some assets that use them as it is useful to express multiple states of eg. same texture or model. (see duckweed)

[USER COMMENT]
I think we should either allow `-` and `+`, or at least coerce `-` to `_` like we would for lowercase letters.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
What would be the use case that can not be fulfilled with just `_`?

[USER COMMENT]
`_` associates tighter than `-` in my opinion, so you can have some more hierarchy. Compare `big_tree-v3`, `big_tree_v3`, `big-tree_v3`. The middle option is somewhat more flat and ambiguous, whether it is the 3rd "big tree", or the big "3rd tree". On the other hand, giving the user more options gives them a chance to forget their naming scheme 🙃.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
IMO this is arbitrary, for versioning there are version control systems and either of these names is meaningless. If those assets are intended to coexist they should not be versioned, they should use adjectives, like large or small, or just numbers. If they are not ment to coexist, use version control. These are human readable portable identifiers, not documentation strings. Moreover this would create precedent to argue for more and more dedicated special chars to be included in IDs to make it possible to encode increasing amount of information in IDs. Such information simply should not be encoded in IDs.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> _ associates tighter than - in my opinion, so you can have some more hierarchy. Compare big_tree-v3, big_tree_v3, big-tree_v3. The middle option is somewhat more flat and ambiguous, whether it is the 3rd "big tree", or the big "3rd tree". On the other hand, giving the user more options gives them a chance to forget their naming scheme 🙃.

If it makes sense to use a hierarchy, then you can express this through the file system `big_tree/v3`, that's probably better for organization anyways.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> What about numbers? 0-9? We do have some assets that use them as it is useful to express multiple states of eg. same texture or model. (see duckweed)

Yeah, right, I just forgot to list them.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
As we saw on [discord](https://discord.com/channels/443805812390100992/702167480654757991/1459570916798234796) there is a possibility to mistype file names like `_defaults`. Additionally, since these are technical files intended to have a special meaning, I think we should also forbid leading underscores in addon ids.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> there is a possibility to mistype file names like _defaults

Does that mean `defaults` should be a reserved name?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Does that mean defaults should be a reserved name?

Without the underscore? No I think forgetting the underscore is much more difficult to make as a typo, and much easier to spot that it's wrong.

// ISSUE TITLE: Upon World Creation, Add Warning For Survival Mode Incompleteness
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Cubyz seems to be getting attention (albiet, only a little), Youtuber dacctal had made a [video](https://youtu.be/wYm9Kvg0qbg) that featured Cubyz and throughout their gameplay, they had a pretty glowing review for the engine technology and the crafting mechanics, but they had confusion surrounding healing, not knowing that it isn't implemented at all, let alone that another mechanic (trinkets) will be involved.

Fundamental components to Survival gameplay are missing, such as monsters, and other components are incomplete, such as progression, energy, and health. It's probably safe to assume that all newcomers will be having issues like this (including me, who was introduced to Cubyz from that video), so there should be something to keep expectations in check.

Ideally, it could link or mention the actual state of completion according to the game design document (Issue #670, perhaps), but I think a more tasteful solution would be to draw direct inspiration from Minecraft's earliest Survival Test days, and simply append "(Test)" or "(pre-alpha)" to the survival option. That way people who were there for that know what to expect, and it's still effective in communicating that it is missing big pieces.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I'd rather not have a sign of Cubyz' incompleteness staring me in the face all day.
Also I think the Survival mode is enjoyable enough to go without these labels, maybe even more enjoyable than some of the other games that have these features.
And the incompleteness is already clearly present as a leading `0` in the version number. Everyone knows what 1.0 means, and a 0 clearly shows that it's before that.

// ISSUE TITLE: The Inventory mutex is too omnipresent
// ISSUE REPORT:
In #1739 I noticed that every block updates needs to lock the inventory mutex, since it needs to be locked when breaking a chest to remove the inventory.

Generally it would be nicer if this was less coupled, particularly it is problematic that we even get into the situation that the inventory mutex leaks into the update block function, this has a big risk of causing severe deadlocks in the future.
I'm not sure how this could be prevented though, since otherwise there is a risk that the inventory gets changed while breaking/placing a block (e.g. maybe the player collects something) which in turn changes the eligibility of breaking a block.

I think the right solution may be to avoid having a global mutex at all, maybe a per inventory mutex suffices? This does however make it more likely to deadlock for operations on multiple inventories.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think the best solution for this would be to execute all the inventory (and other sync-related) code on the server in a single thread, the update thread, asserting that we are on the right thread, instead of locking a mutex.

If you think about it, all this locking is problematic since we end up basically in a single threaded situation anyways, so why not move it all into one thread. If in the future we do need multiple threads, then a dynamic work scheduling system, that considers which tasks execute on the same resources, could be implemented instead, but let's keep it simple for today. I think most gains will come from parallelizing expensive operations like pathfinding and AI anyways.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Alright I think I know what we can do, if we ever have a need to parallelize this.

I made the following observations:
1. inventory operations can be executed out of order, if they don't move stuff around (e.g. taking durability, removing items, adding health), as long as we don't care about which operations fail in case of resource shortage.
e.g. imagine mining 2 blocks behind each other, but (through some other event that the client couldn't know about) the pickaxe only has 1 durability left. This restriction would mean that the game is allowed to break only the further away block.
I think this is acceptable, given that any player-action cancellation is going to be confusing to the player and generally this would require quite specific circumstances.
2. Most player actions happen on a large time-scale. A non-cheating player will not insta-switch while mining blocks at sub-tick speeds.
3. When block updates only ever read there direct neighbor (and never write), then we can parallelize block updates on a checkerboard pattern, first updating all chunks with even (x + y + z), then updating all chunks with odd (x + y + z).
4. Writing could be achieved through events executed in future frames

So overall, we can run inventory operations in parallel, as long as we separate moving operations from non-moving operations, and execute moving operations sequentially.

For now though, the first step in either case is to push things onto the update thread. It's nice to see that parallelization is possible in principle, though I still doubt we need to even go that far.

// ISSUE TITLE: Use XDG Base Directory Specification for data directories on Linux
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Storing data in dot folders in $HOME is outdated practice, when all programs do that, $HOME becomes an unmanageable mess.
XDG Base Directory Specification: https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir-spec/latest/

Ideally:
- cache should be stored in $XDG_CACHE_HOME/appname, and fallback to ~/.cache/appname.
- data should be stored in $XDG_DATA_HOME/appname, and fallback to ~/.local/share/appname.
- user configs should be stored in $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/appname, and fallback to ~/.config/appname.
- logs should be stored in $XDG_STATE_HOME/appname and fallback to ~/.local/state/appname (storing logs near the executable is also not ideal because it makes the app hard to package).

But a lot of apps take the approach of storing everything either in XDG_CONFIG_HOME or XDG_DATA_HOME, which is still better than in $HOME.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
If you want it somewhere else you can always overwrite the location.

For now though I'd like to keep it where it is, ~/ is always the first location where I look for app data.
And honestly worrying about these convention just takes away time from real development, and not such a big issue in my opinion.

// ISSUE TITLE: Suggestion: Multiple terrain generators per world
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Hello.
As I can see, currently the game has support for multiple terrain generators which are stored separately, and world settings specify generator and version, and I hope that new versions of the generator will not overwrite previous ones.
This is good, because players don't always want their world just start generating different terrain resulting in harsh borders.
But there is a matter of transition to a new version.
Simple solution is to just change your mapGenerator setting and allow new terrain to be generated where there is empty space, like in MC.
But I propose adding options for limiting generation dimensions by X, Y, Z coordinate and allowing to have multiple of them specified per world.
This aligns with "No Dimensions" principle and allows multiple styles of generation to exist simultaneously in the world by either stacking generators vertically on top of each other:
```
[Sky world] Y 1000 <-> Y infinite
[Normal world] Y 0 <-> Y 1000
[Caves world] Y -500 <-> Y 0
[Hell] Y -1500 <-> Y -500
```
Or doing regional split by X or Z:
```
[Legacy region] X -500 <-> X 500, Z -600 <-> Z 600
[New region] X 500 <-> X infinite
[Custom region] ...
etc.
```

I realize that it is a bit early, since only one terrain generator exists currently, but I this such fundamental options are worth to consider early, and if properly implemented will lead to better compatibility.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> and I hope that new versions of the generator will not overwrite previous ones.
This is good, because players don't always want their world just start generating different terrain resulting in harsh borders.

That is not the case (I'd rather not collect so much legacy code), and the most common breakage is adding new biomes, and not changing the actual generation methods. Instead the heightmap is stored and interpolated to avoid harsh terrain.

The stacking you suggest can already be done within the biome system with caves biomes (see also #1463). It's not as fine grained as the surface map, but we can't afford to generate multiple surface maps for the world.


The reason I allowed to choose these per-world is to allow some control for mods to bring their own versions without conflicting with each other.

[USER COMMENT]
> That is not the case

At least I hope it will be when the game comes out of alpha.

> The stacking you suggest can already be done within the biome system with caves biomes 

My solution is more generalized, the "configuration" I've provided is just an example. The possibilities could be much greater.

Well, I've noticed the project for multiple things it does right.
My suggestion is based on my experience as a server owner and I've had hoped to become a part of it, in time.
But I think Game Design Principles are a bit restrictive, at least if they are applied to the engine part, not "the default experience" part.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> But I think Game Design Principles are a bit restrictive, at least if they are applied to the engine part

Yeah, the engine will of course be a bit more relaxed when it comes to things like this.
However for the time being you'd need to have a very good reason to add a feature that is not at least useful for the core game. I'd like to avoid an engine with 20 different legacy systems that were once useful for a single addon. This would make the engine worse for everyone.

// ISSUE TITLE: Why is this vendoring a code formatter
// ISSUE REPORT:
I don't know how commonly accepted this practice is in the zig space, but all I see this doing is adding friction for contributors (particularly those who, like myself, rely on IDE format on save and the like). The benefits of bespoke or forked tooling should be weighed against the downsides. I am a mere clueless outsider but in my (admittedly uninformed about the intimate details of this project) opinion the cost vs benefit of bespoke tooling is not worth it here.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
I'm also leaning towards just going with the established way of doing things and following existing Zig style guidelines, where they exist. One thing to keep in mind as well is that this project might become people's first introduction to Zig, so it might be a bit dubious to teach them practices that differ from the norm.

Of course, in the end, this is a quite opinionated matter. For example, I prefer tabs for indentation because it allows people to adjust indentation to their liking, but I'm willing to go with the flow if there isn't an easy way to set a per-project setting for `zig fmt`.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I agree that conforming with the zig style would be good, but I have a strong opinion on some formatting choices.

Tabs just make the code easier to navigate (with arrow keys) and edit in my opinion. As much as space-users proclaim that spaces work like tabs, the reality is that they don't.
Furthermore it is also saving a few bytes, now of course for code it practically makes no difference. But it also bleeds into the loading time of the assets and I don't want to make loading 10% slower just to be conformant with some stupid formatting decisions.

The other thing that I feel strongly about is spaces around operators. I think `a + b*c` is a lot easier to read than `a + b * c`

All the other differences are probably debatable, but would affect a lot of code so I don't know if it's worth it.

Also if the IDE doesn't allow you to set a different formatter on save, then you should make an issue on their issue tracker.

[USER COMMENT]
> Furthermore it is also saving a few bytes, now of course for code it practically makes no difference.

Disk space is plentiful. Compression should counteract any perceived gains of saving network bandwidth or similar, when it comes to choosing tabs over spaces.

> But it also bleeds into the loading time of the assets and I don't want to make loading 10% slower just to be conformant with some stupid formatting decisions.

Is this based on observation or just an educated guess? Skipping spaces in a byte stream should have no functional impact on performance.

Again, I also prefer tabs over spaces, but to me, using standard code styling guidelines and tooling is a bigger boon.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Just so to make a closure on this topic:

The code formatting has been established and won't be changed. You may like it, you may not, but it is there and it is enforced automatically, so it is done properly. I would like to remind you that purpose of formatting is not to suite everyone from the start (as everyone is coming from different tech stacks with different standards in place), but rather to ensure consistency in code style and remove burden of commenting code style from PRs. It will speed up your code navigation once you are used to it and you will get used to it. I guarantee you that if this is first time you have to adapt to formatting style in a project, it won't be the last.

There are solutions to reported increased friction in form of editor extensions (for example Visual Studio Code extension https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz-dev-kit-vscode ) that can be used to bridge this gap.

```

// ISSUE TITLE: CI compile targets // ISSUE REPORT: Right now in release.yml we're targeting `baseline` x86_64. This is okay, but it severely limits the featureset the compiler can utilize. The processor targeted by `baseline` is roughly a core2 or k8 cpu with some missing features. For comparison's sake, though not purely scientific and I need a good way to test this: baseline, 24 render distance, opaque faces counter no longer increasing/chunks no longer loading: Sandybridge, 24 render distance, opaque faces counter no longer increasing/chunks no longer loading: (The photo doesn't catch this, but it fluctuates much less on sandybridge too) Native (alderlake), 24 render distance, opaque faces counter no longer increasing/chunks no longer loading: It's a bit hard to measure because I'm just staring at a constantly fluctuating number. From world enter to chunks fully loaded: baseline: Starts at ~2200 microseconds per task, climbs to ~5800 microseconds per task by the end Takes 2 minutes 22 seconds Sandybridge: Starts at ~1800 microseconds per task, climbs to ~5300 microseconds per task by the end Takes 2 minutes 11 seconds Native (alderlake): Starts at ~1800 micrseconds per task, climbs to ~5300 microseconds per task by the end Takes 2 minutes 9 seconds. The improvements aren't _that_ large on chunkloading, at least on my system. Regardless, I'm curious on everyone's thoughts about either changing the release cpu target to sandybridge (which colloquially is known as x86_64v2) and/or building baseline as a "legacy" package option for the probably few people who run CPUs older than 2011 and play this game. // DISCUSSION: [USER COMMENT] More data gathering: F4 overlay, all chunks loaded, 24 render distance: baseline: sandybridge: native (alderlake): Chunks still loading, 24 render distance: baseline: sandybridge: native (alderlake): [MAINTAINER COMMENT] If you want native performance then you have to compile it yourself, this will be possible with the mod loader. Instead you should focus your effort on real optimizations, such as e.g. improving the performance of the render thread so we are less CPU bound to begin with.

// ISSUE TITLE: Multiple structure maps
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Instead of just the 32x32x32 structure map, there should be multiple, created based on structure sizes. As an example for how the system might work:

1. We load the first biome, all of the structures have a max size of less than 32x32x32. Create the 32x32x32 structure map and add these to the pool.
2. The next biome has a few 32x32x32 structures and one stalagmite that could extend up to 50 blocks tall. The game chooses the smallest power of two that fits the structure, 64, and creates a 64x64x64 structure map. The stalagmite is then added to the pool.
3. Another biome has a huge crater structure that can generate a maximum diameter of 700. The smallest power of two greater than 700 is 1024, so a 1024x1024x1024 structure map is created and the crater is added to the pool.

When generating the world, structure map fragments are generated normally for all of the created structure maps, except chunks are treated like they are as big as the corresponding structure map size. (eg. a 64x64x64 structure map fragment would contain one list of structures for every 8 chunks.)

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Ok, so as far as I understand in current structure generation system, to generate a chunk you need to generate all structures within that chunk and attempt to generate all the structures in chunks directly adjacent to it. My understanding is that your system doesn't change that and effectively means that for bigger structure maps you will have to just generate more chunks around. Considering time complexity of that is like O(n^3) that ain't scaling well, no?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
#2176 has shown that even generating the current 128×128×128 structure maps has a large cost attached to it when it needs to load the cave maps to check where the structure needs to generate.
Generating cave maps in a (1024+margin)³ area is going to be a problem. Not only would it take 1 GiBit of storage to generate all these cave maps, but also generating them is quite expensive.

We need another solution.

// ISSUE TITLE: Grappling Hook | A range Weapon/Tool // ISSUE REPORT: ### Description Considering the massive caves and mountains in the game, the player needs a way to move that's balanced, easy to understand, and, above all, fun. The most obvious solution is a grappling hook. This is my proposal for how this hybrid transport tool and ranged weapon should be crafted and used. **The tool in question and its crafting recipe:*** *Description** The grappling hook is a tool/weapon whose purpose is to propel the player into walls or any target the hook grabs onto. **How does it work?** The grapple's first mode is the left-click attack mode. *The grappling hook will be thrown at the player's target, hitting and dealing damage if the hit connects, and the hook will immediately return to its idle mode. If it hits a mob, the mob will not be pushed but will be damaged, and if it's a block, it will take damage (See ATB:https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/1678) However, holding the right click is where the fun begins. The grappling hook will "charge up," which would be represented by an animation of it spinning around until it reaches its maximum charge.* When it reaches the maximum, the player can release the right click, and this will throw the grappling hook in the direction the player is facing. Now, if the grappling hook hits something when thrown, it deals damage to whatever it hits (be it a mob or block, although very little). Now, if the grappling hook hooks a mob, the player can right-click to propel themselves toward the grappling mob or left-click to pull the mob toward the player. In either case, the grappling hook releases and returns to the player. If the grappling hook is a block, then right-clicking pulls the player toward the block and releases the grappling hook. If left-clicking the block is enough, the block is mined if the grappling hook has enough "mining power." Now, whatever the case, when the grappling hook is released and returned to the player, there is a cooldown that lasts until the player touches the grappling hook and is able to use it again. **"What happens if I switch to another item while holding the grappling hook?"** If the grappling hook is attached to something, it is released and the cooldown is still executed. And if it is already in a cooldown, it remains in effect until the grappling hook returns to the player. **Grappling Hook Stats:** Range Distance Impact Damage Charge Speed Propulsion Power Reload Speed // DISCUSSION: [USER COMMENT] I've been thinking about this, and it has two problems. If ropes(https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/1966) added to the game (which I hope), either the grappling hook or the rope would be rendered useless. To solve this, the grappling hook should use rope as "ammunition" when the player propels themselves toward a target (block or mob) and the distance would be based of the ropes in the player inventory. The left-click attack doesn't consume rope. This would unite both mechanics. The second problem that's fixed with what I just posted is its idiotically huge crafting pattern. So, here it would only be the grappling hook itself, not the rope:

// ISSUE TITLE: An alternative to the Minecraft/Terraria drowning system. // ISSUE REPORT: ### Description In most games, the oxygen bar is a timer until being underwater starts to damage you until you come up for air. The problem is that, in a game like Cubyz, you can take a breath at any time just breaking some blocks or swimming back to the surface without any consequences for running out of oxygen, and this makes the built-in options for counteracting drowning and being underwater in general quite obsolete. This is my proposal to punish the player for running out of oxygen and push them to find ways to counteract drowning --> The player's oxygen bar acts as a timer before drowning. When it reaches 0, the oxygen bar becomes a drowning bar, and the following sequence occurs: **25% drowning ->** 1. Swimming speed is halved. 2. Attack speed and damage is halved 3. Block placement distance is halved 2. Mining speed is reduced to a 1/4 4. A blinding or vision reduction effect is applied to the player. **50% drowning ->** 1. Swimming speed to 1/4 2. The player will be no longer able to place or mine blocks 3. The blinding effect gets buffed 4. Weakness is applied **75% drowning ->** 1. The player sinks, it cant swim up anymore. 2. The player will be no longer able to attack 3. Weakness is buffed **100% drowning ->** The player drowns and die instantly lmao If the player manages to reach the surface the effects of drowning will still persist but the bar will drain and the effects will last until the oxygen bar is completely filled. // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] A drowning bar is a neat idea, I however don't like that the player will be weakened as they drown, as it's not very fair game design. I think it could be done differently. We could take a page out of Don't Starve's book and have the maximum health be drained as the player drowns, then slowly regenerate it while not drowning. That way, players cannot regenerate health faster than they can take drowning damage. [USER COMMENT] How is weakening a player by not taking care of their oxygen a "unfair game design"? (I'm not being sarcastic or being rude, but I really want to know why.) Another question: Does the "Drowning" effect you propose persist until the oxygen bar replenishes? [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > How is weakening a player by not taking care of their oxygen a "unfair game design"? Control should never be taken away from the player, as it can cause frustration. The debuffs listed also cannot be known while drowning and seem to cause exponential difficulty in escaping water. It would be good for a realistic game perhaps, but not here. > Does the "Drowning" effect you propose persist until the oxygen bar replenishes? The drowning effect reverts when the player is out of water, until their max hp is fully recovered. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > Control should never be taken away from the player, as it can cause frustration. Cubyz has unresponsive air control, what are you talking about 😂 [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > Cubyz has unresponsive air control, what are you talking about Control can't be taken away from the player if they never had control to begin with :^) [USER COMMENT] what if you just start losing hp as soon as u get in the water. you're a snail, you'd start dissolving :) [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Snale is neither slug nor snail

// ISSUE TITLE: Should clients disconnecting really be errors?
// ISSUE REPORT:
https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/blob/35eafce15d82a258db9da10e84ad9cc2e32a0c7c/src/network.zig#L602

Right now, it logs an error and appears to pop up an error window.

However, for a headless server, we cannot launch that window. And every error logged pops up that window.

Further, I think it may be heavy-handed to assume the client disconnecting is an error. That's likely just the client disconnecting, I don't see a need to log that action as an error.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
Additional context from log:

Chat: testUser┬º#ffff00 left
Disconnected
Disconnected
Got error.ConnectionResetByPeer on receive. This indicates that a previous message did not find a valid destination.
Could not find window with id error_prompt.

That last line is because this was running headless. But if this is not logged as an error, it should resolve.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I made it an error because in peer-2-peer when inviting someone it could mean that you entered an unreachable address, which in my opinion deserves an error popup. A fix of this would require separating the two modes.

[USER COMMENT]
That specific case I think would warrant it. 

Maybe we should propagate the error out of `ConnectionManager.run` so that either the server or the client can handle the error as needed?

The client getting that error would mean potentially wrong address.
The server getting that error would mean a previously connected client has disconnected. In my experience when the client crashes or the user simply closes the window that error occurs. But I feel it shouldn't be an error for the server to care about if the client caused its own unclean disconnect.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
It's not about server vs client. It's about whether the server is inviting players through the P2P system or not.

[USER COMMENT]
I'll drop this for now. If there comes a time that differentiating between an unreachable P2P address and an unclean client disconnect becomes more useful, perhaps we can revisit

// ISSUE TITLE: A possible solution for the implementation of flowing liquids
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

When browsing the Discord, I noticed some uncertainty on how flowing liquids could be added into the game. I'm not sure where the uncertainty comes from, but I'm assuming some of it is from the concerns presented by cubic chunks (infinite waterfalls, etc).

Water in voxel games like Minecraft behaves strangely. It acts more gelatinous than as full-on water. Realistically, water that starts as a source block from Y=250 and flow down as flowing water blocks would not stay cohesive enough to remain just in a square meter of space as it's falling. 

So my proposal to avoid making infinitely-tall waterfalls from just 1 source block is that water and other fluids should have a "dispersion" property. After a certain falling distance (based on environmental factors within the current biome, such as temp and etc), flowing liquid begins dispersing into droplets and then vapor. The moment the flowing liquid starts dispersing is when players can no longer swim up it.

Fluids should also be given a 'viscosity' value, and flow speed (in blocks per second) should be determined by the formula of:

```
F = (100 / V)

• F = Flow speed in blocks per second
• V = viscosity value
```

For example, Minecraft's water would have a viscosity of 25 since it flows at a rate of 4 blocks per second.

```
F = (100 / V)
F = (100 / 25)
F = 4

• Round the end result of F to the nearest integer
```

From a fluid's viscosity value, we can also determine the 'dispersion distance' value with a second formula that looks something like this:
```
D = (V * (1.5 / (B + 0.5))

• D = dispersal distance
• V = viscosity
• B = biome temperature (from 0.00 to 2.00)

• The 1.5 is a constant to ensure dispersal acts quicker in hotter biome temps and slower in colder biome temps.
• 0.5 is a constant being added to B to ensure we're not dividing by 0 if B = 0.00. It is also a large enough decimal value to ensure there isn't a runaway division involving setup for D. Ex; "1.5 / (0.0 + 0.5) = 3" INSTEAD OF "1.5 / (0.0 + 0.0001) = 150"
• Round the end result of D to the nearest integer
```

As for what happens when a flowing, non-dispersed liquid comes into contact with the top of another solid block as it falls, the dispersal distance resets to 0. This would allow for "chain waterfalls" as long as the liquid makes contact with another solid block after falling a height less than the dispersal distance.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
I like this, would work well with water on floating sky islands

There was some talk of more realistic water physics, like The Powder Toy, and if we go with that we still need a way to deal with oceans draining into caves

// ISSUE TITLE: Allow breaking block with wrong tool/item
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Since I can break blocks with my bare hands, it should be possible to break a block with any item in my hand. If it's not the right tool, it should be as if I'm breaking the block with my bare hands. This is a nice QOL feature because I don't have to swap inventory slots as much.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This is intentional, to prevent tool misuse. If every block can be broken by any tool, player cannot be sure that they are using optimal tool for a material. Allowing that also creates new problems, like potentially (not) dropping resources, which are a poor experience for the player too.

[USER COMMENT]
Players intentionally break blocks with the wrong tool in Minecraft all the time, for example mining dirt with a pickaxe. Not crafting a shovel leaves an extra slot in the inventory, which is a valid gameplay strategy.

Minecraft makes it obvious that a block isn't meant to be mined with a given tool, by making the block take a long time to break. Also, soft blocks which take a very short time to break usually always drop something. There are a few exceptions like grass and glass, and I honestly have mixed feelings about Minecraft's silk touch mechanic, but that's beside the point.

If I am able to break something with my bare fist, I should be able to break it with a tool in my hand. It's not just a gameplay issue; it just makes physical sense. A wall is not harder to punch with a shovel than with my bare fist, regardless of what the wall is made of. Sure, maybe if you allowed crafting tools out of leaves, that might make sense, but you don't allow that (and I don't see why you would want to, unless you were going for something like Tinker's Construct).

Sure, if you already know all the details of the gameplay, this may seem an arbitrary decision, but trust me, it is not. Cubyz is supposed to be fun for everyone, including players who come here without previously experiencing X or Y game. In this case switching to adjacent slot to break dirt block is a negligible price from avoiding poor experiences for new players. 

Real problems you have mentioned, like inventory space, are intended to be handled in different ways than breaking e everything with everything. In general, we prefer to counteract problems in their roots, instead of hiding the consequences. // ISSUE TITLE: Cursors should hide when textbox is not focused
// ISSUE REPORT:
Currently textboxes open with no cursor. After clicking or otherwise entering the textbox, a cursor is displayed. So far is correct behavior.

### Proposal:

Clicking out of the textbox, or hitting escape, or any other action to focus away from the textbox should remove the cursor. Currently, the cursor cannot be removed, even when there are multiple active textboxes. _If you cannot input text, there should not be a cursor_.

This would be in line with standard OS textboxes (nuances aside), and would show the user whether they are currently able to type into a textbox.

### Debate:

Up for debate is what should happen to selections when the user clicks away from them. I argue that we should remove both the cursor and the selection for simplicity.

For reference, MacOS allows separate windows to maintain their own independent cursors and selections. Browsers support a separate cursor/selection in the search bar from the displayed webpage. Focusing in the search bar will hide a webpage's cursor but not a selection. There is also some nuanced behavior that happens when clicking back into a webpage's textbox. We should absolutely not try to emulate this in Cubyz. We would only cause more confusion and waste more effort this way. We should limit Cubyz to keeping track of a single cursor within the entire application. Cubyz menu-windows are not fully featured the way that separate applications' windows are.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> I argue that we should remove both the cursor and the selection for simplicity.

Yeah, I agree, we should keep it simple.

// ISSUE TITLE: Allow tags in recipes // ISSUE REPORT: ## Description Currently recipes can only use `BaseItem` as input and output. It would be nice to have a system that would allow using tags to specify input and output of recipe to avoid duplicating all the recipes for all the wood types and alike. In UI Input/output specified via tag would be displayed like normal one, but would cycle through all the items matching given tag, changing e.g. every second. // ISSUE TITLE: branch problem: should they decay too? or not?
// ISSUE REPORT:
Should branches act like logs and prevent leaves from breaking? (current implementation)
Which could lead to very annoying searches for branches in leaves.

Or we let them decay too. 

Or only leafy branches into non-leafy branches.

Discuss!

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
They should decay like leaves, because they are hard to track down. If we don't decay them, leaf blobs will remain as they are, especially for the SBB trees. They look nice, but we have to compensate for their poor cutting down experience. Alternative is giving up visuals which sucks in a game revolving around huge render distance.

Although, branches are somewhere in-between  - they also have to preserve attached leaves if the branches are connected to logs, otherwise large trees (like ancient sapele from jungle PR) may not work as expected, as they can have leaves like 8 blocks away from the trunk.

[USER COMMENT]
One solution could be a two tiered decay system.
Leaves either require Logs or Branches
and Branches require Logs

[USER COMMENT]
This is my ideal branch decay:

- when a tree's branches are no longer connected to a log, decay the branches.
- when a thin tree (made of only branches) is no longer connected to the ground, decay the whole tree. 
- when a thin tree is broken in the middle of it's trunk, decay the branches that are no longer connected to the ground (the floating half of the tree)
- block updates should not break thin trees

branches "being connected" means the actual branch connections from its rotation mode, it should not care about adjacent blocks it is not physically connected to

I think #2328  is very much needed before this, so that you don't lose the wood you would get from the branches when they decay.

// ISSUE TITLE: What versioning scheme will Cubyz use?
// ISSUE REPORT:
For now we have no versions, no rules about versions, just 4 milestones. But what about the future? Maybe we should already have versions? Maybe we should consider indicating that there were meaningful changes in the meantime? With play session every two weeks we could easily just push version tags to repo instead of saying "latest master".

I am aware of few versioning practices:
- "Folder versioning" - no clear rules how numbers change, just make it bigger with every release (Lethal Company)
- [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/) - most informative, with clear rules for each number included in version
- [Calendar Versioning](https://calver.org/) - basically folder versioning with extra steps
- Build ID - usually obtained through incrementing build number for every internal build
- Git hash - hash of a commit used as version, basically meaningless
- Bullshit versioning - whatever is Minecraft doing at the moment with releasing new features in patches.

I am creating this issue because I want to argue about how we should handle versions, but I can't do that without knowing if we even treat versions seriously or if they are just arbitrary numbers. I couldn't find any related issue.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
For all internal purposes I think we should just use a simple incremental version number.
Now externally, semantic version might be best. Although I'm not sure if we will even have that many pure bug fix releases, so it might end up mostly irrelevant.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I would strongly recommend using same versioning scheme and narrowing the gap between what's internal and external as much as possible.
I do think that semver is best choice here (or even every where 🙃) and with some guidelines how to apply it's rules to what we do, we can get really far.
In our case patches could be used to immediately deploy fixes for assets and code, regardless of minor versions which could aggregate changes and have fixed release cycle. There is also stuff like API breaks, changes which make it impossible to load saves with older versions of the game etc. and it would need to be determined how we approach those and if we even try to backport fixes if it doesn't require any additional work.

That said, I think there is no reason to wait with version 0 - Cubyz development style will not magically change with version 0.
In semver version 0 has a very specific meaning - it is for development with complete freedom what we tag as new versions (just w/o changing major version) 
If we go for semver, the first roughly feature complete version should be 1.x.y
I would recommend tagging new minor 0.x.0 before each play session, ie. with 2 week aggregation cycle and tagging new patch after each bug fix. We can autogenerate releases on GitHub after tag push and generate release notes based on commit titles for now, it's generally a bad practice, but good enough for in-development 0.x.y

If we go with this approach, I would suggest getting copy of semver as markdown and including it in repo.

Its not long date ranges that make releases be on time. It is setting clear boundaries and saying "enough" instead of going for another round of fixes. There is always something that could be improved. However if we were not motivated enough to work on those improvements before the deadline, then they surely can wait for another release (and that is why those releases should be frequent!). Please remember we are talking here about **minor** **development** releases. For fixes you can always make one, two, three or even more patch releases in between and a non-development release cycle is a completely different story.

Many projects set their release schedules ahead of time or have clear rules about phases in release cycle, eg. Python interpreter, Linux kernel or even theoretical work like C++ standards. There is a schedule and if something isn't finished until a deadline it is postponed to next release. [If they were to postpone releases because some random guy decided he needs to add a new feature in bugfix merge window they would have a constant mess](https://youtu.be/O0X8sjMkOh8?si=sK3F-nitwW4AjrYG) .

I think adopting systems like those early will make it easier in the future. The more people are contributing the more opinions there will be and it will be more likely that people will argue and get angry. Now we can avoid this and inform people what they are getting into.

Also, a random thought - not every release has to be an event, but you can easily make an event out of release which introduces something cool.

---

There is also different reason to start doing releases - you can't just sit one day and create system that will seamlessly integrate zig codebase, versioning, launcher, updates and god knows what else to create good user experience. We need to add those parts one by one and try integrating them together, see what comes up, what works and what is a waste of time. Only way to do that is to actually do stuff, eg. do those god damned releases we are arguing here about and not spend all of our time on doing that stuff.

Starting in October this year (would be great if you could make a release script that automatically compiles and packages and uploads the releases for Linux and Windows until then).

And if we notice that this strategy doesn't work out we can switch at any time.

// ISSUE TITLE: `main.items.Item` should have one more state: `null`
// ISSUE REPORT:
As a result, when `Item` union is used it would no longer need to be optional (eg. as part of a stack), as optional-ness would be included as part of the struct itself.

---

This could simplify constructs like:

```rust
stack.item != null and stack.item.? == .baseItem and std.mem.eql(u8, stack.item.?.baseItem.id, "cubyz:selection_wand")
```

To just:

```rust
stack.item.hasId("cubyz:selection_wand")
```

(With appropriate implementation of hasId method)

---

Instead of:

```rust
if(_item == .baseItem) {
	if(_item.baseItem.block() != null and _item.baseItem.block().? == newBlock.typ) {
		return .{.yes_costsItems = 1};
	}
}
```
We could have:

```rust
if(_item.hasBlock(newBlock.typ)) {
	return .{.yes_costsItems = 1};
}
```

---

```rust
if(item == .baseItem and item.baseItem.block() != null and item.baseItem.image().imageData.ptr == graphics.Image.defaultImage.imageData.ptr)
```

To:

```rust
item.isBlock();
```

---

```rust
switch(_item) {
	.baseItem => |baseItem| {
		if(std.mem.eql(u8, baseItem.id(), "cubyz:chisel")) 
```

To:

```rust
std.mem.eql(u8, _item.id(), "cubyz:chisel")
```

Last one implies shared ID space between tools and items, imo as viable as shared ID space between SBBs and blueprints 😜 

---

Basically we could move some of the dispatches between properties of different types of the item into helper methods.

We would remove constructs similar to one below, scattered across codebase:

```rust
fn isItemBlock(block: Block, item: main.items.ItemStack) bool {
	return item.item != null and item.item.? == .baseItem and item.item.?.baseItem.block() == block.typ;
}
```

Grep for `baseItem.` for more use cases.

As for `tagSelector`, this is mostly a place-holder idea to implement #1348, included only because its is related to `Item` tagged union.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I like the idea of encoding the empty case in the union instead of an optional.
Though I would prefer calling it `.null` instead of `.empty`.

And yeah more helper functions for the union, like isBlock would be useful too.

However I do not think the tagSelector should be a thing.
Note that we'd have to handle this case everywhere we switch on the Item type, but in reality it would only be used for recipes.
I'd suggest to change how recipes are handled, instead of hacking it into the Item system.

// ISSUE TITLE: [MODS] Allow enough control over rendering to support 3rd party ray tracing implementations
// ISSUE REPORT:
Question: Can and should we provide enough rendering control for mods to allow 3rd party ray tracing?

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The answer is yes, but we shouldn't do it any time soon, since the current rendering architecture is not final yet.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Although honestly changing something that's so deeply integrated into the core game is not something that can be easily put into a moddable interface, so I think fork-modding is probably the right strategy for this. This would also give them more control over other parts of the engine, e.g. they would probably not want to generate and store voxel floodfill light data.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Ok, that is true, there is no clear separation between the game and graphics engine, so this is not viable most likely.

[USER COMMENT]
Hi I'm interested in writing my own ray tracing renderer for Cubyz primarily for offline screenshots but would also like to explore realtime ray tracing. This is best done via Vulkan or DX12 due to hardware RT support. 

Given this I believe we have a couple options for how to provide some level of modding support for custom renderers:

1. Rewrite the rendering engine in Vulkan and add modding support to that. I believe this option in a non-starter due to it's complexity and maintenance burden. It's simply not worth it at this point unless somebody really wants to spearhead it.

2. Share GPU memory between Vulkan and OpenGL and write to the framebuffer from Vulkan but present it in OpenGL. This is doable and would have great performance (zero copy) but is a bit of a nightmare when it comes to cross platform and cross vendor support. (It's a lot more viable on Linux with DMA-BUF with what I can tell). I think this probably not an option but needs to be investigated further.

3. Blit Vulkan image into OpenGL texture (GPU->GPU copy), from what I can tell this is supported on all vendors across both Windows and Linux (didn't check OSX). While this isn't zero-copy like above it is still very performant and enough for hgih framerate realtime purposes. I think this is a real option that shouldn't:tm: require too much modification of the existing code.

4. GPU->CPU->GPU copy. While will work 100% of the time it is also bound to have serious performance problems in many cases. This should only ever be used as a worst case fallback.

5. Keep the current rendering engine in OpenGL but use Vulkan to present. This avoid rewriting the engine but still will require quite a bit of dev time and effort. I think option 3 is probably better.

6. Abstract the rendering at a higher level or even separate it completely, this may or may not be possible with the current codebase. I suspect this will require a lot of thought around design if this were to be the approach taken so I think this is not the way to go for now.

My personal opinion is that 3 is the best option for now (with 4 as an optional fallback but 2 as an optional optimization). From what I can tell this would require an API to 1. disable OpenGL world rendering (as in the "main renderer", OpenGL is still used to present) 2. Get relevant CPU data that would be used to render the world. 3. Do the stuff outlined in option 3. 

I'll look into these options in more detail when I get time (no promises as I'm quite busy right now) but I'd love to hear thoughts on my ideas.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Note that rewriting to Vulkan is already accepted and set in motion (#102) though it will probably take quite some time. Though at least some of the setup stuff is already done and you could make use of that, and possibly contribute to the whole vulkan rewrite project.

Other than that, if you want this for screenshots, then I wonder: Why even bother with moving the data back to OpenGL? Why not just make a completely separate renderer in Vulkan that only renders the things you want in the screenshot (terrain, entities, particles, item drops) and then directly outputs them to an image.

[USER COMMENT]
> Note that rewriting to Vulkan is already accepted and set in motion ([#102](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/102)) though it will probably take quite some time. Though at least some of the setup stuff is already done and you could make use of that, and possibly contribute to the whole vulkan rewrite project.

That's great to hear, I'll definitely check it out.

> Other than that, if you want this for screenshots, then I wonder: Why even bother with moving the data back to OpenGL? Why not just make a completely separate renderer in Vulkan that only renders the things you want in the screenshot (terrain, entities, particles, item drops) and then directly outputs them to an image.

For screenshots it's not super necessary but it would be nice to have it integrate well with the existing UI and window (with a live preview). For realtime rendering it's obviously a different story and integration is of much higher priority.

// ISSUE TITLE: The amount of damage a tool takes when it swings should be smaller when the amount of health left is smaller.
// ISSUE REPORT:
For example, if a block takes 1.1 swings to break, the last 0.1 should do only 10% of the regular damage to the tool. It takes much less effort to break a block 90% broken already, and it should cause much less strain on the tool.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Wouldn't that partially undermine the reason for having dedicated swings in the first place?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The problem is that making slightly suboptimal pickaxe basically doubles its swing time and halves its durability, this is a very severe disadvantage, often making the pickaxe waste of resources and discourages experimenting with pickaxes anywhere outside of the workbench - as we saw during last play session either you do the math or you are left with something that is 4 times worse than what you could have crafted (2x swing time, x0.5 durability, since you have to swing twice). Forgive me for the simplification I used here, of course there are materials which require more than one swing to mine, but majority of blocks you will mine in your Cubyz experience will be one-hit mined blocks, ores etc. are much much much smaller portion compared to building blocks / regions underground you would want to clear to build a base there.

I see few solutions:
- one of them is what is proposed in the title, but to tweak it we could make it non-linear, so being closer to required damage is less punishing, while being off 70% is almost as punishing as just having to swing again. This does not solve swing time issue tho.
- different approach would be to make the damage and swing time slightly randomized. As an effect being off by 0.1 of your pickaxe damage would mean that most of the time you would still break the block you are mining. We can either introduce upper-lower caps in tool description, or treat displayed information as lower cap, so it is not annoying. This would open doors to have other randomized modifiers which eg. increase the chance to get higher upper bound for the damage, luck would make sense again.
- we could add flat damage on last strike if we are just a bit off with exponentially growing durability cost. This does not solve durability problem, but solves the swing time problem.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
You know, one of these fixes sounds like #1339 :^)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Cool, more reasons to go down the luck road

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Wouldn't that partially undermine the reason for having dedicated swings in the first place?

Also yes, it would XD

That's why I proposed "over-mining" the other day, because it brings attention to the per-swing

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> different approach would be to make the damage and swing time slightly randomized.

I think the time to break a block should not change randomly, you said yourself in https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/1430#issuecomment-2869610854 that already encountering a coal vein can break your rhythm. If every tenth swing randomly takes only one swing to break the block instead of 2, then I think that could lead to more frequent mistakes. Like imagine you want to break a block that's in front of a carefully chiselled low health block.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Also, we should make it a point not to hide important information and mechanics from the player. I call these "invisible mechanics" and they often cause frustration and confusion while giving an advantage to people who read wikis.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Also, we should make it a point not to hide important information and mechanics from the player.

Yes, I absolutely agree. I also observed this effect with Gera yesterday. I designed the current balance system so I knew perfectly well where I needed to put my things to optimize the swing time of a tool, but Gera was cluelessly trying random things.

As much as I enjoy working with the impact of elasticity, I think it might be too difficult to discover it on your own.

Also another important thing that goes in the same direction: Right now efficiency is strongly dependent on knowing the exact health of the target block. If you don't know that the health of sandstone is 20, then you will have a very hard time creating a good pickaxe for it. And randomness doesn't really help here, in fact I think it probably makes it even harder to discover the real value naturally, since you now also need to know how to do statistics.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Hmm I am not sure about the message you linked, but could it be that it was in different context? Context of breaking multiple blocks vs breaking a single block (here). This is pure randomness, probably best way to interpolate integer values (like durability) and I don't see a way for anyone to exploit it even if they know it exists. If there was an algorithm that allowed you to predict when empowered swing happens, yes that could be exploited, but randomness? (Pseudo-randomness, but still)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Anyway, this may cause mistakes and make it harder to understand what's going on, that is true

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Yes, I absolutely agree. I also observed this effect with Gera yesterday. I designed the current balance system so I knew perfectly well where I needed to put my things to optimize the swing time of a tool, but Gera was cluelessly trying random things.
> 
> As much as I enjoy working with the impact of elasticity, I think it might be too difficult to discover it on your own.

Sounds to me like elasticity needs to be scrapped and tools shouldn't have averaged stats, instead opting for something that makes sense to the player

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Context of breaking multiple blocks vs breaking a single block (here).

The outcome is the same.
If you think you need 2 swings to break a block, and keep the mouse button pressed for two swings (let's say 400 ms).
Now suddenly, randomly, the block already breaks after 200 ms, so you have 200 ms to react, which (with input delay) is about the human reaction time, so there is good chance that you won't react in time.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Averaging is good for durability, not so much for other stats. It is logical that mixing worse materials with better materials will lower the overall durability, not necessarily the same thing happens other way around, but it is not that confusing.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Current swing time is a mess, eg. Adding  some wood to the top of the pickaxe can legitimately lower the swing time for no reason.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I agree that it would be more intuitive not to average things, but if we don't average the mass that makes any optional slots completely unviable since it obliterates your swing time. Unless we make swing time independent of mass, but that feels also wrong.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The old optional slots gave you more damage and durability in exchange for worse swing time. That was the draw.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Well, we can make the swing time less penalizing overall, so you can't really get it above .5 and below .2 without modifiers.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> The old optional slots gave you more damage and durability in exchange for worse swing time. That was the draw.

Yes, but they don't give you enough extra durability to counteract the loss of damage and additional time you have to spend on swinging.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Yes, but they don't give you enough extra durability to counteract the loss of damage and additional time you have to spend on swinging.

You could make it additive for those slots in particular

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> You could make it additive for those slots in particular

That completely ignores weak spots in the tool.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We could re-add fragile modifiers on stone and remove the fragile modifier while encased? Either way I absolutely disagree with making them 50%, this is way too much.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Additive durability doesn't make sense tho, if you mix weak material with strong material you don't get even stronger material.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Alright crazy idea time:

// ISSUE TITLE: Speed boost when breaking a block based on leftover damage
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior

An alternative to over-damage #1430 to solve #1643

Basically if you break a block with leftover damage, then the next swing will be faster based on the remaining damage.
e.g. if you have a tool that deals 50 damage at 1 swing/s and a block that has 10 health, then this means that the next swing will be shorter by 40/50 = 80% of its swing time, so in this case a swing would only take 0.2 seconds

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Wait, that's complicated. Why not just transfer leftover damage to the next swing?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Wait, that's complicated. Why not just transfer leftover damage to the next swing?

Why didn't I think of that before?!!

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Why not just transfer leftover damage to the next swing?

Because then we have to wait another 1 second before breaking the next block with 90 damage against 10 health, gaining us nothing.
It needs to change the time.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Wouldn't the swing time infinitely stack then?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
No, it wouldn't since it's only applied to the next swing.
It would just mean that the swing time gets basically 5 blocks/second in this example except from the initial swing.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Okay, but doesn't this also count as an "invisible mechanic?"

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Well, it would be quite visible in the animation, or what do you mean?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I mean how would the player know what is effecting this, and by how much?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Do they need to know? If at all this makes the DPS metric when crafting a tool more accurate, since it's now independent of block health (still depends on block resistance, but that's usually less relevant).

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think it makes less sense than having over-damage, and it could throw off the player's rhythm. Plus.. there's not much you could do with such a concept in terms of modifiers.

I suppose if you want to go this route, you should make a prototype to see if it feels good.

// ISSUE TITLE: Should we have one exclusive crafting station for each tool type?
// ISSUE REPORT:
This would allow us to move one UI element, the tool choose button, into the world, making it more immersive and less annoying (#1067).

Furthermore this gives the player more options and more reasons to decorate. And this restriction could simplify some future features, like having a stored inventory for these blocks.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This can be both nice and annoying. 
- You would have to run between stations to make tools.
- Carrying more than one station is not practical, so you will probably make them and leave them after you are done crafting if you are caving atm.
- They are useful blocks with decoration value, no more inverted stairs to just fill your house. 
- We would no longer need to solve ordering problem for tools, as now you can place the blocks as you please and you always have the tool you right clicked.
- This does mean that adding new tool now involves adding UI for it (will there be a generic UI?) or at least a crafting station block textures.
- Each crafting station should have visibly distinct texture including shape of the tool both on top and on the side, so player don't have to remember details of the station.
- This simplifies UI of each tool crafting station (no drop down)
- This further clutters crafting list.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> so you will probably make them and leave them after you are done crafting if you are caving atm.

I'm already doing that anyways, I gladly trade the occasional 4 wood for an extra slot.

Also note that this is in line with the "give the player a reason to return to base" design that you and Ikabod have been pushing for.

> This does mean that adding new tool now involves adding UI for it (will there be a generic UI?) or at least a crafting station block textures.

// ISSUE TITLE: Make wood too resistant for player to break by hand
// ISSUE REPORT:
### How will player obtain first wood?

Branches.

### Why?

To force player to make axes, to use them and to allow logs to be less common resource.

### Why make the wood less common resource?

So we can stop balancing it around assumption that player has to break it by hand.

### What is wrong with wood balancing?

Damage range for axe is very narrow, it will be hard to balance and create incentive to make powerful axes, swing time will be the most important stat, which is not exactly healthy.

### That doesn't seem like much, what else?

- Totems, we can now control how much progression player has to do before they are allowed to have totems. This gives our second limiting factor aside from vertical progression without relying on rare materials which may be very hard to find with Cubyzs large biomes.
- Player has a very good reason to return to surface, as gathering large amount of wood early in the game is not as trivial.

### Can't players just break a lot of branches to gather a lot of wood?

Yes, that is why they should few seconds to break, so it is not practical. Additionally they could craft into one planks block, instead of two. Enough to craft basic tools, but the process should be time consuming enough so you want to create axe as soon as possible. Wooden axe still would not be able to break logs, but would speed up breaking branches. Stone axe would probably allow breaking logs, but slowly, eg. 2s per log.

### So, surface progression parallel to vertical progression?

No more running just deeper and deeper. You have to go and come back for wood. This means infrastructure is no longer optional, it is a must.
IMO, copper axe should still be inconveniently slow, eg. 1s per block, so you have a good reason to gather some and go search for iron.
This means that you have similar surface progression to vertical progression. After being capable of comfortably gathering wood with iron axe, you will be able to dive deeper / explore further to create better axes for rare wood types which require even better tooling.

### But I don't like running back and forth

Well, that is good for making sure player doesn't just skip all the progression steps by just playing dropper for an hour, sure, materials have trane-offs or whatever, but we all know that end-game tools have to be just better, otherwise having them wouldn't make sense. So we want to slow down progression. Go gather some resources on the way back, you definitely didn't collect all the copper and coal (or maybe you just missed some?). Additionally, this increases impact of forest caves, with that change they become very, very impactful, instead of being just cool thing to encounter when you have 3 stacks of wood in inventory.

// ISSUE TITLE: Event scheduling
// ISSUE REPORT:
I am proposing same thing I described [here](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/pull/1338#issuecomment-2891804511), basically events following Command Design Pattern: You declare a function and set of arguments you want it to be run with and you schedule it to be run after n server ticks in the future. Server should save which actions it was supposed to run when it closes if there are any not run so it can load them back next time it starts. Actions may require information about their position, so they are bound to a chunk and loaded with that chunk. 

This would allow us to implement fast leaf decay as an event that would be scheduled by breaking log block. That event would check direct neighbors of that log to check if they are leaves or logs and either destroy or keep them accordingly, possibly scheduling next event with higher radius. This would distribute the load of destroying the leaves for multiple ticks and would allow for batching light updates.

This could also be used to spawn torch particles every X ticks. This system is effectively a cousin of random ticks but more reliable when you want to enforce certain speed of actions, instead of relying on randomness to make it right.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> This could also be used to spawn torch particles every X ticks.

Note that these would require different systems, since leaf decay is server side and particles are client side.

Also wouldn't all of this already possible with block entities? We just need to add an update function to the VTable that can be used to iterate over all active block entities of that type.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Do you want to make all the oak logs or all the leaves block entities? Both of them are very basic building blocks. The problem is also that doing poof all leaves are gone may not give best impression.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Right now in #1446 I have the block entity system set up such that e.g. empty signs or empty chests wouldn't have a block entity instance associated to them.
This could be used to implement events, when you start the event, just instantiate the underlying block entity, and on deactivate you can remove it again. You wouldn't have to make everything a block entity.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Ok, then I will close it, we will return to the topic while implementing specific features.

// ISSUE TITLE: Inlined SBB definitions
// ISSUE REPORT:
## Description

As requested in https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/pull/1382#discussion_r2073723613
Allow inlined SBB declarations, so it is not necessary to create SBB zon file when SBB has **no children**.

## Syntax could be:

```zig
.{
	.blueprint = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/branch_3",
	.children = .{
		.crimson = .{
			.{.structure = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/leaf_1"},
			.{.structure = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/leaf_2"},
			.{.structure = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/leaf_3"},
		},
	},
}
```

Where `cubyz:tree/oak/1/leaf_1` must map to existing blueprint asset.

## Instead of:

```zig
.{
	.blueprint = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/branch_3",
	.children = .{
		.crimson = .{
			.{.structure = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/leaf_1"},
			.{.structure = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/leaf_2"},
			.{.structure = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/leaf_3"},
		},
	},
}
```

And `assets/cubyz/sbb/tree/oak/1/leaf_1.zig.zon` with:

```zig
.{
	.blueprint = "cubyz:tree/oak/1/leaf_1",
}
```

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Why not connect the ids, so we wouldn't need the `.blueprint` field?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I have a suggestion, that may or may not be related to this:

.{
	.blueprint = identical,
	.children = .{},
	},
}

For specifying files in sbb that have the same relative path in blueprints

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Why not connect the ids, so we wouldn't need the `.blueprint` field?

Because dynamic interpretation of IDs and data structures creates a lot of pain when there is an error, creates generic and unhelpful error messages and painful edge cases when the interpretation is ambiguous and you have to check for those. Best contract is the strict one, there is no cost on asset creator side to explicitly state their intent, and ofc in implementation there will be no blueprint field, it will simply imply different data structure, mutually exclusive with normal SBB.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> I have a suggestion, that may or may not be related to this:
> 
> .{ .blueprint = identical, .children = .{}, }, }
> 
> For specifying files in sbb that have the same relative path in blueprints

This is orthogonal to this issue, it is basically a workaround for not wanting to write down unambiguous and hence long IDs. I think it is better to avoid having SBB file for those with no children instead of removing even more of the contents of that file. By the way .children can and should be omitted when empty. It should be an error if you do that and there are children TBD

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I can create Visual Studio Code extension that provides autocompletion for Cubyz IDs as a better workaround.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Because dynamic interpretation of IDs and data structures creates a lot of pain when there is an error, creates generic and unhelpful error messages and painful edge cases when the interpretation is ambiguous and you have to check for those. Best contract is the strict one, there is no cost on asset creator side to explicitly state their intent, and ofc in implementation there will be no blueprint field, it will simply imply different data structure, mutually exclusive with normal SBB.

Well we have this for textures, e.g. there is one name.png, one optional name_fog.zig.zon, one optional name_emission.png and so on. I don't see any ambiguities there, and if we put the .blp and the .zig.zon in the same folder it's also hard to make mistakes.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Blueprints are independent from SBBs and can have different use cases than just SBBs (eg. segment based maze generation or generator with no intersection between structures guarantee)

Anyway, should they share ID space or only directory structure?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Blueprints are independent from SBBs and can have different use cases than just SBBs (eg. segment based maze generation or generator with no intersection between structures guarantee)

Well, in those cases these probably either also want substructures (e.g. to randomize rooms in a maze), or they don't have any substructures, in which case the .zon file would be empty anyways, right?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
What if you have a different SBB that uses the same blueprint as some other one? For example cubyz:birch/stem/0 uses cubyz:oak/stem/0 just with substitution map. Now since blueprints are Independent from SBBs you just link both to the same blueprint and if you change the SBB storage structure you can leave the blueprint the way it is. If you store blueprint alongside SBBs you can choose to desync them or propagate changes to all related SBBs. Also no shortcut syntax for those, as they can't be next to each other for folder names to be meaningful. Your proposal assumes that not very blueprint needs SBB, but makes it awkward in an inversed case, where not every SBB needs a blueprint.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Why not connect the ids, so we wouldn't need the `.blueprint` field?

How would that work? If you have SBB and Blueprint of the same ID, you need implicit precedence of SBB over blueprint and you are denying user the option to explicitly specify which one they want. This makes it also less readable, because you don't know if the ID is resolved to a bluepirnt or to SBB. And it also makes it more awkward during dispatch because you have to check two hash maps.

This is also different from the texture case, as those different textures have different destinations, they don't override each other. Here Blueprint and SBB would have the same designation, only one of them can be used to generate structure.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> What if you have a different SBB that uses the same blueprint as some other one? For example cubyz:birch/stem/0 uses cubyz:oak/stem/0 just with substitution map.

Well in my mind you'd then tell it the substitution map at the higher level, so in the place where you import it as a child, you'd say `.{.structure = "cubyz:oak/stem/0", .substitutions = .{...}}` instead of `.{.structure = "cubyz:birch/stem/0"}`
Or ideally just say `.{.structure = "cubyz:tree/stem/0"}` and define substitutions only once at the root level.

> This makes it also less readable, because you don't know if the ID is resolved to a blueprint or to SBB.
