// ISSUE TITLE: Is it time to go back to garbage collection?
// ISSUE REPORT:
No, this is not the java rewrite ;D

#### use case
A prime example for this would be #1244
It allocates a bunch of dynamically sized strings with the global allocator, the data structures knows and has control over all the allocations.
And most annoyingly there are many code paths that need to take care of freeing strings. Wouldn't it be nice to have garbage collection?

#### implementation idea
The basic operation is quite simple:
- All allocations happen with an ArenaAllocator
- Garbage collection is triggered after n allocations, where `n` is the number of active allocations on the previous garbage collect. (This means amortized 1 copy operations, at 2× memory overhead)
- On garbage collection it just creates a new arena, calls an application callback that iterates through all the alive pointers and copies them into the new arena, and then destroys the old arena.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I have decided to use a different form of garbage collection in #1413.
I think this concrete variant wouldn't be that useful in the end.

// ISSUE TITLE: Binary storage for inventories
// ISSUE REPORT:
Currently inventories are stored as Zon files. It was fine for few players but not good enough when things like chests get involved. 

- [ ] inventory storage format should be changed to binary (retaining backwards compatibility for loading). 
- [ ] when storing items we should use `BaseItem` `indexes` instead of string names to allow for item migrations.
- [ ] tools have to be stored too, we should also consider adding **tool palette**, migrations and indexes for them.

[Discord discussion](https://discord.com/channels/443805812390100992/702167480654757991/1355918197420855366)

Questions:
- Do we change the design of `Item` to instead of pointer to `BaseItem` contain `BaseItemIndex` or alike? It will not reduce memory usage, since tool must remain a pointer.
- Do we change `Tool` to contain `BaseItemIndex` instead of `BaseItem` pointers? It would reduce memory usage of `craftingGrid` and `materialGrid` 4 times on 64 bit systems (from u64 to u16) from roughly 200+2048 to roughly 50+512
- If we keep current object oriented design of `Baseitem`, does it make sense do anything beyond having `index` as property on `BaseItem` (we need to access that index for serialization, in all other places we have pointers)?

Related to: #1291

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> It was fine for few players

For players it should remain human-readable. This is quite useful for server hosts.

> tool palette

Yeah, that makes sense. We need some common ground to transmit it over the network.

> Do we change the design of Item to instead of pointer to BaseItem contain BaseItemIndex or alike? It will not reduce memory usage, since tool must remain a pointer.

Right now it wouldn't really make a difference, so I'm not sure if it's worth it. It would make sense to keep things consistent though.

> Do we change Tool to contain BaseItemIndex instead of BaseItem pointers? It would reduce memory usage of craftingGrid and materialGrid 4 times on 64 bit systems (from u64 to u16) from roughly 200+2048 to roughly 50+512

Yeah, that seems like it would be a nice reduction.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> For players it should remain human-readable. This is quite useful for server hosts.

Why is that useful?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Why is that useful?

I've needed it multiple times already:
Searching for and removing illegal/cheated items, migrating a player inventory to a player's new name, setting players gamemode with cheats disabled.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

`/inventory open <player-query>#hand`

> migrating a player inventory to a player's new name

`/inventory clone <player-query>#hand`
`/inventory clear <player-query>#hand`

> setting players game mode with cheats disabled.

`/permissions set <player-query> #admin enabled`

- command group:

`/permissions set <player-query> @worldedit enabled`

- individual commands:

`/permissions set <player-query> /set enabled`

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
What you describe here requires a lot more code and complexity than just simply having human readable player data.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Yes, but this decouples moderation of the server from physical (or remote) access to file system server runs on so this is desirable feature anyway. Maybe we shouldn't start working on it now, but long term imo such features should appear. Any server that is not "just for close friends" will require that.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Elasticity is material's ability to stretch or bend

// ISSUE TITLE: Handle ores on different stone types.
// ISSUE REPORT:
There are three options to handle:
1. don't, let the addon creator handle it(→ lots of duplicate work, but would allow making special textures for each ore and stone combination)
2. Generate a bunch of new block types during startup for all combinations of ores and stone. This would cost a bunch of "index space" reducing the maximum number of possible blocks.
3. Use rotation mode and store the source block in the 16 bit data section.

And the textures could either be auto-generated and stored in a texture, or recalculated in a shader.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> And the textures could either be auto-generated and stored in a texture, or recalculated in a shader.

The best option would be to just draw the overlay in a separate face, just like it's done for the block breaking animation.
This avoids the explosion of resources from storing generated textures, and it also means the shader has to be less complex.

// ISSUE TITLE: Increase player control in the air
// ISSUE REPORT:
You lose all control when jumping through the air, making it impossible to correct your trajectory after leaving the ground. Yes, air strafing is not realistic, but it feels nice for a video game like Cubyz.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This could be done like this:

The closer the player is to 0 y-velocity, the more air control they have.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This is a commonly requested thing, but after playing without it for a while, I think this is one of those things where we need to decide against the player comfort to make the game more fun overall.

- Without air control cave exploration is more dangerous and interesting, one falsely timed jump and you die, whereas with air control most of the time you could just correct your mistakes making it easy to navigate caves.
- Air control would make high slipperyness a bit pointless. If you can just get more contro when jumping, then a slippery surface doesn't bother you anymore.
- Not having it by default opens up more opportunities for accessories. E.g. a Terraria-style hook could help you save yourself when you slip off in a cave, or a glider could give you back more air control when at higher speeds.

So I have decided not to do this,

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think this should be reconsidered, since it would help a lot for detecting and fixing bugs.
But it would be nice if we could somehow pipe the output into a log file as well.

[USER COMMENT]
It seems that Zig 14.0 moves GPA into DebugAllocator and adds the "for ReleaseFast" SmpAllocator. GPA/DebugAllocator implies stack traces and optional safety features. SmpAllocator has neither traces nor safety but is faster (I do not have benchmarks). Previously we used GPA without safety features.

Enabling DebugAllocator+safety for debug scripts is a no-brainer. This is what we have now.

For release scripts, we have the options of DebugAllocator+safety (equivalent to switching to ReleaseSafe in Zig 13.0), DebugAllocator-safety (current), and SmpAllocator.

I feel like most people would want SmpAllocator for themselves, until they find the rare heisenbug and can't report it properly. If these bugs are common, perhaps DebugAllocator should be the default. Perhaps there should be some way to opt out in favor of SmpAllocator (`vi src/main.zig`, or by passing ReleaseFast in the commandline). If we start accepting compile flags in general, it might be useful to save them to a file like `build-type` for reporting.

TL;DR
Based on the previous message, Cubyz should use DebugAllocator when Zig 14.0 comes around.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Usual the types of bugs, that the DebugAllocator catches (→use-after-free and leaks and double frees), are easily caught in debug mode during development.
What I'm interested in are mainly assertion failures and just simply getting stack traces for segfaults.

// ISSUE TITLE: Open a terminal on linux when executing the `run_linux.sh` bash file from the file explorer.
// ISSUE REPORT:
Opening a bash file from the file explorer does not open a terminal by default.
Like in #381 this can lead to some confusion when a compiler error happens.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
As @garrett96 mentioned, you can test for a terminal with `test -t 0`. If true, then we're good. But otherwise, we have several rather ugly options.

Unfortunately, the name of the terminal app is not standardized, and there may be more than one.
Here's a list of common terminal applications compiled from [this SO post](https://superuser.com/questions/215483/how-can-i-open-a-new-terminal-window-from-a-terminal-in-linux).
```
For X11 -> xterm
For Kde -> konsole
For GNOME -> gnome-terminal
For xfce4 -> xfce4-terminal
For Cinnamon -> x-terminal-emulator
For MATE -> mate-terminal --window
For Unity -> gnome-terminal --profile=Default
For Pantheon -> pantheon-terminal -w ''
```

But we don't really know which one the user wants to open. It's possible to guess by using the most specific one, or the one they are running right now, or by trying to match `$TERM` or something like that. All this feature sniffing would take time and complexity and add even more confusion when it doesn't work. It also reminds me of browser sniffing on the web, so I'll leave that for someone else.

Alternatively we can just pipe everything to `screen` or a log file, and let the user know where to find the logs. But at that point we might as well ask that they open Cubyz in a terminal in the first place.

// ISSUE TITLE: Mac Developer Support
// ISSUE REPORT:
This issue is about merging the necessary modifications to allow Cubyz to compile on Mac.

This has several benefits:
- Prevent my modifications from constantly going stale.
- Allow Mac developers to contribute to Cubyz more easily, and potentially test features themselves.
- Forms an initial step for later pursuing #299.
- Allows us to give feedback to Zig and Mesa developers on their own Mac support.

This is similar but distinct to issue #299. #299 refers to native Cubyz support for Mac, which requires adapting Cubyz or OpenGL to work on Mac with native performance. This issue only requires that Cubyz compiles, regardless of playability.

Changes to consider:
- `Cubyz`:
  - Update `build.zig` with Mac libraries.
    - Note: Mac `libGL` will have to be in a non-standard location, unless we ship it with `Cubyz-libs`.
  - Allow GLFW context to be switched to EGL at runtime for debugging the Zink driver.
  - Unfortunately, from past experience, `run_linux.sh` might also involve rather invasive manual linking.
- `Cubyz-libs`:
  - Update `build.zig` to build libraries for Mac.
  - Note: because of the new platforms, this requires duplicating `libcubyz_deps.a` several times (20 MB with `-Doptimize=ReleaseSmall`), which may be undesirable.
- Other niceties, with secondary utility:
  - Install scripts or instructions for additional libraries/applications, such as Mesa LLVMpipe/Zink `libGL`. Alternatively ship our own versions of these.
  - Include GLFW as a submodule referencing the official GLFW [repo](https://github.com/GLFW/GLFW) or our own fork. This is for isolating those changes and updating the dependency easier.
  - Include an "official" build script for Cubyz-libs releases.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I'm mostly in favor of this. However since the future probably involves vulkan #102 I would like to avoid adding too much extra stuff, especially all the zink stuff, to the repositories for now.

> Install scripts or instructions for additional libraries/applications, such as Mesa LLVMpipe/Zink libGL. Alternatively ship our own versions of these.

You are the only person on mac who will be going through all that trouble to get a barely playable game, so honestly we don't need install instructions for now. I think install instruction might actually be harmful because their existence would give the impression that the game would work on mac, which it doesn't.

> Include GLFW as a submodule

Git submodules are annoying to use. It's better to use the zig package manager for that (similarly to how I did it for freetype and harfbuzz).

> Note: because of the new platforms, this requires duplicating libcubyz_deps.a several times (20 MB with -Doptimize=ReleaseSmall), which may be undesirable.

> Include an "official" build script for Cubyz-libs releases.

I've decided to extract these into a separate issue #325 which would be a requirement for this.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
By the way hasn't this been fixed a while ago?
You have been able to develop on mac for quite a while I think.

// ISSUE TITLE: Resolution up-/downscaling
// ISSUE REPORT:
I would love it if other games had this option.
My monitor is often too big for my integrated graphics card, which makes games lag and sometimes even setting all graphics features to lowest is not sufficient for lag-free gameplay.

Additionally this feature might be useful for debugging and performance-testing.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I've seen some games implement it as resolution scale, usually configurable from 25% (downscaling) to 200% (upscaling). It shouldn't be too hard to implement

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Yeah, super-sampling is also a good idea.
Though the downscaling might be a bit finicky. I cannot just linearly interpolate because I need to do some post-processing on the individual pixels first for fog calculations.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Usually, rendering scale is just applied to the viewport size, so let's say the window size is 800x600, a 75% would have a viewport size of 600x450 (like if the window was 600x450) instead of 800x600. So in theory it shouldn't change anything in the fog calculations (unless Cubyz can only render with sizes of powers of 2 but that's not the case) and the only thing that change is that the UI is still rendered at native resolution.

Basically do fog calculations before downscaling

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The problem is that the shader that currently renders from the internal framebuffer to the screenbuffer is also handling the fog.
This shader can and should be directly reused for upscaling, but for downscaling a new shader would be required, that manually samples and interpolates.
> a 75%

I don't want to do weird stuff like that. I want to use nearest-neighbor sampling for upscaling. I don't like how blurry it looks when games use linear sampling. And for nearest-neighbor sampling only powers of 2 make sense. So I would probably limit the entire thing to the factors ¹⁄₄, ¹⁄₂, 1, 2

// ISSUE TITLE: Mossmans
// ISSUE REPORT:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/33da6a0c-4f8a-48b2-9c4a-2aa04c3322bd)

**Mossmans Behavior is as follows:**

- Mossmans will spawn individually.
- They cannot move traditionally. Instead, they will burrow into the ground and pop out. They can only burrow out of full blocks and only move up to 16 blocks each time.
- Their idle behavior is observing other entities, wiggling, and self-grooming. They may also occasionally re-locate around their initial chunk.

Attacking:
- If an entity they are uncomfortable with approaches, they will immediately enter attack mode.
- Attack mode: unburrow near target --> look at target for ~3 seconds --> spit --> look at target for ~3 seconds --> burrow and relocate, repeat.
- They will always try to relocate to a spot within line-of-sight with the target. If their target is out of range, they will burrow again.
- When burrowing, they will not know where their target is, but rather the last place they remember the target being. If they cannot find the target, they will relocate a few times before giving up and going back to idle behavior. This may be interrupted if they find a new target.
- Mossmans will stop following if they are lead too far from their territory, opting to go back home instead of pursuing further.
- If mossmans see something being targeted, it will join in, for fun.

Stats:
- Mossmans are immune to knockback, but have little health. Inversely, their spit deals very little damage, but produces knockback.
- The purpose of this enemy is to be a bother at long distances, but be relatively easy to deal with otherwise. They also incidentally support other enemies by knocking their targets down if they try to escape by building up.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This is an inherent limitation of flood-fill lighting. There is no way around it without switching to a different technique.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
No, I know for certain that it won't work.
There is a reason brightness doesn't get added together in flood-fill.
If we were to add light from multiple light sources, then we'd need to repropagate all light sources in the area every time you place a block. This is insanely expensive.

// ISSUE TITLE: Game crashes - ability to run on older OpenGL
// ISSUE REPORT:
Hello.

I would like to play this game, but when I try to run it I get this error:
```
GLFW Error(65543): GLX: Failed to create context: GLXBadFBConfig

GLFWFailed

/tmp/Cubyz-master/src/main.zig:616:74: 0x4d21c2 in init (Cubyzig)

  window = c.glfwCreateWindow(width, height, "Cubyz", null, null) orelse return error.GLFWFailed;

                                                                         ^
/tmp/Cubyz-master/src/main.zig:694:2: 0x53a468 in main (Cubyzig)

 try Window.init();

 ^
```

I have searched a lot, and found out that all your shader files are made for OpenGL 3.4, while my computer supports 3.2 at most.

I also tried on other computer, but with the same error, as its maximum GL version is also 3.2.

Is there a way to run it on older OpenGL?

Because if most computers don't support 3.4, then your audience kinda grows much slower.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I'm sorry, but I won't support older OpenGL versions.
There is a bunch of things that I need from newer OpenGL versions, like compute shaders and shader storage buffers.
Without these it would be a bit of a pain to implement many of my features, and in the future I would probably like to take more advantage of compute shaders.

According to wikipedia most GPUs that were made within the last ten years should support OpenGL 4.6, which I'm currently targeting. So in 5-10 years, when Cubyz turned into a playworthy game, most people will have a GPU that supports it.

However there might be a solution here still:
1. If your GPU supports OpenGL 4.6, but your driver doesn't, then you could update your driver.
2. It might be possible to use an API translation layer. For example Mesa Zink translates OpenGL commands to Vulkan, which might be supported on your PC(I doubt it though).

[USER COMMENT]
I have an intel integrated gpu. My processor is i5-3570, which was released in june 2012, two months before 4.3 got released.

[USER COMMENT]
@IntegratedQuantum But really, new computers are expensive, and if something works good then why change it

Really, many people have such old (and even older) gpus/cpus. Not everyone is a gamer with sponsored latest hardware.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I get it, and I'm sorry.
But I got limited time on my hand, and I'd rather spend that time working on game features, than working on compatibility with 12 year old hardware.

[USER COMMENT]
@IntegratedQuantum Is there any way to emulate it or something?

You suggested mesa zink, but there aren't really any informations on how to get it working.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Honestly, I don't know much about it. Mesa was already installed on my linux machine and I could run it by setting some environment variable. It's probably much harder to get working on windows.
Also I'm not sure if your GPU even supports vulkan anyways.

[USER COMMENT]
I forgot where I saw this quote, but, "Zink can't expose more features than your hardware already supports". If your GPU doesn't physically have compute shaders, then you can't get GPU acceleration for compute shaders, no matter what translation layers you use. Compute shaders are more powerful than vertex/fragment shaders, so they can't be emulated via vertex/fragment shaders in general.

On the other hand, there is software rasterization such as Mesa's LLVMpipe driver, which offers OpenGL 4.6 without any GPU whatsoever. Only problem is, it doesn't use the GPU, so it renders Cubyz at 1 fps at a pitiful LOD. In theory you could mix hardware and software rasterization to take advantage of hardware acceleration when it is supported, but I doubt there is any work on this.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I have fully committed to using modern GPU features, and there is no way I'll ever go back.
And since this issue will only grow less severe over time, I might as well just close it now.

// ISSUE TITLE: Can we build Cubyz on android?
// ISSUE REPORT:
Hi there
Could you please support Cubyz to run on an android device?
Thank you so much!

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I want to support as many platforms as possible, but it's too soon for that.

1. Cubyz isn't much of a game yet, there is still a lot of stuff missing.
And honestly in a few years I'd rather have a fun game for PC than a fancy tech demo that runs on all devices.
2. I'm kind of having problems with java right now(the garbage collector is causing lag spikes) and I might end up switching the language. Now if I had everything ported to android already that would make it a lot harder to switch.
3. Supporting android is more than just "implement it once and then forget about it". Once it's there I have to put effort in maintaining and testing it. For example I'd have to make sure new graphics features run in OpenGL and OpenGL ES depending on the platform. And that early in development I might throw the feature out entirely a few updates later.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

However the open source nature of the game makes it possible for anyone to create a third party android version of Cubyz.

Single bar implementation has better handling of double handed weapons, it gets more complicated with double bar implementation, we would need to disable other hand bar when one of the hands selects a double handed weapon and that sounds more complicated than doing nothing.

// ISSUE TITLE: mass-independent swing time
// ISSUE REPORT:
An idea I came up with in https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/1567#issuecomment-2927006307

Basically swing time of tools is constant (except from modifiers/accessories).
Instead each tool has a new mass parameter, a sum over all densities, in order to swing you tool your strength needs to be larger than the mass.
Strength can be affected by accessories and effects.

If your strength is bigger than the mass you can use the tool at a constant swing time, otherwise (to be decided) you will have a large penalty (constant and significantly slower swing time) or you just cannot use it at all.
Strength can be improved with accessories, armor and effects.

If your strength is bigger than maybe (to be decided) `2×mass` then you use no energy for swinging the tool. Below that you will use energy depending on `2×mass - strength` (to be decided: It could also be a fixed threshold independent of your strength)

Initial player strength should be large enough to use early game tools (metal-tipped wood) without requiring any energy so you can slowly get started without having to worry about hunger from the start.
And it should be large enough to carry you through a big part of progression (until you need to start using gold) without having to worry too much about hitting the strength limit.

In the workbench UI and the tooltip it should be clearly visible if you don't have enough strength to use it and how much energy it uses per swing.

// ISSUE TITLE: Critical damage when a tool breaks
// ISSUE REPORT:
Basically the Breath of the Wild mechanic.
On the last point of durability, your tool does critical damage, and then breaks. This does a few things:
- It's cool
- Lets you break the block you were in progress of mining (instead of having to get another tool, then go mine it again.)
- Encourages you to use your tool until it breaks (for the satisfying critical damage)
- Makes the `single_use` modifier slightly more useful

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
How big would this critical damage be?

[USER COMMENT]
IMO the crit multiplier should differ between players and monsters. Having a 2x multiplier against players will lead to a meta where PVP players run around with alot of almost-broken weapons, just to gain an edge over other players.

// ISSUE TITLE: Pawn - Test Entity
// ISSUE REPORT:
The pawn will act as a basic testing entity. The behavior is as follows: 

- When they see the player, they enter attack mode.
- In attack mode, they will walk towards the player, pathfinding its way around the environment. It can jump over gaps too.
- If you are far away enough from them, they will exit attack mode and stop persuing the player.

That's it! This will just be a test of basic behavior and pathfinding.

Note:
The mob does not have eyes on the back of their head, so they have to be looking at you in order to spot you.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
IMO the multi-break is a good idea, tho only if breaking blocks directly behind one you hit (so it doesn't instantly make sulfur `explosive` modifier obsolete), also this should only affect blocks that would be in range for you considering your current position. It creates a natural cap on how high you may want a tbbt on a tool unless the tool is only for gathering resources. You would not want your pickaxe for tweaking single blocks in your builds to have too high tbbt, otherwise it will be hard to use - and that is good, this means that you get wider tbbt range to work with while balancing and you are creating a tradeoff for player to decide. win-win situation. 
This also means that you can legitimately think about pickaxe power by considering it tbbt instead of thinking separately about swing time and damage, you still can balance them separately, but you can measure them via one value, more tools, easier to interpret. We were already doing that, but that was a mistake - there is a huge difference between a block that one shots a block with double the power needed and one that one shots with just a little over power needed. 
There is also separate problem to consider - relation between tbbt and durability - lower damage means more swinging, so I propose separate measure `durability per <block> break` (durability / damage, lower is better) and to consider it during balancing too. This creates two simple ways to mesure how good a tool is at doing its job, taking all 3 tool properties into consideration.

> Mass. is mass. More mass = slower swing speed. I don't know why the heck it's averaged.

It's not realistic at all, but there are two big advantages of averaging the tool properties:
1. It's easier to balance: Take the material with the highest and lowest masses, multiply them with the factor and you instantly get the available range of values. There is no variance from using more slots, so one less variable that you need to worry about.
2. It makes it viable to use the optional slots, before the cost to swing time from the added mass of just one optional slot could make the tool a lot worse, and if you need 3-4 more slots to add a 50% damage boost, then you might just break even with a significantly larger material cost.

// ISSUE TITLE: The game lags when flying with hyperspeed at large render distance
// ISSUE REPORT:
## Problematic case

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/055c3113-01d8-41cf-85b0-d7f242386155)

Flying around with hyper speed (quite a lot tbh) results in high fragmentation of ChunkMesh memory and significant perf hit.
The reproduction sequence seems to be:
- join world
- move with hyper speed, ~30s
- wait for Queue size to go to 0
- move with hyper speed, ~30s
- wait for Queue size to go to 0

Outcome: ~50fps on RTX 3080 and ~1GB of fragmentation reported by F3 debug info

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fb5aefff-5b01-4093-93ce-494f993b636c)

## Normal case

Just loading into a world and waiting for Queue size to go to 0 works fine.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2cd8ac3d-07b3-4629-8969-dda552cf0ab8)

With vsync disabled we can easily reach ~200fps

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8e494b88-2ff1-4817-a77e-2417cf6301ca)

Is that limit testing or just being silly?

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The fragmentation is not an issue, any allocator (that cannot free pages) will have high fragmentation if you suddenly free a large portion of the memory. 

And as for the performance issues you are getting, this is just a result of freeing so many chunks at once.
It is probably possible (would need to measure that) to improve the performance of the GPU allocator (currently it iterates through all chunks of free memory on each allocation and free)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
There is a [blog](https://www.gingerbill.org/article/2021/11/30/memory-allocation-strategies-005/) with description of use of red black tree to implement a free list allocator.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Yeah, any tree will probably be good enough.
Also note that all allocation data structures must exist outside the memory itself, since we don't want to read stuff back from the GPU buffer.

// ISSUE TITLE: Cuboid selection wand
// ISSUE REPORT:
#1141 introduces commands for editing worlds by selecting, copying and pasting small fragments of them. Currently only way to create selection is for player to align themselves with build they want to select and use `/pos1` and `/pos2` commands to set opposite corners of selection. This is enough just to introduce world editing as a feature, but not a convenient long term solution. 

Therefore a cubic selection wand should be introduced. Wand should be an item only obtainable through creative inventory. When held it should visualize current selection, but without it in hand current selection should not be visible (that is not the case now, selection is visible always as long as `pos1` and `pos2` are set). When holding wand, a block, player is aiming at, should be outlined. For air blocks wand should be able to select last air block in range, for solid blocks it should be first solid block in range, solid blocks should always be preferred over air blocks. Right clicking with wand should start selection at block where wand was currently aiming at and set `pos1`. Until right clicked again wand should update `pos2` every time player changes the block they aim at. Selection should be visualized as wireframe in real time. This should allow starting selection in one place and dragging it until right click is used to complete selection. (Alternatively it could be left click to start, right click to end if that is easier to implement)

Effect should be similar to what MCEdit offered.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/421c8b11-2b69-4f62-8073-4471cbaf00ac)

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c0eecb4b-1c4d-4fce-b796-6526d0e793dd)

It is possible that in the future different selection wand types will be introduced [similarly to what WorldEdit has](https://minecraft-worldedit.fandom.com/wiki/Worldedit_Commands), but let's start with something simple.

// ISSUE TITLE: Nudging the Player
// ISSUE REPORT:
Given that this is a open-world sandbox game, we can't force the player to do anything. If we try to, then we take away control from the player, which ends up making the game less fun.

Instead, we want to encourage the player into doing certain things, such as building bases, creating mineshafts, long transportation systems, and etc.

-----

### Encouraging Long Transportation Systems:
Terraria and Minecraft both feature minecart transportation, but they are both outshined by other methods of transportation, such as teleportation and flying. Admittedly, Terraria handles this better than Minecraft, but rails still become *mostly* pointless later on in the game.

In Cubyz, we want to avoid teleportation at all costs. If teleportation is to be used, it should be immovable and perhaps one-way, so the player doesn't rely on it. Instead, we want to encourage the player to create long railways.

Related: #811 

### Encouraging Building:
In Minecraft, there's not much of a reason to make a permanent home, or even a home at all. Sure, you can make a roof over your head, but a field of torches achieves this to the same effect.

> Here's an example of what we can do to encourage building bases: having workstations break into lesser parts rather than themselves. This would encourage the player to think carefully about where they place their stations, and reward more permanent residences, while not completely destroying the nomadic playstyle.

We decided that a better way of doing this would be to make workstations unstackable/take longer to break, so they're less convenient to carry around.

Related: #983

And regardless, eventually the player will gather so much stuff that they will want somewhere to put it, so they'll start a base to store their items.

// ISSUE TITLE: My Wildlife Ideas
// ISSUE REPORT:
Based on a discussion Carrie, Archbirdplus, and I had in the Discord: the food chain in Cubyz is flipped on its head. The idea of this came from the concept of flying fish.

I don't think this has really ever been done before; it could provide a unique environment to the game, and it wouldn't be entirely alien to the player since these animals exist in real life.

--------------------
### Birds → Fish
Fish are now airborne creatures, flying through the sky on sunny days, occasionally taking dips in the water to drink. Their prey are birds, who now swim in the sea. Whales are now considered a "Fish of Prey."

### Cats → Worms
Fish eat worms, but not in Cubyz! In Cubyz, worms eat fish!

### Dogs → Spiders
Dogs turn into cute spiders. They live in packs!

### Humans → Snales
Of course your default player character, the snale, is the replacement for humans. So if the humans aren't here, where are they exactly?

### Bugs → Mammals
If mammals are now bugs, then the other way around is also true. Small bugs can be based on cats, dogs, etc. Now, imagine what a "big bug" would turn into. Probably a terrifying beast that snarls and hisses.

// ISSUE TITLE: Boost stats when sleeping well
// ISSUE REPORT:
Sleeping well could boost stats such as movement speed and stamina (#257) regeneration
This encourages players to sleep before boss fights and adventuring.
Additionally one could take into account the surroundings. For example when the players is inside a house they sleep better, getting a bigger stat boost. Additional boosts could be windows, sky visibility, decoration blocks, a ventilated fire place and whether the player sleeps with their armor on.
This would encourage the player to build shelters all around the world.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Probably not. However, it might absolutely be possible that certain gases (e.g. radioactive fog) might damage you. Whether these gases get simulated is a different question.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think we should keep the core mechanics simple and not add too many random chores the player has to do to boost their stats before going into a fight.

// ISSUE TITLE: Stamina
// ISSUE REPORT:
Stamina is replenished naturally, consuming hunger.
Not all actions consume stamina. For example we don't want to slow down exploration, so sprinting does not consume stamina, but you can only sprint if your stamina is above a certain level.
When using a tool or weapon stamina decreases depending on its weight.

This has the potential to add a lot of depth to fighting and crafting. For example a low-range dagger might mean that you get hit more often, but consumes less stamina than a long range spear.
But as you get more stamina conserving in your combat style, or against certain bosses, the spear might be the better option.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
Stamina has multiple aspects to it. Of course, there is the global "running out of air" state. But each muscle has its own temporary store of oxygen/energy which it can use for some amount of time. That means that each muscle could be tracked independently. There's also a several categories of muscle fibers, which each use an/aerobic respiration and are used at different times.
This adds even more complexity to the game. Anaerobic respiration, for example, probably depletes hunger more quickly due to being less efficient. This means that running and sprinting could have slightly different stamina trackers. It also means that in one Cubyz day, it could make sense to split up work by muscle group. (Aside: sleep quality aiding stamina recovery could encourage players to build temporary shelters at night while exploring to allow for more optimal performance the next day)
On the topic of exercise, even more complexity could be made from simulating muscle growth. Cubyz could even consider the quality of sleep, or the amount of carbohydrates, fats, and amino acids that the player consumes throughout the day, compare that with the types of exercise each muscle had performed...

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Let's not overcomplicate things. A single "out of air"-stamina value is a good enough approximation that is easy to communicate to the player.

About sleep: it is an interesting idea to give the player a stat boost for sleeping well (though that is a different topic #258).
About exercise: That would basically be a skilling/levelling system. I'm not yet sure if I want something like that. Skilling often makes players grind more, and I don't know if I want that.
About nutrients: While it would be great to encourage eating different foods, it would put some pressure on biome design, since all nutrients should be accessible in most surface biomes. So I would like to leave it open for now.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I don't think this is a strong enough use case to justify adding another bar to the UI.
Furthermore I think we might be able to achieve similar mechanical depth with the energy bar concept.

// ISSUE TITLE: Enemy Philosophy
// ISSUE REPORT:
I want players to feel safe in their home, but also I want them to be able to go and search for danger when they want it.

1. The surface should be safe, even at night to allow the player to build stuff without getting annoyed by enemies attacking.
2. Once an area or structure has been conquered by the player, it should be safe forever. However if the final boss wasn't defeated or there is still some creatures left when the players leaves, enemies may slowly respawn in the structure.
3. Enemies can be exclusively found in structures, or summoned by the player in terraria-style invasions.
4. Enemies that drop rare or useful stuff should never respawn to encourage exploration over building mobfarms.
5. Structures should be frequent around the world, multiple large structures should be visible at default render distance.
6. Some enemies should be able to break and place blocks to reach the player. This could lead to some fun tower-defense invasions.
7. Enemies should get harder when the player progresses.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
You can implement a rogue-like difficulty curve, where enemies get exponentially harder when digging deeper. This keeps the endgame from stagnating.

[USER COMMENT]
Surface enemies could be passive unless provoked, so the player has to actively choose to fight them. 
Or they could become hostile based on the player's actions, like a forest spirit becoming angry that the player is chopping trees, or a fish getting mad that the player is taking water from a pond.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Since chunks are only saved when modified we can exploit this system by spawning entities during generation to automatically get a lot of what we want:

- regions claimed by players by building will not spawn entities again, as they are saved and not generated again. 
- regions unclaimed by players will remain potentially hostile, as after chunks are unloaded and loaded again entities would also be spawned again.

This makes it possible to grind valuable enemies, so those entities which drop them should be excluded from that system or they could trigger chunk saving after they are killed (eg. spawn a loot chest instead of dropping stuff)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Preferably we don't want mob grinding to be possible. We could save entities and chunks separately, so an unclaimed chunk can still be unloaded and reloaded, but entities will not respawn.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We could also just keep track of the number of entities, without storing them individually.
Who cares if it turns a wolf into a goat when you return after a few hours?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
most of this has been added to https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/blob/master/GAME_DESIGN_PRINCIPLES.md

// ISSUE TITLE: tool sharpening idea
// ISSUE REPORT:
I assume tools have edges, (example: normal pickaxe - 3 sides: front, right and left), but in case they don't, this operation will apply changes to a tool as a whole.
In workbench, or smithing station:
Add sharpening modifier and a tool to sharpen tool.
Sharpening deacreases tool durability, encreases damage dealt to blocks and entities.
Each time sharpened tool is used, it looses sharpness and gets more damage from being used than usual, until sharpness value is 0.
Sharpening harder materials will lower additional damage and sharpness loss from being used.
Sharpening modifiers are: sand, rock (cobblestone), other sharp tools, sandpaper, blunt tools (hammer, sledgehammer etc).
Additional sharpnening modifiers are (smithing stantion only): heat: coal, charcoal, lava bucket, wood, wooden tool.
Blunt tools can only be used on smithing station.
When sharpening with blunt tool, tool being sharpened gets quadruple damage and half less sharpness value added if is brittle or / and is soluble  or / and made of wood.
When sharpening with blunt tool, tool being sharpened gets half less damage if is hard or / and is made of metal.
When sharpening with sandpaper, tool being sharpened gets 1/4 damage and 1/4 sharpness value added if is hard.
When sharpening with sandpaper, tool being sharpened gets 1.25 damage if is brittle or / and is soluble.
When sharpening with rock (cobblestone), tool being sharpened gets half more damage, if is made of wood or / and is brittle.
When sharpening with sand, tool being sharpened gets 1.25 damage and 0.75 of sharpness value added.
When sharpening with sharp tool, tool being sharpened gets triple damage and double sharpness value added, if is made of wood.
When sharpening with sharp tool, tool being sharpened gets 0.75 damage and 0.25 sharpness value added, if tool is not made of wood.
When sharpening in a smithing station with blunt tool and additional heat sharpening modifier, tool being sharpened gets 0.1 damage and 0.75 sharpness value added, if tool is made of metal or stone (stone will need higher quantity of heat additional modifiers).
Note that tools used as sharpness modifiers and sandpaper loose durability when used.
Other sharpness modifiers have no durability are gone upon usage (one sharpness modifier per use).
Possible look of smithing station UI (concept schematic):
![image](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/assets/82651369/db9ac9fd-d299-4e8b-956d-8c281c896418)
Purple marked slot is for sharpness modifier.
Green marked slot is for tool being sharpened.
Red marked slot is for heat additional modifier.
Yellow marked button is a rundown list to choose side being sharpened (in case edges of a tool are a thing in game' code)

Possible look of sharpening in workbench (shapeless craft):
![image](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/assets/82651369/afc3fd0e-6a4e-44fb-a042-dc1d7356eb5f)

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I generally think sharpening could be an interesting mechanic to have, but I think your approach is too complicated.

How about the following instead:
- You can use different stone types to make a grindstone, which can be placed in the world.
- No UI, just right click the grindstone with the tool. (Also maybe there should an indicator next to the crosshair)
- Whenever a tool is sharpened it directly looses durability. This makes it more obvious to the player what the trade-of from sharpening is.
- The durability loss is proportional to the max durability of the tool.
- When using a tool, sharpness is lost. Sharpness loss is proportional to the durability of the tip material.
- Sharpness value after sharpening depends on the grindstone type.
- Sharpening applies to all edges of the tool, that's just easier to implement.
- Sharpness affects damage and efficiency calculations, and increases them by a small amount.

[USER COMMENT]
I think from a systems point of view a generic solution for building buffs/debuffs for items would definitely be good. Then the effect of the sharpness buff would depend on the context of how it is granted. So the grindstone would determine that based on the type and material of the weapon/item being used on it. But you could also have good loot with particularly long or effective sharpness buffs already attached via RNG, etc.

[USER COMMENT]
Grindstone seems to be the better way to implement sharpening, tho I think materials of sharpened tool should impact results greatly as well. Also we could have pocket way of sharpening, like sandpaper, to sharpen tool right from the inventory, but tradeoff for not using grindstone in that case could be something like less sharpness value and limit of maximum sharpness value that could be achieved that way.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Also we could have pocket way of sharpening

Yeah, a grindstone, that you can carry around and place anywhere :P
I think no one would use the weaker sandpaper, when they can just take the real thing.

> tho I think materials of sharpened tool should impact results greatly as well.

I agree, but I fear that this might make the system to complicated. Mod creators and players alike need to keep in mind more, and more interactions between these different systems.
That's why I proposed to make it depend only on the durability.

[USER COMMENT]
I agree that it shouldn't be too complicated. With this sort of game less is often more. 
Also you might not want this to be something that is so good and easy that players feel compelled to always do it all the time, because that just means it becomes a tedious, rote action that just gets in the way. So making it balanced could mean that players only do it when they're in a hurry and want to mine or cut trees quickly, but at the expense of a significant hit to durability.

[USER COMMENT]
> Mod creators and players alike need to keep in mind more, and more interactions between these different systems.
> That's why I proposed to make it depend only on the durability.

I want to find a way to keep things simple while getting complex results. Having durability as the only thing that impacts sharpening would be a good way to keep system simple, but there would be no complex results.

What about using thing, that would describe specific custom behavior of something, if needed? lets say, attributes, that can be assigned to item or entity. Those attributes have type and store one or few values. When grindstone encounters tool with "tool" attribute type, it uses value X, value Y and value Z to alter resulting sharpness, durability and max durability changes of tool, otherwise - everything happens as usual, getting sharpness while using only durability and max durability values.  If attribute is present but no value is set within - it be ignored. Lets assume value X represents sharpness multiplyer, value Y represents durability multiplyer and value Z represents max durability multiplyer. Those values can be assigned to a tool requiring specific grindstone behavior and are not general, assigned to specific item according to whatever formula defines those, when crafted.
 After that, instead of using just value of sharpness, durability or max durability in whatever formula this could use, it uses those values multiplied by relevant multiplyer values, stored in attribute. 

 As a result, if someone wants to add their own brand new pickaxe of netherium diamond ingots via altering game code, but doesn't want to deal with custom properties and assigning their ingots as new material, setting properties etc, they just dont have to, and their tool will be just as any other, with their custom or not durability, efficiency and damage, having no attribute that would alter sharpening of tool of theirs when using grindstone.

// ISSUE TITLE: itemdrops sometimes freeze the GPU
// ISSUE REPORT:
This has happened since day one, and it seems I haven't made an issue yet.

This issue seems to happen randomly, mostly when blocks are being broken/were being broken recently. Sometimes I think it may also be angle dependent, but I'm not sure. It happens so rarely that it is a pain to reproduce.

What I do know is that the game freezes for 10 seconds and I get a log from my graphics driver:
```
amdgpu: amdgpu_cs_query_fence_status failed.
```

From what I have experienced in the past this means that either there is an infinite loop on the GPU or an illegal memory access. Since the raymarching for item models is limited by a counter, it must be the latter.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This was a fun one.

I'm not 100% sure on all the details (GPU debugging is hard), but from what I could gather this seems to be caused by the following:
OpenGL always runs the fragment shader on 2×2 quads, even if those fall outside the given triangle.
This produces out of range values, which (at very steep angles) can cause it to sample the buffer out of range.
For normal textures this is fine, since the texture sampler automatically guards against this, but here I'm reading the values from an SSBO.

## Enemy 1: Mossman
Best comparison to existing enemy: Fire Imp, Tim, Goblin Mage

### Spawning
- Enemy spawns underground

### Idle behavior
Choose one from random while idle:
- Look around
- Burrow, then unburrow after a short time
- Make an idle sound

### Attacking behavior
When the enemy has a target:
1. Look at the target
2. Spit moss ball at target
3. Burrow
4. Unburrow after a short time
5. Repeat
---------
## Enemy 2: Splint
Best comparison to existing enemy: Zombie, Flinx

### Spawning
- Enemy spawns in forests

### Idle behavior
Choose one from random while idle:
- Look around
- Walk around
- Make an idle sound

### Attacking behavior
When the enemy has a target:
- Follow the target, attempt to occupy the same space to deal contact damage.
---------
## Enemy 3: Grump
Best comparison to existing enemy: Corrupter, Hornet

### Spawning
- Enemy spawns 200 blocks or higher above sea level 

### Idle behavior
Idle:
- Fly around

When despawning:
- Fly into the sky

### Attacking behavior
When the enemy has a target:
- Follow the target.
- Occasionally spit clouds at the target.

// ISSUE TITLE: Smaller-sized blocks
// ISSUE REPORT:
To allow more details and artistic construction, we could make the block size smaller (to 1/2 meters or 2/3 meters). However this would require the following changes:

- [ ] Multiblock structures
- [ ] Ability to place multiple blocks at a time
- [ ] Ability to remove multiple blocks at a time

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
Some thoughts:

- If you divided the block into 64 (4x4x4), you could use a single 64-bit int as a bitfield to determine if a given chunk is present or not.
- I would encourage pushing the mesh generation into a compute shader, to reduce client-side computation and time required to push the much more detailed meshes to the GPU
- I'd also err on the side of simplicity... for example, cull all faces that aren't visible from outside the entire block space, even if it would otherwise be a full face (ie. hole in the middle)
- Multiple textures could be achieved with an integer per block surface

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think we're more likely to opt for 1/2 m (in which case blocks are divided by 8), also optimising could be done by making it such that if there is the same block filling a large space, it can be compressed (and in the GPU mesh data too) into a larger cube.

About your 3rd point, it's already what we do with invisible blocks.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The ²⁄₃ sized blocks have been implemented in ea478e0bd25754c3cae551d2926d13fd3a418091.
The ability to place/remove multiple blocks at a time isn't necessary as ²⁄₃ sized blocks aren't significantly smaller.

The launcher takes care about all of this and stores the libraries and assets in `.cubyz` in your home directory.

// ISSUE TITLE: Ship the source code and zig compiler
// ISSUE REPORT:
This should be possible with the Zig compiler, but I'm not 100% sure if it would work on windows (if a visual studio installation is needed, that would be problematic).

But this would be advantageous in so many ways:
- Compiling on the user's machine would allow compiling for their CPU, so people with avx or avx512 would get a performance boost.
- Exposing the source code would allow for a community maintained modding environment, like in minecraft (which also exposes it's source code due to java decompilation)
- This would make it easier for me to debug the game on other peoples computers, because I don't need to compile the game for them 100 times. Additionally people could run the game in debug mode directly without requiring a separate executable.
- This would make it easier to publish a release version, since I wouldn't need to compile the game for many different operating systems.
- People can download development versions to test new features without needing to wait.
- Although that probably won't affect many people, this would make it easier for people who never programmed before to get into programming, because they wouldn't need to install tons of stuff (that's a step I failed on when I started to learn about programming)

It does come at some cost though:
- First game launch will take more time due compiling the game
- Higher disk space usage due to the compiler and its caching

### Requirements
- [x] #104
- [x] #117

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This is basically done. I only need to get certainty on whether this actually works well in practice without needing to install extra packages or something like that.

[USER COMMENT]
Downloading Zig12 was the least of my problems building Cubyz, probably thanks to `run.sh` doing that for me. It probably saves bandwidth from having me google why my homebrew Zig11 fails with a useless error.

Building Cubyz after applying commit 9978fd2 also helped me isolate the Zig crash. I suppose for normal use-cases, Cubyz-libs would work just fine. But it does make prototyping or using an unofficial Cubyz-libs more complicated.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> I suppose for normal use-cases, Cubyz-libs would work just fine. But it does make prototyping or using an unofficial Cubyz-libs more complicated.

That is true. But I found that it isn't too hard, since you can just change the entry in the build.zig.zon to import a local file.
It still requires a few extra steps sadly, like packaging the library into a tar.gz every time and clearing the `.seed` field, otherwise zig just keeps using the outdated version from the cache.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I don't think this makes sense for a small-scale multiplayer game like Cubyz.
A centralized server is needed in order to store a consistent game state. Otherwise it would be possible to get conflicting game states and I don't want to deal with that.
Additionally, a decentralized system would require a lot of additional work and storage, since everyone would be storing copies of the information.

By the way, Cubyz is already in some ways peer to peer:
There is no authentication server.
The game server can be behind a firewall, due to using holepunching.

// ISSUE TITLE: Noob compile UX: terminal window closes
// ISSUE REPORT:
As many people (as per the official instructions) open the `run_release` script from the OS file-opening gui, the script closes upon completion, deleting the error messages. Tech support then has the user run the script in an existing shell to access error logs.

Key issue: `run_release` exits and destroys error logs when executed from a gui.

Option 1:
Call `read` or equivalent if the Cubyz executable is not generated or zig exits with non-zero exit code. This prevents the window from closing.
Side effect: forces power users to press enter unnecessarily.

Option 2:
Detect if the shell will close if the script exits, then proceed to Option 1. This may not be possible.

Option 3:
Create another script, like `run_release_gui`, for running from gui, then proceed to Option 1. See also points 1. and 2. of #241 about renaming the scripts.
Side effect: extra complexity.

Option 4:
Pipe/redirect/`tee` all logs from the script into `logs/log123.txt`, for example.
Side effect: terminal colors do not always play nice with pipes.

I have no doubt there is a better solution, though.

This issue stacks with #241, which brings up script renaming and extra logging.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Regarding option 1: We could detect if the script was run by double clicking or not (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/9419875/determining-if-batch-script-has-been-started-executed-from-the-command-line-cmd/9422268#9422268) and only then call read. Not sure how reliable this works for other terminals, like for example the VSCode builtin terminal.

[USER COMMENT]
Linux may be able to abuse `ps` and `$SHLVL` if double-clicking behaves differently. MacOS, however, just opens a terminal window with `/path/to/script ; exit`, which should not be detectable at all.

// ISSUE TITLE: Distributable linking
// ISSUE REPORT:
Cubyz is actually really poorly set up for building to distribute on mac. I suspect the same might be true for linux as well.

Currently, Cubyzig has the following rpaths:
```
     path /Users/USER/.cache/zig/p/12204ba799cc74baeec8284b8a22cd0b597b58d5fa32c6eac999635fdc1834c950fc/lib (offset 12)
     path /usr/local/GL/lib (offset 12)
     path /Users/USER/Cubyz/Library (offset 12)
     path /opt/homebrew/Cellar/libx11/1.8.9/lib (offset 12)
     path /Users/USER/.cache/zig/p/12204ba799cc74baeec8284b8a22cd0b597b58d5fa32c6eac999635fdc1834c950fc/lib (offset 12)
```

There are a number of problems:
- cubyz_deps is listed twice (it shouldn't be at all, since it's a static library).
- The Library path is nonsensical because Zig evaluated it at build-time instead of runtime.
- Cubyz should link against XQuartz X11, not homebrew.
- X11 doesn't need an RPATH.

So, at present, every single rpath is just plain invalid for distribution.

Ideally, the rpaths would just be the following:
```
    path @executable_path/../Library (if app bundle)
    path /usr/local/GL/lib (if local build)
```

But I'm not sure if this is even possible within Zig build! Zig seems to discourage linking libraries by their paths alone (demanding an entire compile step or just a query to pkgconfig), and forcefully includes some (incorrect) rpath.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
This might be Zig's Build library rebelling against us doing something that violates its standards of beauty. It's standards are too idealistic though.

It may be possible to link these things manually by abusing commandline Zig, which might have fine-grained rpath support - https://github.com/ziglang/zig/issues/5827. The commandline linker might not be exposed though.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Is it really a problem to have unused stuff in the rpaths?

Also I wonder if there is a ways to manual add `@executable_path/../Library`.
Maybe `exe.root_module.addRPathSpecial("@executable_path/../Library")` could work?

[USER COMMENT]
That looks like it!

It's not necessarily wrong, but it's not necessary. It also leaks my username from my builds. And also it is wrong. So I might as well fix as many of these together as I can.

[USER COMMENT]
Thanks for the `root_module.addRPathSpecial`, I never would have found it (`root_module` doesn't even appear in Zig docs).
I also found us a `exe.addObjectFile` for cubyz_deps. Since it turns out X11 doesn't need to be linked either, I removed it in my PR.

// ISSUE TITLE: [Ore] Titanium
// ISSUE REPORT:
This issue is a proposal to add titanium to the game.

## Physical properties:
- hardness in Mohs scale: 6.0
- density (at 20° C): 4.5 g/cm3

## In game properties:
- titanium is a **base metal** intended as base for tool heads to be supplemented with modifiers
- titanium provides less durability than iron
- titanium is lighter than iron, so has lower swing times
- titanium has higher damage than iron as it has higher hardness
- titanium spawns deeper than iron

- None

## Spawn rules
- `-? < Z < -?`
- Spawns in veins
