Well, this is very important for the player-player interactions dynamic. If you can't gain anything from killing someone, there is no incentive to run around killing unless they are trespassing or annoying in some other way.

As for protecting ppl from things like spawn kills, I would advocate for mechanism to spawn player further and further from their spawn point if they die too soon after respawning. For example first time you respawn exactly at your spawn point, but if you die within 5 seconds you get respawned randomly in 10 block radius, then if you die within 10s you will respawn in 50 block radius etc. This would also apply to player-set spawn points like beds, if we want to have mechanic like this.

// ISSUE TITLE: Code style guidelines
// ISSUE REPORT:
This issue is intended to aggregate discussions about code style practices in Cubyz codebase.

As of creation time of this issue, contribution guidelines state that:

> 
> # Write correct and readable code
> 
> ## Explicitly handle all errors (except from those that can't happen)
> 
> Error handling usually means logging the error and continuing with a sensible default. For example if you can't read a file, then the best solution would be to write a message to `std.log.err` that states the file path and the error string. It is also fine to bubble up the error with `try` a few levels before handling it.
> 
> Not all errors can happen. Specifically in Cubyz the `error.OutOfMemory` cannot happen with the standard allocators (`main.globalAllocator` and `main.stackAllocator`). In this case `catch unreachable` is the right way to handle the error.
> 
> ## Choose the right allocator for the job
> 
> Cubyz has two main allocators.
> - The `main.stackAllocator` is a thread-local allocator that is optimized for local allocations. Use for anything that you free at the end of the scope. An example use-case would be a local `List`.
> - The `main.globalAllocator` is intended to be used for general purpose use cases that don't need to or can't be particularly optimized.
> 
> Sometimes it might also make sense to use an arena allocator `utils.NeverFailingArenaAllocator`, or a `MemoryPool`. But generally these should only be used where it makes sense.
> 
> Also it is important to note that Cubyz uses a different allocator interface `utils.NeverFailingAllocator` which cannot return `error.OutOfMemory`. Along with it come some data structures like `main.List` and more in `utils` which should be preferred over the standard library data structures because they simplify the code.
> 
> ## Free all resources
> 
> Everything you allocate, needs to be freed.
> Everything you init needs to be deinited.
> Everything you lock needs to be unlocked.
> This needs to be true even for error paths such as introduced by `try`.
> Usually you should be fine by using `defer` and `errdefer`. There is also leak-checking in debug mode, so make sure you test your feature in debug mode to check for leaks.
> 
> ## Follow the style guide
> 
> Most of the syntax is handled by a modified version of zig fmt and checked by the CI (see the formatting section above).
> 
> Other than there is not much more to it:
> - Use camelCase for variables, constants and functions, CapitalCamelCase for types
> - There is no line limit (I hate seeing code that gets wrapped over by 1 word, because of an arbitrary line limit), but of course try to be reasonable. If you need 200 characters, then you should probably consider splitting it or adding some well-named helper variables.
> - Don't write comments, unless there is something really non-obvious going on.
> - But in either case it's better to write readable code with descriptive names, instead of writing long comments, since comments will naturally degrade over time as the surrounding code changes.
> 

## Style practices to be discussed:

- [x] **Order of definitions: top-down vs bottom-up.** Since zig does not carry legacy limitation from C language that definition must precede use, more natural, top-down declaration order is available. In top-down approach high level, more abstract (big picture) functions (and possibly other code objects) appear before lower level implementation details. Implementation details appear after they are used for the first time. Ordering usually do not apply to variables and fields, which are grouped separately. [more](https://github.com/Geeksltd/Programming.Tips/blob/master/docs/methods/stepdown-rule.md) [more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bottom-up_and_top-down_design#:~:text=A%20top%2Ddown%20approach%20starts,subsystems%20of%20the%20emergent%20system.)
- [x] **File as struct vs file as namespace for structs.** Zig documentation states that `Zig source files are implicitly structs, with a name equal to the file's basename with the extension truncated. @import returns the struct type corresponding to the file.`. Therefore unless creating generic data structure, creation of simple struct can be tackled by placing fields directly in file. This has a side effect of promoting one struct per file and hence separation of functionality into dedicated files and removes redundant namespaces. On the other hand, this creates inconsistency with generic structs which have to be created as functions. Currently we can find all possible mixtures of structs, generics, enums and unions in codebase, hence making codebase inconsistent and making it hard to judge which notation to chose, since in many cases they are equivalent.
- [x] **@import**. Zig provides `@import` builtin function which can be used to add files to build and access field and declarations inside them if declared public. Currently we are multiple different practices regarding importing elements from different modules. For example, importing `src/blocks.zig` is done sometimes by `const blocks = @import("blocks.zig");` while in other cases `const main = @import("root");const blocks = main.blocks;`. There are technical reasons to use second form, but first once could be consistently replaced with second one in most cases.
- [x] **Import symbol aliasing.** When using structs / enums / unions defined in different files, they are sometimes aliased, sometimes not. Although strict rules in that matter could harm readability, on the other hand not aliasing objects when they are used many times creates unnecessary code and additional tension in case of rename. Additionally, aliases sometimes are not used consistently, for example in `src/items.zig`, struct `Block` is aliased while imported `const blocks = @import("blocks.zig"); const Block = blocks.Block;` but then `main.blocks.Block` appears in code anyway. A rule could be to always alias if accessing object through two or more namespaces (`main.blocks.`) or when using object at least 3 times. This will also incentivize using more descriptive names to avoid collisions with module names.
- [x] **Location of fields when using file as struct.** Fields are always public in zig, hence they are always part of struct interface and they are both definition and first use, being their own getters and setter. As a result they should be defined first in the struct.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
My problem right now is that I wanted to work on extracting functions like `registerTool`, `registerBlock` etc from `assets.zig`, as mentioned in one of #1125 , but then I noticed that recipes are in items file, so I wanted to extract them, but I am not sure if I should follow Inventory design (file as struct) or blocks/items design.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Order of definitions: top-down vs bottom-up

Whether enforced by the language or not I prefer seeing the declaration of a thing before it's first usage.
This is enforced by the language for local variables, this is common practice for imports and struct field. So in my opinion it only makes sense to use the same pattern for helper functions too.

But in the end I think what matters most is that related functions are close to each other in the code.

> File as struct vs file as namespace for structs.

 Both have valid use cases. But most of the time I think declaring structs separately is the way to go, because it is better when having multiple structs in one file.
Promoting one struct per file is not something I want. I had that in Java and it made it just more difficult to navigate the code.

>  then I noticed that recipes are in items file, so I wanted to extract them

They are fine where they are in my opinion. Just like tools they conceptually belong to items.

> import

Importing root is the preferred way, but it sometimes breaks ZLS, and until that's more stable I'd like to leave it for now.

> Import symbol aliasing

Another tooling issue, until ZLS has a way to automatically create and destroy aliases it does not make sense to enforce it.

> Location of fields when using file as struct

Fields should always be at the beginning of the structs, unless there is something that conceptually should be declared up top (e.g. import statement or sometimes other declarations)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> They are fine where they are in my opinion. Just like tools they conceptually belong to items.

In Cubyz, files are not documented with top level comment. As a result, you don't have a clear statement describing what given file actually contains. You are saying that they conceptually belong to items, so please, make an thought experiment and try to formulate said top level comment, something along the lines of `items.zig file contains...`.

---

Now when I try to do that, what I get is something along the lines of `items.zig file contains logic of items, modifiers, tools and recipes`. Each of those 4 interfaces is tied to each other, as they interact with each other, but at the same time each of those interfaces is also accessed separately form outside of `items.zig` file. (tool interface is accessed via `registerTool` function, recipe interface is accessed via `registerRecipes` etc.). As a result you have 4 interfaces which are technically separate things but they are in one namespace. As a result you have name collisions - `registerRecipes` and `registerTools` could be named just `register` if they were in separate files, same way blocks are separate from items. It would create this really desired repetability and consistency in design where by knowing high level design of one part you can easily understand design of other parts. I agree that items, tools, recipes and modifiers should be grouped, but in my opinion correct grouping would be to have them in separate files in one directory. 

What we could do is to group them under `assets` folder with other assets:

- `src/items.zig` -> `src/assets/items.zig`
- `src/items.zig` -> `src/assets/recipes.zig`
- `src/items.zig` -> `src/assets/tools.zig`
- `src/blocks.zig` -> `src/assets/blocks.zig`
- `src/models.zig` -> `src/assets/models.zig`
- `src/assets.zig` -> `src/assets/loader.zig`

This means that each file has clearly stated purpose. Removes name collisions, as `MaterialProperty` can become just `Property`, `getToolTypeByID` can become `getByID`, `toolTypeIterator` can become just `iterator` etc. thanks to namespaces, it is now responsibility of user to properly alias those names or keep them in namespaces.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> In Cubyz, files are not documented with top level comment. As a result, you don't have a clear statement describing what given file actually contains.

There is a clear description: The file name.
I don't think top-level comments are a useful concept: They constantly need to be maintained (but their correctness cannot be enforced → more review work), they often contain redundant information, and they are are mostly ignored (at least I mostly ignore them when I work on other people's projects).

I agree with you that it would be nice if every file only had one nice and simple responsibility (like e.g. commands or materials), but in my opinion that's not always useful. The reason why it works so well for commands is, because each command has a clear purpose, and commands don't interact with each other, so if I implement a new feature for a command I really only need to touch that one file.

On the other hand Tools, BaseItems and Materials do interact quite a lot. And it's nice to work on them without having to switch between files all the time. And VSCode's minimap makes navigating even large files really nice in my opinion. Much nicer than using file tabs.
Furthermore I don't even see where you would put Materials in your proposal.

> registerRecipes and registerTools could be named just register if they were in separate files, same way blocks are separate from items.
> Removes name collisions, as MaterialProperty can become just Property

The same thing could be done with regular namespaces, it could easily be `Material.Property`.

> `toolTypeIterator` can become just `iterator`

I think that most of the proposed name changes are neutral (it neither makes typing nor reading easier/harder), this one would even make things worse in my opinion, unless you want a separate file just for tool types.

> What we could do is to group them under assets folder with other assets:

I don't think "assets" is a good way to group things. All of these things do completely different things.
An asset to me is just "something that gets loaded from a file". That really is not a useful way to order things.
Many things in the game are assets, but should we put half of the code into the `src/assets` folder?
What if we decide that loading tools from assets (a recent addition by the way) was a bad idea? Where do we put them instead?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> There is a clear description: The file name. I don't think top-level comments are a useful concept: They constantly need to be maintained (but their correctness cannot be enforced → more review work), they often contain redundant information, and they are are mostly ignored (at least I mostly ignore them when I work on other people's projects).

Single word is not a description, it is a symbol - its open for interpretation.
The more words you use, the less room for interpretation there is. This is exactly why comments are either useless or easily get outdated - they either strictly describe some aspects of code and when this code changes they get outdated, or they are vague and provide no value. Technically file name is a comment too, just a very generic one.

I agree that comments are a burden and there is no room for them when project is in pre-first release stage. However, I do not agree that one should not pay attention to them, especially when they are done correctly, see [numpy.fft](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/main/numpy/fft/__init__.py) or [cPython json module](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/main/Lib/json/__init__.py). They exist to provide documentation of features in the module, a guide. This is very useful if module is stable, since it makes it so documentation is actually close to code compared to having separate markdown files.

> I agree with you that it would be nice if every file only had one nice and simple responsibility (like e.g. commands or materials), but in my opinion that's not always useful. The reason why it works so well for commands is, because each command has a clear purpose, and commands don't interact with each other, so if I implement a new feature for a command I really only need to touch that one file.

Whether you will have to touch multiple files or not depends heavily on what you are doing. If you decide to change something in how commands work in general, by adding new field or whatever, you will have to touch command files too. It's only a matter of how hard requirements change. It is not possible to predict how requirements will change, so don't try too. Do what's best now, as often a best way to prepare for future complexity is to keep what here today as simple as possible. 

Why don't we just place everything in one file (except of commands since they are so independent)? Maybe, just maybe because setting boundaries keeps complexity of dependencies manageable. If we keep stuff together and have direct access to everything, then everything is a dependency for us. Complexity grows exponentially.

> On the other hand Tools, BaseItems and Materials do interact quite a lot. And it's nice to work on them without having to switch between files all the time. And VSCode's minimap makes navigating even large files really nice in my opinion. Much nicer than using file tabs. 

I don't see a particular difference (between switching and scrolling). The problem is that the second you have to think where to look, do actual searching, you are wasting time. I'm a bit digressing here, but hear me out: If we follow a rule that implementation details are always below, then we can always scroll below current code an **often** we will find what we are looking for. This is a branch prediction hit, it is good for efficiency. The better our branch predictions are the, the less often we have go back to actually searching for things.

Regardless, if we are mentioning VSC features, you can also use `Ctrl+P` and write `<file-name>` to open a file. On top of that you can also do `Ctrl+P` and write `@<symbol-name>` to navigate to symbol. There is also `Ctrl+P` -> `:<line-number` to navigate to line and `Ctrl+P` -> `#<symbol-name>` too look for a symbol in all files, but the last one doesn't unfortunately work with zig :C  

You neither need scroll nor tabs to navigate codebase.

> Furthermore I don't even see where you would put Materials in your proposal.

This was not a detailed refactor proposal, more of a expression of a general idea. What I noticed is that you are placing **a lot** of functionality in one file without setting boundaries. I have seen that before at corporate scale, trust me, you would not want to navigate a 30+k lines of implementation Vulkan Buffer structure in C++. More stuff you have together the faster it will accumulate new changes growing out of control.

> > registerRecipes and registerTools could be named just register if they were in separate files, same way blocks are separate from items.
> > Removes name collisions, as MaterialProperty can become just Property
> 
> The same thing could be done with regular namespaces, it could easily be `Material.Property`.
> 
> > `toolTypeIterator` can become just `iterator`
> 
> I think that most of the proposed name changes are neutral (it neither makes typing nor reading easier/harder), 

This is not true. If it was true, namespaces would not overtake C style naming conventions in modern programming languages. If there is no name collision, renaming `registerRecipes` to `register` reduces length of the name by almost 50%. This means that user module that imports `recipes_zig` would now use `recipes_zig.register` instead of `items_zig.registerRecipes`. This means smaller diffs, shorter lines, less code. Applied consistently those gains add up. Shortening one name in one module used once somewhere else saves you 5 chars, but doing it for 100 functions used 500 times total saves u 2.5k chars, 250 words, literally an essay you will not have to read. You write the code once, you read it hundreds of times, make yourself read as little duplicated information as possible.

> this one would even make things worse in my opinion, unless you want a separate file just for tool types.

Possible, naming things is hard.

> > What we could do is to group them under assets folder with other assets:
> 
> I don't think "assets" is a good way to group things. All of these things do completely different things. An asset to me is just "something that gets loaded from a file". That really is not a useful way to order things. Many things in the game are assets, but should we put half of the code into the `src/assets` folder? What if we decide that loading tools from assets (a recent addition by the way) was a bad idea? Where do we put them instead?

Naming things is hard, structuring is hard too. No matter what principles you chose, requirements may change. Does that mean we are not supposed to structure code at all? [There is an antipattern describing this approach](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/big-ball-of-mud-anti-pattern/), it has been proven destructive.

I am not emotionally tied to grouping those files in a way I have shown, neither I care about the names, as long as the code is as efficient to traverse, read and change as possible.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Single word is not a description, it is a symbol - its open for interpretation.

It's just enough to tell me what it is about.

> I do not agree that one should not pay attention to them, especially when they are done correctly, see [numpy.fft](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/main/numpy/fft/__init__.py) or [cPython json module](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/main/Lib/json/__init__.py)

These are great example for comments that I would skip right over. "Just show me some code already"
I didn't think I'd ever say something like this, but honestly you'd probably be better of letting an AI write you gist, than relying on the poor skills of developers to keep their comments up to date.

> This is very useful if module is stable, since it makes it so documentation is actually close to code compared to having separate markdown files.

Yes, I absolutely agree. However Cubyz source code is neither stable, nor intended to be used as a module in third-party projects.

> Whether you will have to touch multiple files or not depends heavily on what you are doing.

Of course it does, but for commands in particular I'd only touch one file most of the time, in the rare case of a change in its interface I'd touch all files at once. In graph theory you'd call this a tree, if you change the node, you need to change the children, but there are no links between the children.

This is obviously different to the many dependencies inside the items.zig file.

> Why don't we just place everything in one file

I don't think we need to talk about this. Let's try to avoid the straw man fallacy here.

> What I noticed is that you are placing a lot of functionality in one file without setting boundaries.

I do set boundaries, and I do regular split files when they get too large, or collect too many different things.
To me personally 1000 lines of code is just simply the optimal file size when it comes to complicated concepts.
And of course I wouldn't want to see a 5000 line file anywhere in Cubyz.

> renaming registerRecipes to register reduces length of the name by almost 50%.

Doesn't matter for functions. Most functions are called on a separate line, and whether the function name is 5 characters longer or not makes no difference to me.

> literally an essay you will not have to read.

Those are not really words that you read. Reading a 250 word essay takes time and effort, reading 250 words of redundant variable names just get filtered out by my brain.

> Does that mean we are not supposed to structure code at all?

Another straw man, I never said that, and the code in question isn't even unstructured to begin with in my opinion.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> These are great example for comments that I would skip right over. "Just show me some code already"
> I didn't think I'd ever say something like this, but honestly you'd probably be better of letting an AI write you gist, than relying on the poor skills of developers to keep their comments up to date.

This is "I came here to understand, own and (possibly) modify" mindset. You can't understand, own and modify everything, it's not a sustainable model. You can try going through numpy's code to understand how to use function X and all of the quirks of it's 6 parameters, but you will not capitalize on that knowledge since you had to only use it once. It would be much more efficient to read summary for that one argument you care about and forget.

I'll stop here, since we just clearly have different view on how code should be structured and that's okay. After all it is not my burden to maintain Cubyz, I can just read once and forget and do useful stuff anyway

I guess if I am wrong then it does not matter, and if I am right, you will maybe see for you self.

// ISSUE TITLE: Add block data notation alongside block type
// ISSUE REPORT:
I might be mistaken but currently there is no way to specify block data when using eg. blocks with rotations. 

I have few proposals to alleviate this issue:

## Integer notation

First proposal is to allow numeric value to be included after block ID that gets directly translated to block data. For example for `oak_fence` to specify certain data (and hence orientation of oak fence) one would use:

```
cubyz:oak_fence!3
```

(separator sign `!` is not part of proposal, chosen to be different than namespace separator for clarity)

## Aliases in rotation definition

Second proposal involves specifying a known aliases (basically an enum of possible values) for different data configurations. For example for `oak_fence` to specify certain data (and hence orientation of oak fence) one would use:
```
cubyz:oak_fence!left_right
```

## Use Zon suffix

Third proposal involves notation similar to what Minecraft uses (they use JSON tho). Example use:
```
cubyz:oak_fence!.{.isConnectedNegX = true}
```

Although optional, `!` or any other unique separator is useful to quickly separate data part from block type.

Related to: #1113

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Right now it is implemented with another colon as `cubyz:oak_fence:3`
This feature is currently only used in the modern art biome which is why you may have missed it.

// ISSUE TITLE: How should modifiers of the same type behave?
// ISSUE REPORT:
For example, what should happen if a tool has two items that give +20% power with the Powerful modifier?

I think we have two options here, with wildly different implications for gameplay:
1. They add together, this makes the most sense intuitively, 2 boosts are better than 1.
However this might encourage, just spamming the currently best available material throughout the tool.

2. Only one of the modifiers is used, the other one is ignored.
This would encourage using different modifiers as much as possible, however it might also make the tool creation process a lot more complicated and annoying.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Could make the modifier strength dependent on where it is on the tool.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Could also be that subsequent instances of the same material will increase the modifier by a tiny amount.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I don't think second option is good idea, as it is significantly reducing options you have to boos your tools. IMO diminishing returns based on location of boosting item in crafting grid would be better. We could make it so different modifiers are 100% effective in different places of item while they are less effective in other spots. For example `good at` modifier can be 100% effective in tip spot, as it is logical since the tip is the part that digs. On the other hand `long` modifier makes most sense when used on handle thus when used in other spots it would be way less effective. This would create optimization pattern where user can commit to making tool very fast at digging, but suboptimal from gameplay perspective, creating a tradeoff situation.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I don't think I fully understand this mechanics though. I was thinking that something like runes from Wither 3 would be cool, where you can add slots for runes in dedicated workstation and create runes that have specific effects, then you can place those runes in spots in tools. This would crate a lot of use for different blocks, as they might be necessary to crate different runes, make it viable to add another workstation for rune creation and application. This approach would, instead of extending procedural crafting, add another layer of upgrades on top of it, as it is usually in games, extending progression. Making certain runes require rare materials would create incentive to explore, especially if runes would have different levels of power like in Wither. For example:

- tier 1 good at rune could be crafted from stone and copper, giving 5% boost
- tier 2 could be crafted from `<deep stone or alike>` and gold giving 10% boost
- tier 3 could be crafted from obsidian and diamond giving giving 15% boost 
- tier 4 from materials that are available only in different dimension, or very deep under ground.

In general I think that it is good that those boosts are small, making it so they have to be added on top of each other to make a significant effect. They should also be reasonably cheap so you have a reason to make more than 1 pickaxe, unlike in minecraft where at some point you make netherite pickaxe with mending and you just sit on it for eternity.

I think this approach is better than minecraft enchanting mechanics, as enchanting lack any depth, you just stack books and hope you will land a good enchants with levels you grinded. On the other hand runes from Wither 3 worked well there, rewarding players for exploration. Additionally runes can be much more recognizable than effects that are denoted only by description of item, as at some point in Wither 3 you were able to remember what sing+color on rune means what upgrade.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
You certainly get the ideas flowing. I think we should go with diminishing returns first, just to keep things simple.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
As for the slot dependant one, that could probably be defined in the material.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Oh yeah, and #1 almost sounds like Diablo II's socketing system, where you can put gems into a weapon to give it subtle upgrades

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> For example good at modifier can be 100% effective in tip spot, as it is logical since the tip is the part that digs. On the other hand long modifier makes most sense when used on handle thus when used in other spots it would be way less effective

This idea doesn't scale well, since for every tool slot+modifier combination you'd have to specify how good it is.
My plan is to add all the modifiers you could ever need into the game, and furthermore addons should be able to add their own tools, but if they have to specify how 100 different modifies scale on each slot, that would be very tedious.

I plan to add other mechanisms to restrict modifiers though. I plan to make so you could e.g. say that maybe a gem needs to be encased in gold, or some other metal to work.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I don't like the rune idea, since it feels to me like it's just stealing the spotlight of materials in the procedural crafting system, without adding anything new to the actual gameplay.

And the procedural crafting system could do so much more than boring "rune slots", because the modifiers can depend on the arrangement of surrounding materials, turning it into a more fun optimization game.

// ISSUE TITLE: Shards instead of blocks
// ISSUE REPORT:
Hi,

This is a bold proposal I want to hear comments on.
Currently most of blocks (except ores) drop full blocks upon destruction.
This is a well understood pattern, but it creates a funny interaction with awesome procedural crafting system of Cubyz - you need 4 cubic meters of stone to create a pickaxe (~11 tons). Of course its just a game and this comparison does not matter much, but I think that a way to make it more realistic would be to make most of blocks drop different types of shards of blocks instead of blocks themselves and make the tools and blocks craftable from those shards. 

For example, stone should drop stone shards which can be then used to craft stone tools and stone blocks. This also creates opportunity to create easy to understand crafting recipes for stairs, walls and alike, since we can use some percentage of shards dropped from full block to create a half-block like wall.

In my opinion drop rate should be stable, not random, for example 4/9 shards per block and 4/9 shards to craft a block.

Such change would require balancing changes in tools durability (lowering it) but this is also beneficial in my opinion - it will encourage players to make more use of crafting system. Additionally this would be consistent with crafting from iron ingots etc.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I have experimented with block shards in the past. The results:

- Added too much clutter to the inventory
- Had no practical gameplay purpose
- Is a lot of extra unessesary texturing

If you want to play with a game that has block shards, I recommend checking out Better Than Wolves, as the focus for that game is largely survival then tech. Cubyz is more about exploration and adventure, so it would not benefit from this change.

> For example, stone should drop stone shards which can be then used to craft stone tools and stone blocks. This also creates opportunity to create easy to understand crafting recipes for stairs, walls and alike, since we can use some percentage of shards dropped from full block to create a half-block like wall.

Stairs and slabs can already be made using the chisel, they do not need a recipe.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Could you explain why you need them?
The problem is the more tags there are the more fragile the system becomes.
I have decided on 3 tags per range because it sort of balances itself, the majority of biomes will always have the central/default value, so there is enough of a buffer between the two extremes.
Furthermore in order to avoid long stretches of the same biome, each tag combination should ideally have multiple different biomes, which becomes more difficult the more tags there are.

So if you just want these for more granularity for transition biomes, then I would suggest we search for a different way.

// ISSUE TITLE: [Ore] Magnesium
// ISSUE REPORT:
This is a proposal to add **magnesium** to the game.

## Physical properties:
- hardness in Mohs scale: 2.5
- density (at 20° C): 1.7 g/cm3

## In game properties:
- behavior similar to gemstones - provide special effect instead of stats
- low durability
- low damage
- light

## Modifiers
- ~`magnetic`: (pun intended!) Attracts items on the ground. Attraction force should scale with amount of magnesium player has in his currently held tool and armor it wears.~
- `blunt`: (if used as a tip) damage delt by tool approaches 1 as durability approaches 0

## Spawn rules
- `-? < Z < -?`
- Spawns in veins

Related to: #1269

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
After last playtest we agreed that making magnesium magnetic may have unexpected negative impact on younger Cubyz audience, so that idea will be abandoned. Instead we figured out #1325 would be a good replacement.
That said magnesium is still useful as source for brighter (more end game) torches, comparable to current white crystals. Maybe we can figure out something else on top of that.

- density (at 20° C): 4 g/cm3

- can be used as tip similarly to diamond with similar effect

// ISSUE TITLE: Crafting Principles
// ISSUE REPORT:
1. Crafting should be intuitive to the player, and shouldn't require a written explanation outside of a small tooltip. Terraria does this via a guide, but this can also be achieved with #758, letting players discover recipes on their own.

2. Crafting recipes should make sense and be **_inspired_** by real life. If a player wants to make glass, they would think to melt sand in a kiln/furnace, like how it's done in real life.

3. Crafting progression should be add-ons to existing blocks rather than a new block entirely, making the structure have to grow as you progress, encouraging the player to make a more dedicated space for them.
Examples of this:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/16882438-335f-4202-8e19-104430451900)

_These principles may change depending on feedback and changing ideas._

Issues relating to crafting:
- #89
- #197
- #594
- #625
- #634
- #758
- #759
- #760

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> shouldn't require a written explanation

Some written explanation is fine, if it fits nicely into the remaining UI. A tooltip is a good example for that.
Like for example for the chimney idea, it could say "This looks like it should be placed on top of a furnace" in the tooltip to guide the player into the right direction.

>  If they want to color it [glass] though, we can't exactly add complex chemicals for each color

Well colored glass can be made with metals by adding them before melting. Like if you use gold you can get red and purple colors because the gold forms nanoparticles of a certain size which absorb specific wavelengths of light.
I think it would be quite fun to figure out what metals can yield what colors. The final color would also depend on the temperature, so maybe there is also a temperature selection in the furnace.

[USER COMMENT]
chimney could stack and furnace can produce smoke that chimney will tranport

also love concept

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think we should try to keep issues actionable/solvable. "design principles" issues are not. We now have the design document for this, and the first point is covered there, and I think the rest are covered in other issues.

// ISSUE TITLE: Make a launcher that allows selecting the latest master and tagged versions from github
// ISSUE REPORT:
The batch/bash scripts are great, but they are not really intuitive. It would be best to have a launcher similar to the java version, that allows downloading and updating the game without causing any confusion.

Additionally there should be a button that automatically opens the game folder in the system file viewer.

Also there should be a window that displays the game and compiler logs.
All these logs should also be written to a file, just in case.

The launcher should be in a separate repository or branch.
It should have minimal dependencies to reduce the binary size. Can we get it below 1 MB in ReleaseSmall?

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
@IntegratedQuantum 

It would require you to build every release for every platform and upload the binaries somewhere.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Actually I would still want it to compile the game locally.
The point of the launcher would be to automatically clone the right version from github, removing an extra step and removing the dependency on git.

It would probably make sense to upload binaries for all tagged versions anyways to reduce compile time, but that should be easy with zig's cross compilation capabilities.

[USER COMMENT]
How are you planning to remove the git dependency? Wouldn't it still depend on git internally?

Also, from previous experience, compilation is really sensitive to how your computer is set up, with a high chance of not working out of the box. A compiling installer would just hide all the useful error logs. Though I can understand the utility of an installer that simply downloads existing binaries, or manages different builds from different versions.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
By removing dependency I mean not requiring the user to install it.
> Also, from previous experience, compilation is really sensitive to how your computer is set up, with a high chance of not working out of the box.

From my experience zig is self-contained and works out of the box.

> A compiling installer would just hide all the useful error logs

Good point. It should probably have a window with the logs, similar to the minecraft launcher.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
What is the status of that issue?
I see that there is [PixelGuys/Cubyz-Launcher](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz-Launcher) repository, there are no issues, no PRs no commits.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
It's incomplete and I currently have other priorities. As for issues, this is the issue for it.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We don't need this. The idea of a launcher is mostly a relic from the java version, where the installation process had significantly more problems due to the dependency on the jvm.

The main use-cases for a launcher are:
- compiling development versions of the game → this is solved well enough by the scripts, a launcher wouldn't make the current 3-step process (download, extract, double click the script) much easier.
- making updating easier → this could be done from within the game #1485
- allowing to update without losing saves → #1484
- Allow to capture messages not handled through the logger (e.g. stack traces) → #1486
- make the general installation process easier for the average user → #388

The main disadvantages of a launcher are:
- It needs to be maintained, taking time away from development on the actual game
- It makes the entire installation process more complex, adding more points of failure (e.g. linkrot)
- It adds another step for the average user to enter a world

As you can see there are alternative ways to handle most of the advantages of a launcher. So I don't think we really need a launcher like this, and we most certainly should not block the first official release on it.

There is alternative launcher proposal in #1483 which might be more useful to us going forward.

// ISSUE TITLE: The game crashes when a player with an empty name joins.
// ISSUE REPORT:


// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
When you fix this, you should also make blank player names impossible.

[USER COMMENT]
> When you fix this, you should also make blank player names impossible.

That's tracked in #704

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> That's tracked in #704

You mean this very issue?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
It's impossible to make blank player names impossible (or at least it's a lot of effort), there are just too many non-obvious blank unicode characters. For example many applications allow zero width spaces "​" as names.

[USER COMMENT]
> > That's tracked in #704
> 
> You mean this very issue?

My bad, thought this was 701 for some reason :<

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Could make it so you are required to insert at least 1 valid character.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
What purpose does that serve, if you can just insert a space?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Numbers or Letters only. Having a blank name would be advantageous in multiplayer to hide yourself.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
So what? That's not much different from using a camouflage skin.

Also in survival you probably won't see everyone from infinitely far away anyways.
A faction system like in Terraria could for example allow to only see your allies from a distance.

// ISSUE TITLE: Sound Effect System Checklist
// ISSUE REPORT:
Open to revisions or additions, this is WIP

## Easy:
- [ ] sound audio assets
- [ ] sound definition assets, which allow reusing existing sound audio assets and play with effects
- [ ] volume
- [ ] attenuation 
- [ ] spatial and non-spatial audio
- [ ] sound mixing
- [ ] looping sounds
- [ ] UI sounds

##  Medium:
- [ ] data-driven sfx for addon creators and modders for example, defining block sounds like breaking or placing 
- [ ] sound handle structures, that allow to control some sound effects trough logic, like position and other sound settings (allows making sounds attached to moving entities)
- [ ] random sound picking from user defined list in the sound definition file and sound sequences, requires the ability to have multiple sounds per sound definition asset  

##  Hard:
- [ ] simd mixing
- [ ] doppler effect
- [ ] ambient triggers (birds, scary sounds, wind)

##  PHANTASMAL DIFFICULTY:
- [ ] dynamic sound filters (downsampling, phaser, pitch-shifting, etc.)
- [ ] reverb
- [ ] custom sound/music fading triggers
- [ ] ambient overrides (like adding echo underground or something)

// DISCUSSION:

// ISSUE TITLE: command piping
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

It would be really useful to be able to pipe commands unix-style (i.e. `/spawn | tp`)

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
why not just `/summon entityName ~ ~ ~` or whatever coordinates? This way you don't need to spawn it then teleport it, just spawn it where you want it

[USER COMMENT]
> why not just `/summon entityName ~ ~ ~` or whatever coordinates? This way you don't need to spawn it then teleport it, just spawn it where you want it

I think you misunderstood what the the issue describes.

`/spawn | tp` would get the spawn of the player and then teleport the player to it.

I think this is interesting. And should be a far future goal, but there is a lot of other things which are currently in discussion about commands so this issue should only be done when these are resolved

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
It's not exactly how piping works in Unix shell is it?
When you use pipe, you connect stdout of one command to stdin of different one. It doesn't change the argument list (unless you use a special executable).
Firstly, output of most of Cubyz commands is designed to be human friendly, secondly we don't really have a reliable way to capture it.
Moreover we are never using dynamic input to change the behavior of commands.
Overall, I don't see a use case compelling enough to do major refactoring with major changes to command semantics but you can try to prove me wrong.
Our commands are intended to alter the state of the game, not to output data or take extended input, or do multiple steps of processing.
I don't think there are use cases within that scope that will be unachievable without piping.

// ISSUE TITLE: Tool Material Modifier Ideas
// ISSUE REPORT:
Material should have certain modifiers associated with them, similar to how it's done in Tinker's Construct.
However given that this is a pattern based system, it would also be possible to allow modifiers that only work if certain patterns are made.

If you got any ideas for modifiers, feel free to comment them below.

## Ideas
### Common/Simple modifiers
Name | Concept | Examples
-- | -- | --
binding | Adds durability when next to a junction of other materials | sticky materials, fibers
long(?) | Stronger when multiple items of this type are in a straight/diagonal line | sticks/rods
hard | Increases speed when at the tip of a tool | diamond
brittle | Decreases durability depending on the number of neighbors surrounding it | diamond, glass/ceramic
thorny | Inflicts bleeding effect on the target, damages the player when on the handle | cactus
poisonous | Poison damage (damages the player when used for the handle) | lead, radioactive materials
combustible | The tool looses durability when the player is on fire | wood
hot | Burns the player/enemies, interacts with combustible materials | hot stuff?
cold | Protects neighboring combustible blocks from durability damage, looses durability when next to a hot material | ice
soluble | The tool looses durability when the player is under water | some salts/crystals
far-reaching | increases range of the tool
blunt(?) | damage decreases the longer you use it | sulfur

### Rare/special modifiers
Name | Concept
-- | --
single-use | only one material with this modifier can be used per tool, used to prevent overpowered tools
one-hit | increases damage to 1000×, but tools breaks after one use, without options to repair it
unbreakable | tool cannot loose durability
self-repairing | tool slowly heals durability
explosive | triggers a small explosion on use
non-newtonian(?) | looses power when not used, gains power when used often
"zanite"(?) | gains speed on use
life-steal | heals the player when damage other entities
energy-steal | gives energy to the player when damaging other entities
soul-steal | Increases damage for every entity killed


### Uncategorized
- The surrounding 3×3 field needs to be filled with mundane items to enable some other modifier
- The tool must contain x copies of this material to enable some other modifier
- Activates another modifier if the tool contains no single-use materials
- Activates another modifier if the tool only contains a small amount of items
- strength depends on global stats, like e.g. how many unique mobs the player killed

### Addon options
- Addons could add custom modifiers that enable additional (block) drops

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
rare modifier: non-newtonian: looses power over time when not used, but if used often, gains power.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Material density already impacts these properties. A separate modifier for heavy tools is not needed.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
It could be of use to add a modifier that would make tool very effective against specific block type/tag. For example, lets assume you want to dig a big hole in a mountain of sandstone. You could just make a pickaxe that is effective against every stone type, but it just happens so the mountain is made solely of stone. In such case you could opt for a tool that is very effective against particular type of material, lets say twice as effective as reachable by other means, while also twice less effective against other materials. This kind of specialization encourages players to craft more and put more effort into what they craft. Ofc this would be magic and noone cares why a pickaxe that can easily break obsydian cant deal with stone 😅

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
So cool that I immediately implemented it.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
`blunt` - as durability approaches 0, damage of tool approaches 1. This can be used to penalize using soft materials on tip.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think that instead of just increasing damage, `thorny` could inflict `bleeding` effect that would stack up to N times and deal small amount of damage per second, like 0.01, 0.02 with 2 stacks etc. Stacks would wear off after N minutes. Compared to fire, it does not cancel with frost and it can not be removed immediately. Player should not receive any special effects (like shaking of screen) when damage is inflicted.

We could also have this be inflicted passively when damaging player in armor with that effect.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We could have modifier like `life steal` or something that would heal you for small portion of damage delt. Could be provided by `ruby` gemstone.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We could have `energy steal` or something that would steal small portion of energy of your oponent (if they have energy) and give small amount of energy to you. Could be provided by `citrine` gemstone.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We could have `soul stealer` modifier that would cause blades to deal more damage more entities you killed with it. This is infinite scaling, so generally speaking should not appear at the same time as `unbreaking` or `self-repairing` to avoid infinite damage stacking. 

Overall I am not sold on having `self-repairing` and `unbreaking` in current form, in my opinion they will only cause players to craft less and need less resources. IMO if materials are fairly easy to obtain (as they can just be found in caves) it doesn't make sense to stay with same pickaxe infinitely. This is a bit different in games like Minecraft where creating a top pickaxe is quite expensive and doing this over and over is simply boring. In Cubyz with horizontal material balance I don't think we will have similar problem, as making even best pickaxe still only requires only few items, not hours of farming XP for enchants.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Those are good ideas, I added them to the list.
About unbreaking/self-repairing, I think they make a lot of sense if they are a tradeoff, maybe it takes away the players energy or health. (or maybe it's just blunt, making it useless over time anyways :​P)

[USER COMMENT]

// ISSUE TITLE: A proposal for command modding and ambiguous commands // ISSUE REPORT: Commands are a bit of a special-case: Mods will inevitably want to add commands with the same name. For all other assets this is solved by adding `mod:` in front of it, but for commands this would be rather annoying. Autocomplete would make this bearable, but it would still make commands needlessly long. Beyond that there is a second problem: Ambiguous commands. As commands get more complex we (or a mod author) may find ourselves in a situation where multiple results may be parsed. One example of this would `/tp ` and (hypothetical) `/tp `, if any biome id matches a structure id, it is unclear where to tp the player. To solve this I propose to send an error to the player when this happens and give them an option to resolve the conflicts, possible message: ``` Found ambiguous arguments for command ..., could be /tp[1] /tp[4] ``` The numbers would be the index based on the order of the command arguments union (and the order of mods), to select one variant you could then just run `/tp[1] cubyz:xyz` // DISCUSSION: [USER COMMENT] While this is a solution. I still like the `mod:` solution more. This would give the player a better idea about what the index means. I suggest maybe something in this direction: ``` Found ambiguous arguments for command ..., could be /tp /tp please type either /tp biome: or /tp structure: ``` this could then also later be used for non index based optional arguments. We probably need some kind of reserved key to indicate that the start is talking about the argument name. For overloaded commands you can do: ``` Found ambiguous arguments for command ..., could be /cubyz:tp /mod:tp ``` While this does "bloat" the commands. I think it makes it more explicit and would pave the way for a maybe later more complex system [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Adding biome: to the string may in itself also cause problems, e.g. if there is an addon with the same name, then it could cause more issues. I do however agree that `mod:` is nicer. So let me extend on that: ``` /cubyz:tp:biome /mod:tp:structure ``` Where the last part would be the name of the command in the code (currently it's `@"/tp ..."`, but once we auto-generate these, we could replace them with a better name). [USER COMMENT] > Adding biome: to the string may in itself also cause problems, e.g. if there is an addon with the same name, then it could cause more issues. That's why I said this: > We probably need some kind of reserved key to indicate that the start is talking about the argument name. Something like `/tp $biome:cubyz:rare/rainbow_forest` Or mabye if we don't use `=` somewhere: `/tp biome=cubyz:rare/rainbow_forest` Maybe even go full zig mode: `/tp .biome=cubyz:rare/rainbow_forest` [MAINTAINER COMMENT] The issue with `mod:foo` or even worse ` mod:bar/foo` is that it's very unintuitive. It requires you to start referencing the namespace where something is, instead of referencing the thing they want to use. That makes the auto completion very inefficient, since boilerplate is the first thing you have to write, before you actually communicate to auto complete what you intend to use. Approach proposed in the issue is good, although it would require extra QOL refinement to make it more pleasant. Even though we have history, moving cursor through whole message to add square brackets and a number is tedious. I would suggest to make the command clickable in UI and for shell usage re-prompt for the number alone. > there is an addon with the same name We could require leading `:` to imply mod name has to be inferred. > /tp .biome=cubyz:rare/rainbow_forest Or we can go typical shell syntax route ` --biome=` In general anything that delocalizes the knowledge is annoying. Keyword/flag option is IMO better because it keeps the knowledge localized - meaning of an argument is annotated right next to an argument. On the contrary both numbering and word approaches delocalizes the knowledge moving annotation to the start of the command, regardless of where the ambiguity happened. That also doesn't scale if there are multiple ambiguities. [USER COMMENT] > I do however agree that `mod:` is nicer. So let me extend on that: > > ``` > /cubyz:tp:biome > /mod:tp:structure > ``` > > Where the last part would be the name of the command in the code (currently it's `@"/tp ..."`, but once we auto-generate these, we could replace them with a better name). I would also find this an acceptable solution. Mine would allow as said non index based optional arguments, but this is also soemthing we don't really need. One thing where it could be interesting is with multiple optional arguments e.g: https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/pull/3106#discussion_r3292812776 What if both optional arguments have overlapping ok arguments? [USER COMMENT] > > /tp .biome=cubyz:rare/rainbow_forest > > Or we can go typical shell syntax route ` --biome=` 👍 I like both, but the shell syntax is probably better as its more known. But at the same time `.biome` would fit better with zon syntax wich if people touch addons it is not far fetched. So consistency may also play a role here [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I would also prefer the `.` syntax. I think it's better to be consistent with our addon files, than to be consistent with the command line (where honestly the patterns are much more varied, `--biome=value` is hte most common these days, but I still frequently see `-biome value` or, on that one special OS that wants to do everything differently, `/biome value`) We could also make a poll for this on the discord. [USER COMMENT] i did like the ``/tp[mod]`` idea, but it would add more to type ( ``[]`` is one more characters then ``:`` ), and we want commands to be short/fast to write the ``--biome=value`` type command does fit with CLI, I think there was some talk about using cubyz as a CLI tool. And i think this would be a good step into that direction. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > and we want commands to be short/fast to write Ideally you'd only need to write it when the parser cannot find a clear match [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Yeah I have in mind that we may want to accept shell arguments for Cubyz executable and having single tool do both would be convenient. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] CLI is besides the point. If you think there is a use-case for command-line arguments (when we already have the launchconfig), then please make an issue for it. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Ok, some other remarks regarding `.foo=bar` syntax: - breaks use of relative file paths in commands or requires a check that its not `./spam` - we already have a precedence for unix flag `-v` in paste command. What would it be changed to? [MAINTAINER COMMENT] What should we do if we have both positional and keyword arguments seemingly for the same argument? ``` /foo /foo qux .bar=baz ``` What should we do if keyword precedes the position of the argument in argument list? ``` /foo .spam=baz qux ``` Eg. Python interpreter would report an error if a function was called this way, as i makes assumption that keywords always follow positional arguments. In first case it would be error out with `() got multiple values for argument 'x'` In second one it would complain that `positional argument follows keyword argument` Otherwise we have to do two passes? Collect recursively keywords first, then do positionals recursively skipping, keywords we found. And then we still have problem with all the different branches and doing this exhaustively. That still doesn't give us this error message: ``` Found ambiguous arguments for command ..., could be /tp /tp please type either /tp biome: or /tp structure: ``` As currently argparser is greedy and takes first argument list that matches. So we would have to do all patchs instead and record which ones were successful. --- IMO we should do what every sane CLI does - use subcommands explicitly, instead of having tons of different parsing paths. ``` /tp biome /tp structure /tp player /tp position /tp pos ... ``` Then parsing path is obvious based on explicit second argument representing a subcommand name. Our discussion about keyword arguments in this issue proves that current approach is not good enough and we actually need players to write more than just list of arguments. We silently agreed that its OK to ask players to write more, so why do we have to complicate it? With current approach of "lets try to guess what author meant", autocomplete will have to include both biome IDs and structure IDs when autocompleting second argument, since we don't know which one player meant. That generates extra UX challenge of telling player what exactly did autocomplete autocompleted for them (biome? structure? devil spawn?). With subcommands it would be trivial and obvious what to autocomplete. Additionally we could significantly shrink error messages, since we would no longer consider multiple paths. To remind you what's the current state: With subcommands we could just print one path. Moreover, this would reduce number of alternative ways you can express same command. Right now in many cases unions are just an alternative to optional arguments (with better error messages for reasons mentioned in #3132) Surely there are reasons why we are not doing it right now, right? From technical perspective, using subcommands shouldn't be much different, IMO much cleaner, tho. `Args` would be formed as: ```zig const Args = union(enum) { biome: struct { biome: command.BiomeId }, position: struct { x: command.Coordinate, y: command.Coordinate, z: command.Coordinate, }, player: struct { playerIndex: command.PlayerIndex }, }; ``` At comptime, `main.argparse.Parser(Args, .{.commandName = "tp"});` would generate strings for each subcommand/branch: ``` "/tp biome " "/tp position " "/tp player " ``` Based on field names. `union(enum)`s would be treated as subcommand lists. We could consider if we want to allow multiple layers of sub commands. Another example, `perm` command: ```zig /// Current const Args = union(enum) { @"/perm ": struct { action: enum { add, remove }, list: enum { whitelist, blacklist }, playerIndex: ?command.PlayerIndex, permissionPath: Path, }, @"/perm ": struct { playerIndex: ?command.PlayerIndex, permissionPath: Path }, }; /// Alternative const Args = union(enum) { add: struct { list: enum { whitelist, blacklist }, playerIndex: ?command.PlayerIndex, permissionPath: Path, }, remove: struct { list: enum { whitelist, blacklist }, playerIndex: ?command.PlayerIndex, permissionPath: Path, }, query: struct { playerIndex: ?command.PlayerIndex, permissionPath: Path, }, }; ``` ``` "/perm add " "/perm remove " "/perm query " ``` [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > In first case it would be error out with () got multiple values for argument 'x' > In second one it would complain that positional argument follows keyword argument Seems good > Based on field names. union(enum)s would be treated as subcommand lists. We could consider if we want to allow multiple layers of sub commands. That all seems reasonable. Though I don't know if this really fits all use cases, e.g. how about `/time day` and `/time `? You would need to either make up awkward subcommand names to distinguish them or make a weird type that can be both a number or an enum. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > You would need to either make up awkward subcommand names to distinguish them or make a weird type that can be both a number or an enum. Life is tough and full of disappointments. --- I don't know man. Honestly It seems to me we are reinventing the wheel. Unix shell already solved this issue with `alias`. Moreover we already established in the past that things need namespaces for different add-ons to not collide with each other and now we are basically trying to prove otherwise - that somehow everything can coexist without a namespace. After some thinking I come to a conclusion that args of a single command should be a black box. It's simply too unpredictable what it will contain and how deep it will be. It's fully customizable through struct parsing. Maybe we could just treat Id as chain of subcommands: `/cubyz worldedit undo` tedious but quite clean. This obviously requires aliasing, maybe with some reasonable default explicitly specified in command, that would be taken if no collision is found. Alias map would be saved to cubyz dir. Now a separate matter is how we implement that subcommand dispatch. Maybe instead of having ArgParser instance at command level we would have one global and we would somehow populate it at comptime with appropriate Args structs from commands. Recursive comptime struct building? Wait we already kind of do that no? We wanted to change how we approach mod structure right. I think I diverged. I think it would make sense to prune use of subcommands inside individual files. It's basically repeating information that could be contained inside Id - and usually is. Other than that, imo we already can design the commands to not be ambiguous, we are just avoiding typing due to lack of autocomplete. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > `/cubyz worldedit undo` tedious but quite clean. I think this would be confusing. If we go the aliasing route, I think we should use the id `/cubyz:worldedit/undo`, no spaces. Then you wouldn't need to build anything new into the parser. Other than that the main questions I have are: Who stores and resolves the aliases (client or server), how are aliases displayed in help menus and how are the default aliases constructed, what happens when two mods have overlapping aliases? [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Aliases would be part of command line behavior, they would be fully transparent for everything. Aliases would be per-player, I think client sided would be doable, so server doesn't have to store them. Basically before sending command, your command line would pre-parse the command and map command name to full command ID. It would be up to the server to validate if the ID, alias was mapped to, makes any sense. That means you can preserve aliases even if you currently don't have some of the mods you used them for. There is an UI issue with making this client sided tho - error messages cannot be adjusted to use the alias when alias was used to invoke command, but IMO its a fair compromise, commands are aimed at more advanced users anyway. On the other hand trying to counteract that is going to bring huge amount of complexity, as command name would no longer be comptime known. > what happens when two mods have overlapping aliases? This question sounds to me like its coming from misunderstanding. Such aliases may never exist. Aliases work like a hash map, so there can be only one command corresponding to an alias at a time. Default aliases would not be generated for commands for which its not possible to generate deterministic mapping (ie. more than one command for same name). [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > Default aliases would not be generated for commands for which its not possible to generate deterministic mapping (ie. more than one command for same name). Ah ok. Would you automatically generate aliases for all existing commands? or would it need to be registered separately? [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Not sure. Maybe we should have `preferred_alias` field and do it for all commands on startup, or maybe mod should toggle that in metadata file and we should only do it for mods which enable it? Maybe it should be a command? I don't like the idea to just make the Cubyz commands special, although they inevitably are. // ISSUE TITLE: Permissions based on command arguments
// ISSUE REPORT:
This is a goal I had since the beginning of the permission system.
Here I want finally make an issue out of it and make this the place to discuss how that should look like.

What do I exactly mean with this?
Take for example the spawn command. 
What if we wanted everyone to be able to get their spawnpoint but from no one else (and not being able to set it)

through the argparser I think we can achive this.

For example the permissionPath could be: 
(whitelist)`/command/spawn/playerIndex=null` (allow running /spawn where playerIndex is null => the player themself
(blacklist)`/command/spawn/playerIndex=null/x` => disallows x parameter => you cannot set the spawnpoint (could also do `/command/spawn/playerIndex=null/x/y/z` but with x is shorter

We could also go by the `Args` union and get permissionPaths through that:
(whitelist)`/command/spawn/@"/spawn <playerIndex>"` (how do we set here that the player should only be able to do it for them self?) With `<playerIndex=null>`? or maybe we append the arguments after that like in my first example? so `/command/spawn/@"/spawn <playerIndex>"/playerIndex=null`?

(needs to integrate nicely with #3125 to not make different syntaxes, thats also where I took the `playerIndex=null` idea from)

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Overall I am not to found of this idea, the main reason is that not every command needs same granularity of control resulting in either boilerplate or insufficient control. I would prefer if permission checks were done explicitly, manually and through simple API - does user have permission to do that? No? Then GTFO. Yes? Great, let's continue.
Are the paths arbitrary or hard coded?
Imo it would be best of they were arbitrary, described in command documentation.
The most glaring case of privilege granularity being critical is `/sbb` and `/blueprint` commands - they are very complicated in terms of what they can access (or will be able to access in the future). As I recently described in discord message https://discord.com/channels/443805812390100992/702167480654757991/1524022936988811424

> So, I have ran into `assets.zig` pissing me off again. I guess after I hard resetted my last attempt on 
> refactoring it I am damned to revisit it.
> This time I wanted to finally enable loading blueprints from assets, but that's kind of hard, even if I were to brute force it by hardcode search logic. Basically, I need to be able to re-do the proper asset search process with up to date filesystem data. 
> Also, I have my previous wild idea of adding more abstraction to where server/client differentiation occurs.
> For requirements, just `/sbb` (still doesnt exist) and `/blueprint` suggest following inputs would be very, very important:
> - absolute path
> - relative path
> - id
> Then there are sources:
> - server global assets
> - server world assets
> - server cubyzDir/blueprints
> (and now it gets crazy):
> - client global assets (hey i have this very cool addon that is not there on server but i want to just spawn something from my addon on server!)
> - client cubyzDir/blueprints
> - client cwd (hey I have this very... you get the point)
> - client cross-world asset loading (hey I have this world where I work on my sbb prototypes...)
> On top of that, It would be great if I could just use the same API to write back changed assets, at first I thought I need this only for bps and sbbs but then I thought about runtime manual migrations write back (we talked about that a while back, not sure if we have an issue, please link if you remember!)
> No even mentioning I might go insane if I have to re-figure out path normalization and safety even one more time.
> Ye well Ima work on some prototype, but I suspect discord will cut that message If I try to paste code here. I will let you know if I settle on something that remotely makes sense
> Surely you love my insanity rants I post here every now and then

Each search source may in theory require different privilege, with sources possibly being grouped, resulting in less, but more than 1, privilege. To determine default source it would be best to be able to query user privileges. Command should also allow explicitly specifying sources to use, likely though flags.

What about help command? Ideally you wouldn't be able to even ask about commands you don't have access to, to reduce possible attack surface.

There are two other issues I see from design perspective: 
- fragility/rigidness of automatic behavior we create that will limit what can be changed in, currently mostly unconstrained, argparse
- inherent complexity of such automated system, built on to already meta code heavy argparse

> For example the permissionPath could be:
> (whitelist)/command/spawn/playerIndex=null (allow running /spawn where playerIndex is null => the player themself
> (blacklist)/command/spawn/playerIndex=null/x => disallows x parameter => you cannot set the spawnpoint (could also do /command/spawn/playerIndex=null/x/y/z but with x is shorter
> 
> We could also go by the Args union and get permissionPaths through that:
> (whitelist)/command/spawn/@"/spawn <playerIndex>" (how do we set here that the player should only be able to do it for them self?) With <playerIndex=null>? or maybe we append the arguments after that like in my first example? so /command/spawn/@"/spawn <playerIndex>"/playerIndex=null?

What you are describing here imo can be summarized as "how do we string encode infinite possibilities of command arguments into a unique string". There is already a way to do this, it's called source code.
If we do find reliable patterns in permission queries we might want to build abstraction, but personally i think we are not there yet.

Then the parser just simply skips any subcommand that is not present in the permission path. This would also automatically reduce the error set to only the commands that you have permission to use.

// ISSUE TITLE: We need Path MTU discovery
// ISSUE REPORT:
On some connections the MTU is less than 1500 bytes, which may silently drop packets above the real MTU.
Always setting the MTU to the IPv4 minimum of 576 bytes is not a good solution, as it slows down everyone.
It might be worth looking into RFC 8899 (Datagram Packetization Layer Path MTU Discovery) which is used by QUIC.

- [x] Set the current MTU to 576 bytes (minus headers) as a hotfix
- [x] Change the packet IDs to represent a byte count instead of a packet count
- [x] Store a large continuous send buffer instead of a list of packets with heap allocated memory.
- [ ] Sometimes try to send packets of larger size to probe whether the MTU has increased.
- [ ] If one packet doesn't get received within a certain number of attempts, and if smaller packets do get received, then adjust the MTU downwards (half? 2/3?), keeping in mind the minimum of 576 bytes.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
I sadly can't contribute code because I don't know zig well, have uni stress and need to work on my own projects, but here's an idea how you could fix it temporarly in a better way:

The client dictates MTU size. Send client hellos from very large to very low. Every time a client hello is sent and no server hello returned, make the client hello smaller and repeat.

The server retrieves the mtu size from the size of the client hello and adds that information to a connection and assembles packets with that MTU in mind. Now the connection has a fixed MTU, but one that works (most likely).

I think this is much easier to implement than a real mtu discovery algorithm. Sadly mtu discovery is a chaotic topic because the internet is basically the wild west.

Edit: This would also already build a base for variable MTU which is needed for constant MTU discovery.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I'll consider this if it takes too long to implement real MTU.

[USER COMMENT]
RFC 8899 describes these 3 different probe message variants:
> Probing using padding data:
A probe packet that contains only control information together with any padding, which is needed to inflate to the size of the probe packet. Since these probe packets do not carry an application-supplied data block, they do not typically require retransmission, although they do still consume network capacity and incur endpoint processing.

>Probing using application data and padding data:
A probe packet that contains a data block supplied by an application that is combined with padding to inflate the length of the datagram to the size of the probe packet.

>Probing using application data:
A probe packet that contains a data block supplied by an application that matches the size of the probe packet. This method requests the application to issue a data block of the desired probe size.

The first one would probably involve making an extra channel for it. 
If we use the second or third we would have to decide which channel will to that because we will expect some missing packets when the probe size is to big. (but not much as we will not send that many and often probe messages)
The first one would probably make the logic a bit cleaner. 

From what I have read our network implemention only lacks these probe messages which will then change the `mtuEstimate` variable. For debugging purposes I would also say we add the `mtuEstimate` to the advanced network debug window

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
When I looked into it I think one of the main issues was how to detect and react to MTU reductions (which could theoretically happen if your traffic is rerouted for some reason)

> If we use the second or third we would have to decide which channel will to that because we will expect some missing packets when the probe size is to big.

The obvious answer is the slow protocol, the data inside is supposed to assume that it's fine to arrive late.

Overall which method we choose probably doesn't matter, we just need to ensure that the math is right and that the cost of the probe packet is always significantly less than the expected gains.

In the end it's probably just a question of which is easier to implement.

// ISSUE TITLE: Player cannons
// ISSUE REPORT:
A cannon is a multiblock structure made from a small one block hole surrounded by a two block wall of suitable material:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/dab24f93-3a2a-4541-b77d-388eeeed32b6)

The final height of the player depends on the materials of the barrel, the barrel length, the explosive and of course the air resistance.
