// ISSUE TITLE: Suggestion: Separate update loops
// ISSUE REPORT:
Currently physics are framerate dependent, which can cause inconsistencies down the line. A common solution to this is having physics only run at a fixed rate (I.E. 60 or 120fps) with a potential catch-up mechanic if rendering is slow. 

Some benefits from this:
- #599 Can probably be fixed by making sure something doesn't update too frequently
- #533 Can be alleviated by allowing multiple "physics" frames and input polls per full render. 
- Vsync can be made to only affect the "rendering" rate.

Some drawbacks:
- Rendering would require interpolation of physics (I think this is already implemented for networking though, so might not be a huge change?)
- Would require a larger amount of work to split physics and non-physics updates

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Another disadvantage is that it would increase input lag for players without vsync, because the physics will always be up to one physics tick behind the player input.

I think #599 is happening because the player moves an amount smaller than the margin used by collision. A solution would be to find a way to store more exact collision results without a margin.

#533 can be solved by just implementing the catch-up mechanic without fixing all updates to a certain frequency.

[USER COMMENT]
> Another disadvantage is that it would increase input lag for players without vsync, because the physics will always be up to one physics tick behind the player input.

This is the case for almost any game though, even competitive ones. Could also be possible to separate stuff driven more directly by player input like rotation or placing/removing blocks & interacting with UI to not be bound by a rate.

> I think https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/599 is happening because the player moves an amount smaller than the margin used by collision. A solution would be to find a way to store more exact collision results without a margin.

Hmm, collision does in theory set the position to the collision point near the end of the frame though. I tried disabling the spring & friction logic in case it was that, but didn't seem to do anything. I'll try disabling that part and see if it is. 

> https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/533 can be solved by just implementing the catch-up mechanic without fixing all updates to a certain frequency.

That might not work since the physics happen so quickly. If the GPU take 16ms to render for example, and the physics only take 0.1ms, then it could end up doing all the catch up physics frames immediately then have to wait for the GPU to finish rendering before it can bind it again. I guess if vsync was made to only affect rendering then that might automatically balance it though (or maybe that's what you meant?)

// ISSUE TITLE: Inventory onLastCloseCallback is called after user left
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior

From: https://discord.com/channels/443805812390100992/1500299574085161091

How to reproduce:

1. put items into a workbench
2. leave the world (without closing the workbench)
3. join the world again
4. items gone

What is happening in the code:
1.  User leaves the server (gets removed from the `userlist` and gets moved to `userdeinitList`)
2.  Inventories of the user get closed
3. `onLastCloseCallback` is called
4. `onLastCloseCallback` for workbench searches for user who "owns" the workbench and goes for that through the `userlist`
5. can not find them and does nothing (as the user is already not there anymore and instead is in the `userdeinitList`)

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Could this be related to the player leaking #2858?

[USER COMMENT]
> Could this be related to the player leaking [#2858](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/2858)?

I would be nice (so that we know where it comes from) but I don't see how

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think we should do this through the ECS:
- workbench inventory is an ECS component
- it gets stored with the player
- when they rejoin the items are reloaded, and the component checks if the workbench still exists in the world, if not the onLastClose is called

This also avoids any item loss when the player leaves with insufficient inventory space.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Alternative route (mostly for the record): 
- Make workbench an entity with an inventory.
- Store items permanently in workbench inventory.
- Workbench unloads as an entity.
- Profit.

Negatives:
- synchronization?
- I don't think it generalizes item transformation pattern.
- how the hell do we apply light levels to that?
- boo hoo it's less optimal memory wise

Extra positives:
- non sync data gets automatically managed by ECS instead of needing to implement sending ~~twice~~ for the third time (ECS, sync and block entities)
- animations are now ECS problem
- unloading is an ECS problem too (and they get unloaded much closer to the player)

// ISSUE TITLE: The problems of block damage per swing
// ISSUE REPORT:
I would like to document the problems that this design decision is causing currently, and I think we need to find a solution for each of them, or change the mechanic.

Note that will this ties into procedural crafting and balancing as a whole, this issue is intended to only cover issues with the block damage per swing mechanic, which can be covered independently (I think).

- [x] In order to efficiently mine a particular block, you need to know it's exact health+resistance, if you are off by 1% your tool will be 2× slower, making it useless. Over-damage (see #1430) could solve this, but I'm not yet convinced about the ergonomics of this solution. (solved by #1658)
- [ ] Swing speed has to be quite fast in order to mine blocks at an adequate speed (~10 blocks/s would be a reasonable target), and we might not be able to animate the pickaxe hand model in that way without being impossible to follow, again #1430 could help 
- [ ] The breaking animation not being smooth feels weird. That might get better if we have actual swinging animation, but I'm not sure. In my opinion a smooth transition would feel nicer, particularly for blocks that break in one or 2 swings, even if it's not realistic.
- [ ] In the worst case of the above, imagine having a slow (multiple seconds) pickaxe that breaks the block in one swing, we have no indication if we can actually break the block at all before fully breaking it, since we have to wait for the conclusion of the first swing. I would like to have a sort of "bedrock breaker" tool that falls into this category of insane damage at slow speed, low durability.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
A lot of these issues such as it not feeling smooth would be remedied with swinging animations.

As for the amount of damage dealt to a block being a mystery, let's assume #1590 is added. This would show how much damage the player is dealing to the block, making it so they can calculate it without having to use a wiki. As for blocks they don't know about.. well, that's where learning has to come in.

I don't think we should throw away the idea of individual swings. I honestly think it's a gold mine idea and should be expanded upon instead of given up... on.

2. Open new world created from the commit that triggered the CI, check log for errors.

[USER COMMENT]

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

> This way we can catch both migration-type errors as well as errors generally launching the game on the new version.

[USER COMMENT]
> It would be better to create a curated world

Are we going to require that contributors update the curated world?

I thought that we could trigger LOD regeneration, for example, simply by loading a `0.0.1` world in a build of the latest commit. Which could also be useful for catching errors caused when users try to load their existing worlds in a new build.

> Also a controlled shutdown would be nice, to be able to catch leaks.

Agreed. I think either a `launchConfig` option or a flag (`--ci` or something) can instruct the game to launch the server, run one update cycle, then exit cleanly.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Are we going to require that contributors update the curated world?

Yes, and that's fine, since it's not a problem to be compatible to older worlds than the target.
Furthermore if we only update the world on breaking changes, then it would be a good way to track when an world actually becomes no longer loadable.

> I thought that we could trigger LOD regeneration, for example, simply by loading a 0.0.1 world in a build of the latest commit.

Yes, but if there is no terrain stored, then it won't actually do anything.

> Agreed. I think either a launchConfig option or a flag

I think a launch config that is time based would make the most sense. In the future we may want longer tests that e.g. connect a dummy to the server.
Furthermore a timer like this could also be useful for normal hosts to e.g. regularly take the server down to run a backup routine, or to simply have a time-limited server.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I don't think controlling runtime using a flag and from inside is that good of an idea. It's very limited and won't scale to more complicated purposes. We will be stuck adding new arguments every time. Additionally time is meaningless, on one hardware few milliseconds will be enough to enter the world and tick n times, on other it won't even manage to load the assets. For non testing purposes that granularity is useless as humans are not functioning at that scale. People will run the server using some external restart logic anyway because it may panic and then it has to be triggered externally.

A better approach would be to create a communication protocol for server control, possibly with exchangeable backends (stdin/stdout and socket) and add one set of dedicated flags for making the server expect socket communication. Then a server could communicate that it reached certain execution stage and the controller could (gratefully) terminate it issuing an appropriate command.
That could be accompanied by debug commands reporting status of different systems.
The intent is for controller to utilize same command implementations as in-game commands issued by players.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
As for detecting that we are in CI there already exist a well known way - we should query environment variable `CI`

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Yes, that is a good point. A command through stdin would be nicer than a timer.

[USER COMMENT]
> Yes, that is a good point. A command through stdin would be nicer than a timer.

Can you elaborate on what the command should do?

Argmaster's description is very broad. It sounds like we'll need a calling program separate from Cubyz that can do this socket communication thing and can receive updates from the server about what it's executing and kill it after it reaches a desired point.

My best guess for now:

1. Create a new program that can call Cubyz and read/write a socket that the Cubyz server utilizes as well in CI.
2. The new program calls Cubyz with a flag to run the server in "CI" mode. It would use the socket to tell the calling program about where it is in execution.
3. The calling program waits until a certain communication has been given, then it issues a command to update `running` to false, stopping the server gracefully

There's still a lot of unknowns to me.

1. What are the specifics of this communication protocol? 
2. Do we need to support windows sockets or just unix sockets? 
3. What execution steps do we communicate to the controller?
4. What execution step do we want the CI to kill the server when it reaches?
5. Do we build the calling program in a separate repo and clone it using a script during the workflow?

Probably more questions once we have the answers to those.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Don't focus too much on the socket part, for now stdin/stdout is the way to go. The socket part could then even be implemented by a third party, I personally think that would be preferable anyways (such programs probably already exist).

> It sounds like we'll need a calling program separate

Yes, to test the game we do need another program (which we will need anyways to check the output and look for error logs and to prepare test cases by setting up the launchConfig and all that)

> What are the specifics of this communication protocol?

Honestly `std.log.info` and `std.log.debug` are probably good enough. We should probably have more of these anyways to debug other issues.

> What execution step do we want the CI to kill the server when it reaches?

For this first testing server I'd say we can kill it right after it starts the main loop. In the future we will probably need other tests that also spawn fake players and test some simulation stuff, but let's not get ahead of ourselves here.

> Do we build the calling program in a separate repo and clone it using a script during the workflow?

No, this would cause too much trouble when updating Zig versions and an general syncing other changes.
All tests belong into the main repo.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Calling program - professional therm is controller - should be a Python script. Been there done that, it's easy to create and extend, trivial to load into CI environment basically lowest difficulty threshold you can get.

// ISSUE TITLE: Make it illegal to place blocks inside of the hitbox of entities
// ISSUE REPORT:
Requires #88 

This only matters for other entities, the player itself is already covered.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Seeing as the duplicate #628 was fixed, I think I'll close this. Now we'll be 4 issues away from surpassing java.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think we should address this in the 0.1.0 version, there are a bunch of similar problems (player knockback, anti-cheat, death) that could benefit from a solution for this.

I think the only reasonable solution here is:
- [ ] block placing needs to fully go through the sync system, instead of using a by-pass protocol, this is a requirement to revert local block edits
- [ ] The client needs to send everything through the sync system, position, velocity, timestamp, inputs (very important), frame timestamp. These are needed to accurately revert and reapply movement when a block is placed.
- [ ] Depending on how bad it is a separate client side interpolation between the last predicted position and the fixed prediction needs to be implemented to avoid teleporting the camera

// ISSUE TITLE: The max texture array size is smaller than expected
// ISSUE REPORT:
I somehow always thought it was 65536, but apparently it's only 2048, even on Vulkan.
For reference: A new world currently has 831 layers, so just a few addons could hit the limit.

We've already had a first report where this happened due to a bug in the deduplication logic.

As far as I can see we have a few options:
- Use a texture atlas (which has too many problems, we kind of need GL_REPEAT)
- Use multiple texture arrays, but that seems like it could only delay the inevitable, and also would make sampling more annoying
- Use bindless textures, but I'm not sure how well that is supported, we could make that a part of the planned vulkan survey #2959
- Emulate it in software → NO! Not again!

// DISCUSSION:

i wonder if GL_ARB_sparse_texture could help with this, from what I can see it lets you have larger textures but only have portions of them loaded at the same time (unless i am missing something)

[USER COMMENT]
Pretty simple to override the player's terminal velocity if you want to give it a try.
Just replace the top line of game.zig update function with this. 
```zig
const terminalVelocity = @sqrt((2.0 * 37.3 * physics.baseGravity) / (0.390625 * 1.0 * std.math.exp(@min(-Player.super.pos[2], 0) / 4150.0) * 0.7));
physics.calculateVolumeProperties(.client, &Player.volumeProperties, Player.super.pos, Player.outerBoundingBox, terminalVelocity);
```
37.3 is mass in kg
0.390625 is the cross sectional area of the player (0.625 * 0.625)
1.0 is the air density in kg/m^3
4150.0 is atmospheric scale height (about 8500 on earth)
0.7 is coefficient of drag

This makes terminal velocity ~300 at height 10000

You get about the same if air density is 2.7 and mass is 100

// ISSUE TITLE: Skins todolist
// ISSUE REPORT:
I mostly write this todolist, so my future self remembers it:

## displaying the skin

- [ ] Add a Skin Component
- [ ] Modelrenderer use the skin Components texture if it is detected 
- [ ] don't store the same texture twice (saving gpu memory)

## selecting the skin

- [ ] have a gui to a file explorer (clients .cubyz/skins/ folder), with a preview window to the right.
- [ ] after selecting the skin, it gets send via skin protocol to the server, and stored in the skin component. when storing on disk, it only stores the path.
- [ ] have save1/skin/ folder storing the players skins. 
- [ ] server/world has an option to disallow custom skin selection. (also makes skin selection button disabled with a tooltip saying: "server disallows custom skin selection")
- [ ] texture must be of the same dimension as the default texture of that model
- [ ] Reset skin Component, if a different model is selected.
- [ ] transparent regions on the texture are only allowed if the default texture also has a transparent spot there.

## editing the skin

- [ ] create an inverse mapping texture by drawing the model to a FrameBuffer, but using the uv data as the position, and the color being the actual screen position
- [ ] create different brush shapes
- [ ] after finishing editing the skin, user has the option to store the skin locally .cubyz/skins/

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

// ISSUE TITLE: We need to re-think structure and naming of our entityEntityEntityComponentEntitySystem
// ISSUE REPORT:
Title is a joke. A true one, tho. Let's have a look at the issue:

```
src
├── client
│   ├── Entity.zig
│   └── entity_manager.zig
├── entity.zig
├── entityComponent
│   ├── _list.zig
│   ├── _template.zig
│   ├── bag.zig
│   ├── model.zig
│   └── player.zig
├── entityModel.zig
├── entitySystem
│   ├── _list.zig
│   ├── _template.zig
│   └── modelRenderer.zig
├── server
│   ├── Entity.zig
...
```

As you can see the word "entity" is repeated multiple times in many, many configurations (including inside files) either redundantly, or due to lack of a better name for what's inside, or because we need separate file for server, client and shared code. 
I argue we need a change.

From brief lecture of ECS related PRs, Issues, discussions and finally content of files I assume following requirements:
- we need dedicated client side ECS global state and API -> how do I manipulate entities from outside of ECS
- ~~we need dedicated server side ECS global state and API~~ (I am not sure we do?)
- we need components directory and some logic that handles components at runtime
- we need systems directory and some logic that handles systems at runtime
- we have some legacy things we have to get rid off

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
i think, that the fact, that you had to use an hyperbole in the title instead of finding an actual example, shows that this isn't really an Issue

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I've also noticed that it's a bit all over the place.

> we have some legacy things we have to get rid off

I think most of it comes down to this.

Let me address some of the files you mentioned:
- Both Entity.zig should be merged, and at the point it's likely small enough to not need its own file (see #3241)
- entity_managers are tracked in #3249, once all the special cases are gone the files themselves might be small and similar enough to consolidate them.
- entityComponent will be moved to mods, and should retain the entity in the name (and also it doesn't follow our naming convention for file names, we really need to modify the linter for that)
- [ ] entitySystem, should probably be renamed to just system, it honestly has not a single function that's specific to entities.
- entityModel - maybe it is time to introduce an assets folder
- [ ] entity.zig - I think it would make sense to centralize and if possible deduplicate a few more things there.

// ISSUE TITLE: Violent texture flickering // ISSUE REPORT: ## EDIT: Finding and workaround The issue is caused by missing synchronization between the opaque (which writes to the depth buffer) and the transparent draw call (which reads from but doesn't write to the depth buffer). Currently there is a `glTextureBarrier` there, but its documentation is somewhat vague and I suspect AMD's drivers may have interpreted it differently. Other synchronization attempts didn't work, apart from the brute-force `c.glFinish();`. This shouldn't be a problem when more synchronization options are available with Vulkan #102. The workaround is to add `c.glFinish();` next to the existing `c.glTextureBarrier();` call. ## original post ### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior The *OBSERVED BEHAVIOR* at the border of my render distance, in the fog, textures violently flicker to either, a) a bugged texture, b) Z fighting, c) stops existing. // ISSUE TITLE: Thin ice
// ISSUE REPORT:
- It breaks when you fall on it with enough velocity.
- The surface of water freezes into thin ice in freezing biomes. 
- It can be melted into water with heat sources.
- You can use it to craft regular ice, which cannot be melted (for building purposes)

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Might need some adjustments to the `onTouch` functions to allow capturing the velocity.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I have an idea on how this could be implemented: Fall damage you take can directly translate to block damage. If you fall really far onto some dirt, the dirt will break. Thin ice is like that, except since it's so brittle, it'll take less damage for it to break.

This is a stupid idea because of the amount of lag it would bring, but I thought I'd put it out there.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
No this is a good idea, and I don't think it would lag too much. We'd need #2938 for that though

// ISSUE TITLE: In-air controls are a bit too floaty (in certain situations) // ISSUE REPORT: ### Description Currently, the amount of control you have in the air is very low in comparison to what I'm used to, which quickly leads to a (likely) unintended side effect of falling down further than you intended, as outlined in this screenshot: Green showing the path the player wants to take if they press or even just tap forward to get off the block and land safely a few blocks below. Red showing what often happens instead, causing the player to move further horizontally than intended, potentially causing them to take fall damage or experience death. This issue could be fixed by either making in-air controls stronger in general, or perhaps only for a short time right after leaving the ground. // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Yeah, it would make sense to give you some kind of coyote time to adjust your jump/fall direction. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] a little more air control couldn't hurt, we just have to make sure the way we implement it doesn't make b-hopping the optimal way of travel. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] The problem is that (at least as far as I can tell) there are only two ways to increase air control: - Increase air friction, which makes you fall slower - Increase air mobility, which directly leads to b-hopping Furthermore both of these make ice less slippery, since your air movement gives you also more control on the ground. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I just had a eureka moment. Remember when you were making physics and whenever you landed, you slowed down? Why not have that? More air mobility, but when you land, your character slows down, just like dark souls :P [MAINTAINER COMMENT] That's also how it works in real life [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Can we make the jump be charged? So, how far you can jump would depend on how long you held spacebar, up to some reasonable limit. I think Ori and the Blind Forest has something like this, although it's a 2D platformer. Then you could allow full air mobility, because you simply wouldn't be able to squeeze jumps into running that easily. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] That would also be cool! We should definitely **test** both possibilities. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > Can we make the jump be charged? I know we haven't tested this yet, but this might not feel very good to play with considering how often you'll be jumping in this game. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > I just had a eureka moment. Remember when you were making physics and whenever you landed, you slowed down? Why not have that? More air mobility, but when you land, your character slows down, just like dark souls :P Also instead of having the character slow down, it might make more sense to have their walking speed slowed instead. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I made a prototype for my original idea in #2978 and it appears to work. Together with Argmaster's proposal to have jump height depend on how long you press the key, this should give enough control without increasing air control in other scenarios. I also tried to make a prototype for glider physics to adjust your direction mid-air but it didn't work out because the effect scales with speed, and the issue already appears at low-speeds. [USER COMMENT] > Can we make the jump be charged? So, how far you can jump would depend on how long you held spacebar, up to some reasonable limit. I think Ori and the Blind Forest has something like this, although it's a 2D platformer. Then you could allow full air mobility, because you simply wouldn't be able to squeeze jumps into running that easily. i like this idea a lot. and it makes sense to have to charge for a full jump, since you'd have to squat down to do it irl. actually, this would make animations for jumping a lot nicer, since you can actually have the character squat down/prepare to jump, making animations less "snappy". // ISSUE TITLE: Anvil
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Related: #1608

An anvil is an upgrade to the workbench that also crafts tools.

### The Upside:
Metal materials now have synergistic effects when placed next to each other, giving overall more efficient tools.

### The Downside:
**It can only use metal and gem materials,** meaning that unique effects like _"efficient against stone"_ and other misc. modifiers cannot be added to these tools.

hmm. what about wood? in real life you make the handle and head separately, then combine them together and polish from there. maybe something similar could work for cubyz?

Wood not being allowed in anvil crafting is a part of its downsides.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
It is a nice idea, though it also is different from #197
Maybe we can combine them somehow?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
>How would the new combinations be communicated to the player?

Good ol' faithful tooltip

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> It is a nice idea, though it also is different from https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/197
Maybe we can combine them somehow?

I think having the furnace be a requirement for metal crafting is a bit confusing since furnaces are already required _(well, already required in the future XD)_ to make the metal bars.

// ISSUE TITLE: Metal synergistic effects similar to encasing a gem
// ISSUE REPORT:
I think it'd be cool to see more metal synergistic effects.

As an example:

Copper next to silver could activate an effect in silver that makes it more durable.

In real life, sterling silver (Mostly silver with some copper) is more durable than just silver alone. 
Silver is soft, copper is added to make it less soft and so it's used in lots of jewelery.

Rather than create the alloy directly, we just apply an effect to the tool properties if a metal is next to another metal (like copper adjacent to silver) so players are further encouraged to encase a gem in silver but have copper on the outside for example.

I don't think this applies to every case where you use multiple metals in a tool, but, we can create specific synergies like silver and copper that would complement #2636

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
I think I'll implement this after #2648 is merged, unless you want to do it.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Idea: Have synergistic effects locked behind a different crafting station (an anvil)

So for example:
> **Iron**
> When crafting in an anvil and placing it next to copper, add 50 durability.

The caveat of the anvil is that you can ONLY use metals and gems, so no wood, branches, mushrooms, candy cane, etc. You can either use the workbench for more freedom and niche tools, or the anvil for more overall efficient tools.

// ISSUE TITLE: Landmarks
// ISSUE REPORT:
Our biomes need landmarks that break the monotony of repeating generic trees, rocks and plants and hills.
An example of a landmark that is already in game is triclops statues and meteorites, but we should also have natural landmarks, like broken thick trees, ruins, rock formations etc.
The main point of landmarks is that they are rare enough so when you see it, then leave a biome, then come back, you can be almost sure its the same landmark.
Next step would be to make the landmarks actually useful, but that's a separate challange.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
So, I've been thinking how to make the landmarks useful and I thought about Wither 3's "Places of Power". Basically it was a stone pillar with a glowing orb that you could draw magical power from. That temporarily boosted your spells and granted you an Ability Point to be spent in skill tree. Cubyz doesn't have a skill tree, but we will have effects. We could make something similar, where we would have a rune block that can be used to temporarily grant you one of the effects available in game. Whether it would be one time use and if the effects would be random is a separate matter, but in general that should make finding landmarks more exciting, as you could gain access to effects which are hard or impossible to gain otherwise.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Next step would be to make the landmarks actually useful, but that's a separate challange.

I don't think we should worry about that too much. Not every structure should have an extrinsic reward for the player. I think this should be mentioned in a separate issue.

Frost | slowly damages the player, decreases player speed including mouse sensitivity, it might also be worth trying to delay inputs by maybe 50-100 ms, cancels out with fire
Stun | stunned players cannot act for a short duration, button events are ignored, stun can be avoided by having a conductive armor, a cooldown or stun resistance effect might be useful to avoid stun locking
Root | rooted players cannot move, but they can still attack and look around

Enhanced Vision | counteracts blindness, allows seeing dark blocks (If that's not too expensive it would be nice to show unlit blocks in grayscale)
Poison | Slowly damages the player, counteracts all normal healing effects, prevents eating food

bleeding | take damage over time
slowness | slows the player
stomach ache | cannot eat

// ISSUE TITLE: Discussion: Multithreading in ECS
// ISSUE REPORT:
Let us discuss how we want to handle multithreading in the entityComponentSystem
My idea is to Let each Component and the EntityManager have Its own mutex, that has to be locked when interacting with it. 

**The question is, should component Pointer be given out?**

## Idea 1: The simple approach
Component pointers are given out only if the Mutex is locked. ( get(id) asserts mutex for entityManager is locked )
**benefits**
+ doesn't confuse your IDE

**downsides**
- someone might accidently use entityPointers after they have unlocked the entityManager (not fool proof)

example:
```zig
healthComponent.mutex.lock();
const component:*HealthComponent = healthComponent.get(id); //assert lock
component.health = 15;
healthComponent.mutex.unlock();
//Illegal / not fool proof when:
component.health=17;
```

## idea 2: The more complex approach
have get/set functions
```zig
component.set(id,.attribute,newvalue),
component.get(id,.attribute),
```

example:
```zig
healthComponent.mutex.lock();
healthComponent.set(id,.health,15); //assert lock
healthComponent.mutex.unlock();
healthComponent.set(id,.health,17); //assert lock, fails successfully! more fool proof.
```
which internally asserts the locks for the component, and set/gets an attribute for the component
At no point is an component pointer even given out.

**benefit**
- more fool proof. (100% fool proof is not reasonably possible, like for attributes of components that are pointers)
- We could integrate it with the network in the future: when component.set(..)  then component gets retransmitted to all users automatically, without having to explicity write it every time.

**downside:**
- confuses Intellisense
 
## Idea 3: 
_insert your idea here_

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I think ECS libs tend to treat components as values, thus require explicit get and set ops (like idea 2)
However they also differentiate between flag components and those which actually store data, for performance reasons.
They also do caching of those edit operations and they flush them only now and then to avoid moving entities between archetypes too often.
If you write the ops into the cache you can do this without mutex, just with atomic stack and lock for longer only on flush.
[Look at flecks](https://www.flecs.dev/flecs/)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
After reviewing this part of documentation of flecs https://www.flecs.dev/flecs/md_docs_2EntitiesComponents.html
I found that it technically also allows for obtaining mutable references, further research is needed, since I clearly have no idea what I am talking about 😅

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
My goal is to have a lock-free system, see #2420

For entities this would mean: All expensive (O(n²)) entity updates (except from component addition/removal) are done in parallel based on their position, everything else (cheap O(n) stuff, like checking if your health is negative, ticking down effect timers) and death/component removal is done sequentially on the server thread.

To make this safe, you can use `sync.threadContext.assertCorrectContext` (which is not fully fledged out yet).

With this we also won't even run into the problem you described above, since the thread will be in the correct context for as long as the function runs.

[USER COMMENT]
> With this we also won't even run into the problem you described above, 

Not really, problem 1 also includes:
```zig
healthComponent.mutex.lock();
const component:*HealthComponent = healthComponent.get(id); //assert lock
component.health = 15;
healthComponent.mutex.unlock();
//Illegal / not fool proof when:
somevariablesomewhereelse = component;
...
use in some other function of somevariablesomewhereelse
```

> My goal is to have a lock-free system, see https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/2420
> 
> For entities this would mean: All expensive (O(n²)) entity updates (except from component addition/removal) are done in parallel based on their position, everything else (cheap O(n) stuff, like checking if your health is negative, ticking down effect timers) and death/component removal is done sequentially on the server thread.

Can you expand on that?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Can you expand on that?

Which part is unclear?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Oh yeah let's pick up that issue for no reason!

```zig

healthComponent.mutex.lock();
const component:*HealthComponent = healthComponent.get(id); //assert lock
component.health = 15;
healthComponent.mutex.unlock();

```

In general I don't think that is how API should work;
IMO only valid way to update a component would be to explicitly set it via `set(newComponentValue)`.
I would assume that most of the components are small, ie. They fit in a single cache line. This means that cost of changing it is capped by memory speed. That would also quickly answer many questions of how many attributes should a component have - not many, four u64 to be exact. Enough for Vec3i, not enough for every weird attribute we might want to place inside.
Components shouldn't contain hash maps, nor lists. Most they can have is a pointer into data structure held by a System.
There is also a (arguably) good technical reason to do so; it allows ECS a fine grained control of what and when to cache and synchronize.
Most likely changes to the entity state should be cached and applied as a batch, eg. After system has done full iteration over all entities. This would guarantee that order of the iteration doesn't change the outcome.
I think it would reduce the risk of client desync and possibly number of packets send by networking layer, even though it has its own buffers (?).
That result can be achieved via left-right data structure, where we get two exact copies of the same thing, but we read left copy and write to right copy. Then we sync (copy contents of right to left) and swap. This means unobstructed reading and no cache invalidation (in ideal case).
In most cases systems will be checking flags or current values, not necessarily editing immediately. It should be much faster than sharing original data structure between threads.

// ISSUE TITLE: Song Playlist and Chances System // ISSUE REPORT: Instead of biomes having one looping song, reference a .json file that has a collection of music. These playlists can be referenced during events such as biome music, boss music, raid music, etc. Each song in the playlist has a specified chance of playing. If specified, the chance for a song to play can be set to zero if it's already been played. This resets after each consecutive song or the chance increases based on how many songs are in the playlist. Optionally, you can choose what song plays after another, called a "sub-song." These could be used as a nuanced way to bridge the different musical styles the game has. Again, optional! // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Instead of simple playlists I would propose a more complex system. I think the main problem with playlists is that you end up looking for a lot of playlists for many different combinations of biome type + day/night + hostility. Additionally it seems more difficult to add new music to an existing biome. That's why I instead propose a system based on keywords (tell me what you think about this): Each music track gets a list of keywords associated with it. These keywords could be for example "day", "night", "danger", "building", "grass", "sun", "desert", "water", "cave", ... You can add new keywords in each addon as well. Each biome also gets a list of keyword + weight (can be negative) pairs associated with it. Music is then selected as follows: For each music track the biome weight is determined by adding all the biome weights of the keywords that it has together. From the music tracks above a certain threshold (maybe the threshold would be biome specific as well?) a song is chosen randomly, maybe also based on a parametrisable chance. Some of these keywords can be tied to game functions. For example during the night, all songs that have the "day" keyword, but not the "night" keyword are automatically disabled (unless of course the given biome specifies the day keyword in which case it would always play day music). Addons could also easily define new keywords, to make sure that their music only plays in certain biomes. Basically the song selector, called "Moody" will choose a song depending on its mood, the current biome, and miscellaneous tags such as [Night], [Day], [Underwater], etc. I know it's dumb that I created an entire character to describe how a music selection system should work, but this is the best way I could put it. // ISSUE TITLE: Prevent repetitiveness of textures
// ISSUE REPORT:
Textures are fairly repetitive when seen on a flat surface, so it would be good to have some sort of randomness to them.

## (Some) Approaches
### Random texture rotation
This can be seen in minecraft, but it is limited to a small number of blocks, like stone(→ flat texture) or grass/sand(→ random texture).
Because of that it may be a good start, but doesn't work in all cases.
### Random texture variants
This can be seen on the cobblestone texture in the java version. While this definitely helps, the texture still looks somwhat repetitive, due to the fact that the borders of all texture variants need to match.
### Random white noise overlay
The idea would be to only apply a layer of white noise to the texture. This would be fairly cheap, but it would only work well for noisy textures like grass/dirt/sand.
### Fully procedural Textures (3D)
This approach can be seen in the [procedural_attempt](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/tree/procedural_attempt) branch or in [seperate repo](https://github.com/IntegratedQuantum/ProceduralTextureCreator) I made to experiment.
In these I used a few layers of simple noise, and as you can see that worked quite well for some blocks:
![Screenshot at 2022-12-08 22-07-04](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/assets/43880493/e55e864b-a62b-4454-8fb2-94080f201bca)
However it ultimately failed due to poor performance (an extra 4ms per frame) and because it was too limited for the crystal block and cobblestone textures which require more detail.
### Large 2D textures
Larger textures, such that still only 16×16 are drawn per block, would make it repeat at a larger scale.
However these textures would not match at the sides. (which hasn't bothered anyone in minecraft, so it could be a non-problem)
Additionally no artist would want to make giant textures by hand, so a procedural system is needed.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
#191 could fix this issue using "random" connected textures. This feature is basically a clone of "CTM" (Connected textures mod)

https://www.minecraftforum.net/forums/mapping-and-modding-java-edition/resource-packs/resource-pack-discussion/1256361-ctm-an-outdated-guide#ran

[USER COMMENT]
Another approach is stochastic texture sampling:
[High-Performance By-Example Noise using a Histogram-Preserving Blending Operator](https://eheitzresearch.wordpress.com/722-2/)
[Procedural Stochastic Textures by Tiling and Blending](https://eheitzresearch.wordpress.com/738-2/)
[On Histogram-Preserving Blending for Randomized Texture Tiling](https://jcgt.org/published/0008/04/02/)
I have implemented it into Luanti as an experiment: https://forum.luanti.org/viewtopic.php?t=30600

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
How expensive is this? Would this work with mipmapping and anisotropic filtering?

[USER COMMENT]
> How expensive is this? Would this work with mipmapping and anisotropic filtering?

This is mentioned in Procedural Stochastic Textures by Tiling and Blending.
Section 1.7 shows performance results:
> On average, it is 4-5 times costlier [compared to a classic texture repeat], which makes sense since we fetch the input 3 times, use one additional look-up table fetch and use a few additional operations.

It also works with mipmapping and anisotropic filtering with texture2DGrad() and some prefiltering, which is explained in section 1.5. I have not implemented this in my experiments.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
4-5 times more seems quite expensive, especially given that texture sampling is one of our bottlenecks currently.
I think the best approach may still be to support textures that span multiple blocks, and generate them outside of the engine where performance cost does not matter.

// ISSUE TITLE: Callback refactor/rework
// ISSUE REPORT:
Right now, each callback has rigid inputs and you can't do like, an `onTouch` event that triggers a `openWindow` callback on the client, for example.

I think these should be decoupled.

Right now I'm thinking something like:


block has events like:

onTouch, onTick, etc

Ideally each event can run basically any callback. So we want to generically store all the of relevant data such that any callback can be used.

A simple approach might be to have separate `run` functions for each "type" of callback we might want, which right now are limited such that we can only really run a single function for each event.

`clientRun`, `serverRun`, and `touchRun` just for example.

Each callback decides which `run` function it wants to use, which decides which inputs it needs.

So I think we accomplish this by changing `Callback.init` and the event name in the .zon (`onTouch`, `onInteract`, etc) so we additionally provide the function we want to run. Something that we can do polymorphically would be cool, maybe a vtable so you can move the callbacks into `mods/cubyz` and allow mods to provide their own implementations of functions?

Anyway, kinda a vague idea, but I think unifying the callbacks will open up a lot of possibilities for even just base addons.

TL;DR: If a BlockCallback is run (maybe rename that to an event?), it should give all data you could possibly need for any function (server, client, ontouch), and then the callback can independently decide which of the functions it wants to run.

// ISSUE TITLE: Vulkan
// ISSUE REPORT:
While it probably won't make the game any faster since it's not API bound, I think Vulkan does offer some advantages:
- better tooling (there is a GPU profiler from AMD that only works with Vulkan)
- better platform support: with MoltenVK there is apple support. And Vulkan also supports mobile GPUs, which at the moment would require OpenGL ES
- better error detection with validation layers. OpenGL is really inconsistent with errors and warnings, which appear to be driver dependant.
- Vulkan does provide more consistent performance. For example on my intel laptop the OpenGL driver creates a copy of a given buffer when doing glBufferSubData.
- Vulkan is probably more future-proof at this point.
- Vulkan allows for larger buffers (SSBOs can be up to 4GB instead of 2 GB like in OpenGL)

But all that comes at a heavy development cost.

I heard that this guide should be useful: https://vkguide.dev
There is an official documentation now: https://docs.vulkan.org/spec/latest/index.html
Here are some examples, which might be helpful to look at: https://github.com/SaschaWillems/Vulkan/tree/master
Here is a Zig project that I'd like to reference for all the Vulkan infrastructure, since it doesn't use the SDK: https://codeberg.org/ssmid/zeppelin

Prerequisites:
- #101 would make porting easier

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
There is also https://vulkan.gpuinfo.org/ page which is very handy for querying for how widely are different Vulkan features available.

[USER COMMENT]
Question, I did see the use of glad for loading the vulkan function pointers and providing the headers. Was the following project ever considered https://github.com/Snektron/vulkan-zig? to do that instead? It has some large advantages over a c library like glad. 
- Provides a type safe zig interface (packed structs instead of flags, enums as handles instead of opaque ptr's)
- Provides an error set per function making it very clear what errors can be thrown by what functions
- Provides some convenience enumerate functions that return an allocated slice.

I have used a custom fork of this library that worked very well for my cases. It removed the proxies it provided, I preferred keeping the vulkan code more to the original c api, functions can be called like try vk.destroyInstance() instead of when using a proxy call like instance.destroyInstance().

It would mean replacing glad third party with vulkan-zig as third party. If you don't like having another lib you could just generate the bindings once and include it directly in the project source. (If going this route you can always fork the generator so you are in control over the code.) 

If no objections I could provide a pr updating the current vulkan code. This seems like a great project and I would like to contribute to it. 

If this is the wrong place to ask these kind of questions please let me know.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
C libraries have a great advantage over zig counterparts - they have a stable API. Zig changes dynamically and Quantum tries his best to keep up with this, but he doesn't have the time to understand and maintain someone's else's codebase if they happen to give up doing so.
Additionally this adds cost of having to understand abstraction someone invented on top of Vulkan. Using plain C API means you can refer to majority of existing online resources. Further you go from it, the harder it will become to translate one into the other.

[USER COMMENT]
I do get the stable API part though in this case I'm note sure it really applies here. The language constructs used in the library are quite basic. I don't see those change any time soon. 

The abstraction as well is absolutely minimal and basically the same as with glad. The library generates it's bindings from the vk.xml same as with glad, the only difference here is glad does it in c and vulkan-zig in zig, making use of some zig constructs to make it more type safe. if you would target for example vk 1.3 you would just need to generate the bindings once and they should work forever. 

Here a sample of the generated code for createInstance:
```zig  
pub const CreateInstanceError = error{
    OutOfHostMemory,
    OutOfDeviceMemory,
    InitializationFailed,
    LayerNotPresent,
    ExtensionNotPresent,
    IncompatibleDriver,
    ValidationFailed,
    Unknown,
};
pub fn createInstance(
    p_create_info: *const InstanceCreateInfo,
    p_allocator: ?*const AllocationCallbacks,
) CreateInstanceError!Instance {
    var out_instance: Instance = undefined;
    const result = base_dispatch.vkCreateInstance.?(
        p_create_info,
        p_allocator,
        &out_instance,
    );
    switch (result) {
        Result.success => {},
        Result.error_out_of_host_memory => return error.OutOfHostMemory,
        Result.error_out_of_device_memory => return error.OutOfDeviceMemory,
        Result.error_initialization_failed => return error.InitializationFailed,
        Result.error_layer_not_present => return error.LayerNotPresent,
        Result.error_extension_not_present => return error.ExtensionNotPresent,
        Result.error_incompatible_driver => return error.IncompatibleDriver,
        Result.error_unknown => return error.Unknown,
        Result.error_validation_failed => return error.ValidationFailed,
        else => return error.Unknown,
    }
    return out_instance;
}
```
It maps almost 1:1 to the normal vulkan functions instead of using an out parameter and return error code it returns error or result. I don't think any of these used zig language constructs will change any time soon?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> and vulkan-zig in zig, making use of some zig constructs to make it more type safe.

This part is exactly the problem. All these wrapper libraries make small changes throughout the API making it more difficult to use with the official documentation and tutorials. The renaming of things (like c.vkCreateInstance to vk.createInstance) alone is already making this more difficult for no good reason.

[USER COMMENT]
I don't think it is a good idea to replace opengl for vulkan, you will run into a lot of bugs in vulkan. 

For example GTK4 now has a vulkan renderer, it is full of bugs and people often have to set `GSK_RENDERER=gl` to fix those issues.

The vulkan renderer was also totall broken with intel gpus (even the latest gpus) and mesa had to [fix it with a workaround](https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/mesa/mesa/-/merge_requests/38373)

At pkgforge-dev we also experimented with shipping a trimmed down version of mesa with only zink as the opengl driver, but that went nowhere because on some systems you have to set [`LIBGL_KOPPER_DRI2=1` for zink to work](https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/mesa/mesa/-/work_items/13929). I also had to [bring back nouveau GL](https://github.com/pkgforge-dev/Anylinux-AppImages/issues/527#issuecomment-4301538906) because the performance of zink is bad on old nvidia gpus to the point that it is noticeable on simple GUI apps.

// ISSUE TITLE: Optionally use `ARB_fragment_shader_interlock` extension for volumetric fog when the device supports it
// ISSUE REPORT:
On Vulkan #102 this extension is available on more devices, including AMD. So I think it makes sense to support this, at least as an option.

There should also be a button to allow disabling it.

Old issue text:
This extension seems to be widely available on intel and nvidia hardware. (sadly no support on AMD)
There are some potential performance improvements from this (it would allow using an f11/f10 framebuffer), which could be especially helpful on the (generally weak) intel iGPUs. Furthermore it would also allow some visual improvements (support for denser fogs, less graphical glitches and maybe even lighting)

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I implemented a prototype in the impossible_fog branch.
Functionally it works well and fixes the annoying problems of volumetric fog:
- no more artifacts when looking at the fog backface (this also fixes the blooming pixel errors)
- no more artifacts when the fog strength or fog distance get too large

I still need to check the performance impact and try out lighting.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I have investigated the performance impact of this, and while the f11/f10 framebuffer does increase performance by ~600 µs on my intel iGPU test device, it is weight up by the ~500 µs cost of copying the depth buffer.
Additionally there is an ~5-10% performance improvement in the transparent rendering step. However this does not matter much in the grand scheme of things.

Because it makes only such a small difference, I have decided that ti's not worth the extra code complexity for now. Maybe in the future, once more devices (most importantly AMD) were to support this extension, it might be worth another look, due to fixing some of the visual issues/limitations of fog.

// ISSUE TITLE: Final Moffalo Behavior
// ISSUE REPORT:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/04723d5d-2632-4f12-8bbd-be3ddc6214d3)

**Behavior is as follows:**

- Moffalo spawn in groups of 3 to 6, where the oldest is declared the leader. Younger Moffalo will copy the behavior of the leader.
- If the leader Moffalo dies, a new leader is elected based on whoever is the oldest.
- If the player approaches a herd of Moffalo aggressively (sprinting/making a lot of noise), they will send a signal to other nearby Moffalo to run away, sometimes splitting off into multiple groups.
- If the Moffalo is attacked or sheared, they become aggressive and try to stampede over the player.
- If it is night, the Moffalo will sleep together. The player can shear the Moffalo while they are asleep, but they must make sure not to make noise or provoke the creature.
- Idle behavior includes self-grooming, sitting, stomping, playing, and frolicking.

**Hunger:**

- Moffalo will graze, eating any grass vegetation it can find (may require #1409)
- If the Moffalo cannot get enough food, it will convert to a "hungry" state, in which it will not grow back wool or give meat when killed.
- After the hungry state, it will become starved, then die.

// ISSUE TITLE: Ziplines // ISSUE REPORT: ### Description ## Foreword The main idea behind the Zipline is, as a function, a simple and (mostly) permanent way of traversing from a Point A to a Point B, and looking cool while doing so. ## How it Works (Gameplay) ### The Items Getting a bit more into the specifics of things, the two main items you need is Hooks and Cables. Hooks would be a Metal Block that can Hold the cables (these hooks can probably also be used for ropes #1966 among other things) The Cables are where we get into something more interesting. A Zipline Cable, crafted with Metal (probably 1 ingot for 8, though can be experimented with), are where the main usage of the Zipline comes into play. These are placed between the Hooks in order to form the Zipline. This could hypothetically be expanded upon as well, wherein Different Metals make Different Types of Cables (differing visuals, friction, length, etc.) ### The Build How it works is as follows: First, place Hooks at the start and end of where you want your zipline to be. Second, use a Zipline Cable on the first hook, and then on the second hook. The amount of Cable needed to form the zipline will be used from your inventory, and *voila,* you have a Zipline! The amount of cable used from your inventory will be based on the Length that the zipline will cover between point A and B, up to a certain Max Distance. Therefore, the shorter your zipline is, the less Cable items it will cost to make. But what if you wanted to go Longer than the maximum? In that case, if you set up a *Third* hook, and connect your Second hook to it like described above, you can create a *Second* Zipline, thus extending the total length of your Zipline by Daisy-Chaining both of them together. This can then be extended as much as you want! ### The Use The general idea for how to ride the zipline is that you'd interact with the line where you want to get on, and then you start to ride from that spot. The exact method required I'm not entirely certain on, so I have a Few Ideas for how this could be handled: **Bare Hands:** This would be the easiest for the player, as they won't need any prerequisites to ride the Zipline after it's set up, as you'd be able to ride it regardless of the Player's Equipment. However, this could potentially lead to scenarios where the player accidentally gets on when they otherwise don't want to (such as trying to interact with something else). It's also not the most realistic/comfortable solution either. **An Existing Tool:** This would help with clutter, as it would just re-use existing tools like the Pickaxe and such, but still give some *intent* behind a player choosing to get on. Issues being that the player would need to have a tool to begin with (albeit this is very a likely scenario), and the aforementioned Accidental Mounting of a zipline (though less likely than otherwise.) **A Dedicated Tool:** Having a specific tool dedicated to riding ziplines would solve the intent problem outright, as the only reason you'd need to use the tool is *if* you want to ride the Zipline. Alternatively this could be an Equip-able Accessory (see #604) if taking up a hotbar slot is undesirable. The main issue with this approach is that creating "Dedicated for the Task" items is a fast track to Inventory Clutter. Furthermore, we don't need to limit the ziplines to being ridden with a single tool. As discord user "iNiKKo" suggested, we could attach Mechanized Carts to the zipline for more automated travel. And you could even use them purely for Decorative purposes, such as Rope Fencing and Powerlines! ## How it Works (Mechanically) I have some theory as to how this would work out Programming wise (granted, I am myself not a programmer, so this could be easier said than done for all I know), so I'll lay those out as well ### Rendering/Internal Structure In terms of the Behind the Scenes of it all, the structure of the Zipline would lie Entirely in the cable itself: It would ideally be a 3D Line Curve (or if we wanted to be cheap, just a flat 2 Point line) between the two points chosen via the Hooks, likely as some form of Lightweight Entity. The hooks wouldn't need to hold any data either, as they just serve as Valid Locations to place the cable on. Think something along the lines of [the Leads from Minecraft](https://minecraft.wiki/w/Lead) (Java, the Bedrock ones have physics which is frankly too much for this case) In terms of LODs you could reduce the amount of points in the curve on near levels (such as 0.5 or 1), and beyond that just Stop rendering the zipline altogether (it'd realistically be too small to see at that point anyways) ### Speed Calculation Seeing as the Zipline is just a 3D Vector Curve, the speed should (hypothetically) be easy to calculate by taking the Angle of the curve and applying math based on it (Relative angle to gravity, account for Air Resistance and Friction, etc.). In essence, the more "downward" the angle is, the more speed you get from the Zipline. Therefore you can't ride a zipline going Up (at least not without some alternative method, such as the Cart or Otherwise). ## Conclusion Realistically speaking, I know that not everything I say here is going to make it in exactly as written, but I truly believe that this would be a great fit for Cubyz given it's emphasis on Exploration and Scale. // DISCUSSION: [USER COMMENT] i being able to still use both hands and a tool for riding the zipline would be great, but when using hands you would be much slower and it would grant you an ability to get up a rope [USER COMMENT] Bare hands: It would go slow and damages you. Stick: You could right-click using a branch and it would allow you to ride the zipline but it would "break a.k.a dissapear" after a few seconds. Tool(All tools): The animation would be the Snale using the handle of the tools to ride the zipline but it would cost durability. [USER COMMENT] It should be possible to implement the zipline rendering with the systems that we already have, however actually moving with the zipline is more complicated. Either way I think this is something we should figure out once we get there and have an idea of whether or not that would make sense balancing-wise. // ISSUE TITLE: Fluid/Gas System
// ISSUE REPORT:
Fluids and gases should be stored and simulated outside the normal voxel volume.

## Goals
- heavy liquid should flow downwards
- light gases should flow upwards
- biomes should have an associated liquid/fog block. Whenever a block in that biome is broken, the gap must get filled with the right fog/fluid block
- Fluids/Gases should exist in seperate memory to allow for underwater fences and similar
- Fluids should not propagate endlessly, flooding the entire world

## Relaxations
- fluid volume does not need to be preserved
- the flow does not need to be realistic
- big bodies of water like the ocean can be assumed as infinite, as long as they don't flood the entire underground

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We cannot mix gases like on the left, since this will create an exponential amount of gas types.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

[USER COMMENT]
We could write gases as vectors (smoke, cloud, poison, ...) as long as we
- control their proliferation (unlike rotation modes)
- decide their color in O(d) time/space using maths instead of lookup tables

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Mixed gases are also problematic since every border between different gas color/density needs a transparent face, which makes every biome with mixed gases a lag biome.

[USER COMMENT]
Then we could have fluid glob together at a uniform density to reduce the number of faces to render. If they glob independently then we only have to deal with O(liquid dimensions) faces. We could also assign several levels of fluid to the same transparency to have the same dynamics without adding extra faces.

// ISSUE TITLE: Crop Mechanics
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Putting this here so it can be discussed!

----------------------
### The following describes a generic crop.

Growth:
- The crop has many stages of growth. It has a random chance to grow every minute (chance is dependent on soil)
- It has a chance of sucking moisture from the soil it's planted on every minute (chance is dependent on crop)
- The crop will only grow during the day and needs sunlight.
- The crop can grow under glass (so greenhouses can exist!)

Soil Mechanics:
- The crop can be planted on any soil type.
- The crop can still suck moisture after maturing.
- You cannot replace the soil without breaking the crop.

Soil Types:
- Mud: Most efficient soil. Transforms into Soil.
- Soil: Medium efficiency soil. Transforms into Dirt.
- Dirt. Low efficiency soil. Allows crops to wither.
- Clay: Medium efficiency soil. Allows crops to wither. It will wither faster due to its efficiency.
- Aerosoil: Low efficiency soil. Allows growth without sunlight. Allows crops to wither.
- Permafrost: Very low efficiency. Allows crops to wither.

Withering Mechanics:
- If a mature crop is on a soil block that allows withering, the next time it grows, it will begin to visibly wilt.
- There are multiple stages of wilting. Only the final few stages will prevent a successful harvest.

Harvesting:
- Crops can be harvested slowly by hand, or efficiently by sickle. (Or you can break the block under it by shover)
- Mature crops will yield 2-3 crops and 0-2 seeds.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Hmm, I think with these kinds of things simpler is better, we are not vintage story after all.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
What do you mean? This *is* simple! Vintage story has you watering crops and stuff and checking on values. This system would be purely visual and easily understandable to the player.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Plus, all crops don't have to work this way. This is just an example of one crop.

I think the chance-based whithering is what makes it too complicated in my opinion. I think it would be best if crops just don't do anything when on dry soil, would also safe them if you go on a long trip and can't water them.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
I put it in there to keep the system from being "mindlessly plant crops here and then go away."

I think we should still have withering, but I can propose a different way of having them wither.

// ISSUE TITLE: Add ZON notation for block data
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

As an extension to https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/pull/2773 
It would be convenient if we were to establish a generalized way to write block data in human readable way.
For ores it is enough just to write the id of the host block, but for other rotation modes it is not that simple.
Defining the data as a number, binary or not, is awkward and hard to read without cross-referencing with rotation implementation or seeing it in the game.

Therefore I propose to add a ZON based notation for block data looking similar to this example:

```
cubyz:oak_fence:.{.isConnectedNegX = true, .isConnectedNegY=True}
```

Mind that if we allow arbitrary whitespace in such block data definitions, certain parsing tasks suddenly become way less trivial, as we no longer can rely on just splitting on whitespace.

Notation like this could be also used for block entities to define eg. chest content or loot table IDs in world edit commands.

Other pitfall coming with that notation is key stability. Since hash map notation is proposed and hash maps don't care about order of keys, we need to make sure that we consistently resolve notations with reordered keys into same block data, same palette entries, etc.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Well zon allows you to omit spaces, so I think it would not be a big issue.
Later, with the command argument parser, we can probably add support for parenthesis.

However what concerns me more is how verbose zon is. e.g. take the thin birch biome, which defines branch blocks for the trees as
`cubyz:branch/birch:0b11`
I'd rather not write
`cubyz:branch/birch:.{.isConnectedNegZ=true,.isConnectedPosZ=true}`
Instead I'd propose maybe comma separated short values:
`cubyz:branch/birch:+z,-z`, or `cubyz:branch/birch:up,down`
These could then be easily parsed as enums.

More examples:
`cubyz:sign/oak:up180°`
`cubyz:sign/oak:down135°`
`cubyz:sign/oak:+x`
Ores and piles could stay as they are right now, with block id/number.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Notation like this could be also used for block entities

As for block entities, in my opinion they should always be specified separately, since they are separate from the rotation modes entirely.

// ISSUE TITLE: add more command Target options
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

With #2661 
We got inside `_command.zig` a Target struct which commands can then use to Target either the caller of the command or other players via the `@<playerIndex>` syntax. 

Here is the question: do we want to add more options, if yes which and how?

My ideas:

- force (would be able to target players who are not online) (maybe add a `!` at the start of the other options like `!@<playerIndex>`)
- group (would target permission groups #2587 )  
- all (would target all players)

this would mean that we would have to expand the Target struct to the needed functions which can be applied to users, so commands should probably be able to specify which options they support. 
(for example: `/tp` doesn't support `group` as you cannot teleport to multiple people)

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
Versatile commands are a big reason for why I like minecraft. In minecraft there where many cool things that I did in my worlds with the help of commands, that simply would not have been possible otherwise (modding excluded). Besides that I would commonly kill all of a certain type of entity with commands. Usually that would be items.  
Due to that I think that more targeting options should be added. How? Well if we you don't want to just rip off minecraft

## I'd do it like this

`@` is already a selector for players, so let's keep it that way.  
`&` for selecting entities (including players) (works like `@`).  
`$` for members of a group (possibly a permission group, but something that entities can optionally be assigned to without side effects)  
If no number comes after the selector symbol, then select everything (for `@` all players and `#` all entities)  
`!` to include offline players sounds great, if someone is willing to implement that. Sadly the same can't realistically be done with entities.  
`?` can optionally be appended after a selector symbol with or without id to filter through all the entities based on a number of filters which are written after the question mark as a comma seperated list. (basically ripping off minecraft anyways, but at least it isn't 1:1) 
 

## Example

To select all entities in a range of 5 blocks:  
`&?r=5`

To select every loaded entity and offline players:
`!&`

To select every entity between two points:
`&?x=20,dx=10,y=200,dy=3,z=50,dz=20`

To commit a genocide on all citizens of second class in your "how to be an evil dictator" server:
`/kill !$secondclass`

As you can probably tell that is not particularly pretty and might not be what you want, but I hope reading this was useful to you anyways.

[USER COMMENT]
remember that a few symbols are already blocked because of formatting (most prominent #, *, __, ~~ and §)

[USER COMMENT]
Oops, welp gotta replace `#` with `&` (or one of these `%°:` I wouldn't use `%` because windows uses that for variables and it could be confusing). Even less pretty now, but there aren't that many special characters to choose from.

[USER COMMENT]
> I wouldn't use % because windows uses that for variables and it could be confusing

Never mind, that's stupid. `&` is even more confusing. This is trivial to change now that nothing has been implemented yet anyways, so just choose what you think looks best, if you even want to do it this way.

In this state, the player is drowning, and their maximum health begins to reduce (I'd say at least a debuff should be applied to the mining and attack speed, but it's better not). **Suffocation**
- A generic effect applied to players who don't have oxygen.

// ISSUE TITLE: Key modifier revamp
// ISSUE REPORT:
The Modifier code in the Key struct is a bit rigid right now.

I think we can clean it up a bit.

1. Remove all the manually added possible modifier key booleans
2. Replace Modifiers struct with tags
3. Whenever we need a new category of modifier key, we add a new statically declared Tag in tag.zig.

How this'll work:

When declaring a keybind which itself is a modifier, we provide a `.tag` to declare which modifier it is.
When declaring a keybind that requires a modifier, we provide a `.requiredModifiers` array of Tags.

Any place where we currently check GLFW's mods via the Key's pressed modifiers, we instead check `.modById(<tag>).?.pressed`. (Need to ideally find a cleaner way to reference that modifier keybind than using an assert before `.?`)

The Tag system would allow for an easy pathway to add new modifier keybinds, which we can give sensible defaults like Left Shift, Left Control, Caps Lock, etc, but can be changed by the player without making it just a single modifier that's rebindable. Then, any place we want to use that as a modifier for an action, we call `.modById` to check its pressed status.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]

[USER COMMENT]

Then we check for a Tagged Keybind's `.pressed` instead of using the GLFW mods stuff.

Maybe loop over them at start, and when keybinds are changed to build a tag -> bool hashmap stored somewhere accessible to all keys. 

When toggled, the boolean is set to true. Then the actions that depend on modifiers can use that globally accessible hashmap to check the tags they're set to look for when performing the action

// ISSUE TITLE: [discussion] On climbing mechanics // ISSUE REPORT: ### Description I figured I'd create this issue to discuss how climbing should work mechanically, and potential implementations (and limitations). There was some conversation in the Discord but I don't feel comfortable quoting or summarizing anyone else's input. Though here's my thoughts: ## Essentials - While climbing, keep the player in place (unaffected by gravity) - Outside forces should still affect the player (like pushback from an explosion) - Press `jump` to go up, `sneak` to go down (obviously) ### Free climbing (Minecraft style) - While within reach of a climbable block, there's two options on how to start climbing: - You automatically start as soon as you're off the ground. - You have to hold `jump` or `sneak`. - In theory this could be made configurable? - You can freely move side-to-side, back and forth, and of course up and down. - If climbing is automatic, you can only "let go" by moving out of the reach of any climbable blocks. - If climbing is manual, in theory there could be a control for letting go. ### Lock-on climbing - Interact with a climbable block to lock onto it and start climbing. - It's possible to lock the player into moving only on one axis or two. - Limited movement to the left or right could be possible, including to neighboring climbable blocks of the same type? - Interacting with another climbable block could let you "jump" to it. (limited reach distance) - Interaction, like attacking, placing or breaking blocks, could be limited to while you're stationary. - Easier to add dedicated animations for? This mechanic could potentially be more easily expanded to non-block climbing mechanics, such as hanging ropes/chains/vines, or moving entities, because the engine has an easier time tracking what you're grabbing onto. ### Ledges Especially in a vertical-heavy exploration setting I believe this is a great addition: The ability to climb 2 and 3 high ledges without resorting to breaking or placing blocks. The further you are from pulling yourself up, the slower the climbing process is. It should be faster to place ladders than to pull yourself up 3 block ledges repeatedly. In free climbing this may be achieved by holding `jump` and pushing into a wall. Perhaps appropriate blocks could simply be treated as climbable themselves (assuming there is a ledge to "grab" in reach). The downside is climbing state would be immediately exited when you stop holding `jump` or looking away. In lock-on climbing, maybe you could interact with the edge of a block itself. Maybe this can only be done with an empty hand. It would disable other interactions until you stop climbing. One upside here is that you could hold onto ledges without continued holding of the `jump` key. Pressing `sneak` would let go of the ledge. ## Related issues Original feature request: #1031 Existing prior work: #1266 (closed) Said issue mentions #1728 being related // DISCUSSION: [USER COMMENT] I kinda like the idea of manual climbing, maybe using mouse buttons as a control. e.g. alternate between left and right mouse buttons at a specific interval to climb, the more accurate you are the faster you climb. [USER COMMENT] @SnekProgrammer That sounds like an accessibility nightmare, like for people with RSI (Repetitive Strain Injury). [USER COMMENT] I would _love_ to have a ledge grab, I hate seeing stacks of random junk blocks along the sides of what would have been a pretty mountain. [USER COMMENT] Ledge grabbing sounds fun. Maybe it could be gated by food, low food slower grabs, above that to full normal grab speed, and if a higher health than base is implemented (or already exists?) it could be increased to a fast speed. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Here's what I propose. It's best to keep the control scheme simple. [USER COMMENT] I am definitely for a better climbing system. It can be gated behind a type of boots or something with it being tiered like tools are right now where you can use different amounts of different materials to get faster climbing speeds. It would make mountains go from a nuisance to something you could reliably base on. Another idea that could mitigate some of the worst of the issues is an elevator of some type. Something that gives entities such as players anti gravity for a few seconds, wind, or any number of things.

Before: After: Test Addon: [replacetest.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25833353/replacetest.zip) // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] replaceBlock is doing what it's intended to do. // ISSUE TITLE: Placing torches on partial blocks
// ISSUE REPORT:
imagine being able to place torches on partial blocks like branches, slabs, fence post sides, wall sides and basically all other partial blocks. This could be done by offsetting the model into a neighboring block.

Note that, with 16 bits of rotation data and 5 sides, we can only afford 2 bits for each side (which would be enough to special case on the cases listed above).
However if we give up on the ideal of being able to place multiple torches in one block, we could easily afford the 4 bits needed for just one side of offsets.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
Is there any way to do this during mesh generation instead of storing it in rotation modes?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Yeah, maybe, but I think it may hurt meshing performance, particularly the fact that with it, faces inside the model would then have a chance to depend on the neighbor chunk whereas currently only the faces that are exactly on the border do.

[USER COMMENT]
> faces inside the model would then have a chance to depend on the neighbor chunk

In what way would this be a problem, wouldn't we have all of the needed chunks loaded already?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Yes, of course we have all the neighbors-
But right now the neighbor chunk code of mesh generation only has to check a very thin slice (roughly 1/32th of all faces) of the mesh and despite that it's already taking up a significant portion of meshing time. With your proposal it would need to check the entire block and not just the thin surface of it. In total that would be a little under 6/32th of the faces of the mesh.
A lot of the optimizations are targeting the bulk volume, but if so much now is in the border region, then it may be a lot slower in the end.

[USER COMMENT]
> With your proposal it would need to check the entire block and not just the thin surface of it.

Could it not just check the block bounding box? I don't see why it would need to be that accurate.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The check itself is if we optimize it at most checking a bool, and then going to cold code, no one cares about this one check.
My point is that the whole block (and with it an entire plane of blocks) now potentially depends on the neighbor chunk and thus needs to be moved to the part of the meshing code that handles neighbor-chunk interactions, since it could need to be updated when the neighbor chunk changes (this includes when it first loads)
