// ISSUE TITLE: execute command
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

/execute <player> <command> command parameters.

Would allow server owners / moderators to execute commands as a player (example Set the playerspawn or teleport them) 

Other way would it to have a optional Argument at the start of every command to execute it as another player

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We should keep things as straightforward as possible to avoid needless possibilities for moderator abuse.
If the moderator can execute any command as a player, then that would include e.g. impersonating them using private message commands once we add them.

Instead I think these should be separate commands or subcommands with separate permissions.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Administrators can do that regardless just by editing server code. What you are trying to achieve is [security through obscurity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Security_through_obscurity), it won't work on a open source project.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Administrators can do that regardless just by editing server code.

The main administrator can do that, but moderators will not be allowed to edit the server code. (unless you give them ssh access to your server, but then it really is your own fault)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Moderators can have limited command permissions that would exclude access to `/execute`.
Execute command can be useful in debugging context, to control all players on the server from one game window.

// ISSUE TITLE: Identification Mechanic
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

**Identification explained:**
- Some/most loot found in chests will be "Unidentified," meaning their stats are completely hidden from the player. This can include tools, accessories, foods, or potions (just as examples.)
- A player can use a "Scroll of Identification" to identify the loot, revealing its stats to only that player.

**Advantages of this mechanic:**
- Identification is per-player, meaning that if an item is identified by player 1, then given to player 2, player 2 will still see it as "Unidentified." This can lead to scenarios of trust-building among players, or potentially trolling and scamming. Either way, it would lead to fun outcomes!
- An unidentified potion may have negative or positive effects, but can still be used. This introduces risk/reward to looting while also giving the player the option to negate the risk at the cost of a resource.
- Scrolls of Identification themselves can be placed as loot in chests.

This mechanic will likely need much testing before being added, hence why I've given it the experimental tag.

// DISCUSSION:

items could then have their own `permissionPath` like "/items/[tool/item/etc]/identification/[unique_id]" 
A player who uses the Scroll of Identification would then just get this `permissionPath` added to its permission whitelist

// ISSUE TITLE: Require TLS certificates for the server (when joining through a domain name)
// ISSUE REPORT:
While #1737 does prevent a man in the middle from stealing an existing account after you joined the server once, there is still the possibility of taking over your account when you join for the first time.
This could be fixed by properly checking the server certificate using TLS.

Joining by direct IP should of course always remain without the certificate check.

We can assume that it's easy enough to get the certificate from an existing certificate authority for the server owner (see comment below).

<details>
<summary>Old text</summary>

This would require either relying on the existing certificate authority infrastructure (how easy is it to get a certificate nowadays?), or making our own certificate authority (since we control the application this is actually a valid option).
Making our own certificate authority could have certain advantages, since we have control over who gets a certificate (→we could effectively ban servers that misbehave, that could also be seen as a disadvantage though), and how much they pay for it(→this could be a potential revenue source if the project grows).

</details>

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> This would require either relying on the existing certificate authority infrastructure (how easy is it to get a certificate nowadays?),

It's trivial: https://letsencrypt.org/

> Making our own certificate authority could have certain advantages, since we have control over who gets a certificate (→we could effectively ban servers that misbehave, that could also be seen as a disadvantage though), 

It's open source project, people will just edit in a different one.

> and how much they pay for it(→this could be a potential revenue source if the project grows).

It's open source prohect, people will just edit in a different one.

The point of TLS is not to ensure that the server is safe in a literal "not a bad party" meaning, but that the communication is safe from interception.

Also, considering development of government regulations of what should be blocked and what not we should make it as impossible as possible to block servers. Otherwise you will end up with a bunch of lawyers telling you that you must ban x and y server right now.

That last part suggests that we should support adding custom authorities to bypass potential bans in the existing infrastructure. 

Free as in speech, not free as in beer.

[USER COMMENT]
> Joining by direct IP should of course always remain without the certificate check.

I know that WebTransport gets around this by requiring a certificate digest upon connection, that is used for verifying that the server you connected to is indeed the one you intended to, in cases where a domain certificate is not available. So something similar could be considered for Cubyz.

It does require sharing and entering the digest, which is much like a long, randomly generated password, so its no good for entering manually. (If you can assume everyone has access to copy-paste it's not a huge problem.) If a centralized server browser will ever be considered, (assuming its authority is verified,) it can automatically tell the client the digest, even if a separate (easy-to-remember) password is required to connect/register with that server.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Well, but does it matter, if you connect to an IP you are likely playing with friends on a self-hosted server. What's the worst thing man in the middle can do? Have you join the self-hosted server with another account and listen in? Send you to another server without the ability to listen to anything?
This is a game, I don't think either of these are in any way critical.

Now for servers with hostnames, which may be competitive, it certainly would make sense to check certificates since a man in the middle could have you join with another account and then later take over that account.

[USER COMMENT]
There is the case of hosting a public server, with a domain that has an A, AAAA and/or SRV record, that does not have the ability to set up (and maintain) a certificate linked to that domain. Having the self-signed certificate with the option to provide a digest on connect to verify would be a meaningful fallback here.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Why can't you get a certificate for these?

[USER COMMENT]
There's cases where you might be renting a server from a hosting provider and for whatever reason the provider does not want to bother. (I'm also not sure how SRV records interact with domain certificates.)

It would improve security by default and it would still encourage server owners to set up domain certs, if they wanted to have a smoother experience (not requiring entering the certificate digest on connect). And anyone who doesn't want to bother with the extra work, but still wants people to be able to connect via a domain gets the same security guarantees. Isn't that a win-win-win?

This could (in-theory) encourage players to create paths & infrastructure or seek out better items for movement to avoid these. - It serves as an early-game lesson for the player to pay attention to their surroundings, as it doesn't do enough damage to kill them outright. - Would look great in a lot of biomes! **Proposed appearance:** // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Ooh, a barbed wire shape might look great. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I think there are two important differences between Terraria and Cubyz that make it hard to apply here directly: - Cubyz is 3D which makes it significantly easier to navigate around these obstacles, whereas in Terraria, especially in the early game, there is no quick way around them. - Blocks are bigger on screen, part of why they work in Terraria is because it's easy to miss them, especially when you are focused on fighting. // ISSUE TITLE: Cave Layers
// ISSUE REPORT:
(I updated this issue to be closer to what we are currently planning/imagining, you can scroll down for the original issue)

## Surface Caves _(0)_
This includes your surface-biome-based caves (#556), such as:
- Sandstone caves
- Glacite caves
- Jungle caves
- Flooded caves

In more temperate biomes (forest, grasslands) you will find stone caves with pools of water and some plant life: more small mushrooms, vines, mosses, etc. 

## Standard Caves _(-1000)_

The generic caves we currently have, with their 3D cave biome distribution.

## The Mantle _(-15k to -20k)_

A scorching hot hellscape made up of the tough "pyrolite" stone.
There is a lava pool at the bottom of this layer.

## green jungly area?? _(-25k to -30k)_

In the goldilocks zone between the Mantle and Cold Core, there's a huge, lush, ancient jungle.

## Cold Core _(-40k)_

A frozen, icy wasteland.


## The Dropoff _(-48,125)_

A small layer of the world that appears to be crumbling into The Void.

## The Void _(-48,500)_

An abyss separating the surface world from The Root.

## The Root _(-50,000)_

A evil, twisted landscape of inverted caverns.
<details>
<summary>Original issue</summary>

Me, @IntegratedQuantum , and @Argmaster were discussing how content should be distributed in caves leading up to The Root, and that some of the special cave biomes should be pushed deeper into the world.
Here are my ideas. They aren't perfect so let me know what you think.
There is still lots of gaps where nothing new appears, so be sure make suggestions. I will try to update this list as more ideas come.
I don't have depths for most of these so I'm not having to shuffle them around every time I change this.

## Surface Caves _(0)_
Features large cave entrances exposed to the surface which quickly thin out once underground. Not many large, open caverns here
In more temperate biomes (forest, grasslands) you will find stone caves with pools of water and some plant life: more small mushrooms, vines, mosses, etc. 
There are many relics of past spelunkers here- abandoned camps, small shelters, etc.
This includes your surface-biome-based caves (#556), such as:
- Sandstone caves
- Glacite caves
- Jungle caves
- Flooded caves

### Special Loot
- Large coal veins (coal seams?)
- Copper

## Standard Caves _(-1000)_

Similar to the default cave biome right now. You won't find much life growing here.
Ruins of ancient cities can be found.
You can also start to find some of the basic cave biomes here:
- Stalagmite caves
- Marble caves
- Ice caves
- Giant mushroom caves

## Ferrous Caves _(-?)_
Wide, flat caverns made of ferrock which have been stripmined previously. A thick crimson fog fills this area. 
You can find many abandoned mineshafts here. Be cautious of explosive traps from old blast mining operations!

### Special Loot
- Large iron veins
- More sulfur veins

_(I would like another layer in between here, or at least some breathing room with regular caves.)_

## Frozen Chasm _(-?)_
It's so cold that this whole layer has frozen over. Features large open caves with slippery ice.

### Special Loot
- More silver
- Cyan, aqua, blue glowcrystals (rare)

## Waterfall Caves _(-?)_
With the heat increasing as we go deeper, the ice from the above layer is melting downwards, creating many waterfalls. Emphasis on verticallity in these caves.

### Special Loot
- Aqua, blue, indigo glowcrystals (rare)

## Underground Ocean _(-?)_
A large, open cavern filled with water. Thanks to thermal vents some life is surviving here.

### Special Loot
- Copper returns in large veins
- Blue, indigo, viridian glowcrystals (rare)

_(I would like another layer in between here, or at least some breathing room with regular caves.)_

## Tropical Caves _(-?)_
Humid, lush caves with ancient plants and creatures. This is a sweet spot where the water and warmth allows complex life to thrive.

### Special Loot
- An exclusive wood type?
- Large sulfur veins
- More plentiful coal
- Lime, green, viridian glowcrystals (rare)

## Deep Caves _(-?)_

Caves will become more hostile, with small pools of lava or magma patches in the base biome. 
This is where the extra special cave biomes appear:
- Classic crystal caves (random colours)
- Crystal forest
- Lava caves
- Giant Glimmergill caves

## Frostburn Caves _(-?)_
Magical caves with both lava & ice. This layer would be _especially_ dangerous and provide a good challenge to the player.

### Special Loot
- More gold veins
- Magenta, pink, red glowcrystals (rare)

## Basaltic Caves _(~-38,000)_
Basalt is now the main stone type. More lava, more magma.
There are industrial structures here made of an old, rusted metal. They seem more advanced than what you have seen before.
You cannot access the next layer through caves- you must mine through the mantle.

### Special Loot
- More diamonds
- White, grey, crimson glowcrystals (rare)

## The Mantle _(-40,000)_

A scorching hot hellscape made up of a tough "mantle" block which will take a powerful pickaxe to mine.
There is a lava pool at the bottom of this layer.

### Special Loot
- Yellow, orange, red glowcrystals (rare)

## The Dropoff _(-48,125)_

A small layer of the world made up of voidstone that appears to be crumbling into The Void. Try not to fall!

### Special Loot
- Dark grey, black, indigo glowcrystals (rare)

## The Void _(-48,500)_

An abyss separating the surface world from The Root.

## The Root _(-50,000)_

A evil, twisted landscape of inverted caverns. It's got some good loot!

</details>

Also, I just noticed the similarity to Minecraft.
Minecraft Steve begins in the Overworld, uses some witchcraft to get to the Nether, then collects enough cursed stuff to earn a one-way ticket to the End dimension. The Overworld is generally friendly aside from the monsters at night, the Nether is largely hostile in terrain and in monsters, and the End is devoid of life and terrain. Cubyz does the same thing environmentally but with a smooth transition. The surface/caves begin friendly, then slowly heat up until they turn into lava, then the threshold is crossed and the player falls through the void.
I don't know why this is such a powerful structure. My guess is that the first half of the game increases the challenge and the heat as if they are preparing the player, and then drops the player into the void to face the end of the world. At the end of the world there are no safety nets (literally), and the player must face the challenge on their own to demonstrate their mastery. Alternatively the End/Root symbolizes the "ascension" of the player as a cosmic power within the game.
Point being, there is a narrative around Cubyz and Minecraft terrain and we should play into that.

// ISSUE TITLE: Allow easier packaging for distributions and building from source // ISSUE REPORT: Currently cubyz is very difficult to package by linux/bsd distribution maintainers. I would like to put to you three suggestions that would make it a lot easier to package cubyz: 1. Use a released version of zig for each release of cubyz. This will mean a distribution maintainer will be able to use an already packaged version of zig if available on the system, making a special versioned build of a compiler just to compile a single program is not ideal. I'm not even sure the source of development versions of the zig compiler are available indefinitely, a packager would likely have to snapshot it from the zig source repo, not viable on most distros. 2. Allow building using system libraries. Dependencies like GLFW and freetype are packaged by the distribution maintainer and may include patches and configuration to make them work correctly on that system. Cubyz vendored version of GLFW doesn't work on my system for example because it is built without wayland support. 3. // ISSUE TITLE: Escaping the Underground [IDEAS]
// ISSUE REPORT:
The underground of Cubyz is really big, and escaping from a deep cave without the ability to teleport would genuinely be a reason for many to quit a world. Here's some ideas regarding escaping the caverns.

---------------------
> ### Boss Battle
> You want to escape this cave? Beat this boss and he'll transport you back to the surface. The deeper you go, the harder the boss will be. It's a risk/reward thing, in a way. You can scale back up the cave, wasting your time but guaranteeing a safe exit, or you can face this boss, risk your life and belongings, but save your time. He can be summoned by "eggs" that are littered around the caverns.
---------------------
> ### Tubes
> A long, one-way tube you can go into that connects to the surface. You can put the end of these tubes at depth increments of 250, forcing the player to go deeper before they can escape. Due to the long nature of these tubes, they can be followed to allow the player to find the end of it.
---------------------
> ### Thermal Vent
> Step on this block and you'll be shot up! These things are littered around caves, making them both a hazard and a useful tool for escape.
---------------------
> ### Gateway
> Yeah, you know End Gateways? Just that, but in caves. It's teleportation, but it's better than having to climb a million miles. Get this: they can only be used once. Next time you go down in the cave, prepare to find a new gateway; you have to go deeper and deeper each time.

For now, we can leave ipv6 out of the address display, i guess, due to the possible confusion. > And there are still many devices that do not support IPv6 at all. this is not true, all devices manufactured in the last 20 years will do just fine with an ipv6 connection (even an ipv6-only connection), and any OS updated in the last 20 years will do so too (even windows 7 has IPv6 enabled by default). the only bottleneck are ISPs that think theyre too fancy to deploy it (and a few routers that have it disabled by default for some reason), of which there are fewer and fewer. see https://www.google.com/ipv6/statistics.html#tab=ipv6-adoption for stats on that, and these are a pretty pessimistic estimate that doesnt quite match my experience - most people i talk to have ipv6. here is a thinkpad from 2005 with 32-bit windows 7 passing an ipv6 test with 10/10 points: ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a200907d-3b82-4798-8008-6471a60d5b68) ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6b612932-ec60-4748-a4dc-1a127241675e) > Since I don't know how to detect carrier grade NATs, I'd need to use IPv6 for everything which reduces the bandwidth (IPv6 headers are bigger). you can also default to ipv6 and switch to ipv4 if that doesnt work, or the other way around. one way to accomplish this is outputting both ipv6 and ipv4 in a single base32 value, which the other side can enter. cubyz can then find whatever works. symmetric NAT / NAT4 (what CGNAT often is - this is the actual issue with it, not that its carrier grade) can also be detected by having two STUN servers, and checking whether using the same egress port on the client will result in the same egress port being shown in both STUN results (if not, NAT is symmetric and thus ipv4 traversal impossible). So what do you propose then? Two separate sockets on the server? Seems like it would be rather annoying to implement and maintain (and thanks to Windows, the network code needs a lot of maintainance). > symmetric NAT / NAT4 (what CGNAT often is - this is the actual issue with it, not that its carrier grade) can also be detected by having two STUN servers We already do that, the reason for creating this issue was that there was a cgnat (somewhere in the UK) where it didn't detect symmetric NAT but connecting was still not possible. I couldn't find a reason for it and I just blindly hoped that IPv6 could fix it. [USER COMMENT] > less than 50% supported devices seems pretty bad this statistic is the number of devices that Actively Prefer ipv6 over ipv4 for requests. this is part of why i consider the stat so pessimistic. > We already do that nice > I couldn't find a reason for it and I just blindly hoped that IPv6 could fix it. well, it can, if both sides have it. > So what do you propose then? Two separate sockets on the server? a socket listening on [::0]:47649 will automatically also receive ipv4 connections, at least to my knowledge. as a result, thats what i usually use. im not sure if you consider this adequate of course, as you expressed concern over MTU, which is 24 bytes less in udp6 than in udp4 as far as i can tell [USER COMMENT] > So what do you propose then? If i remember correctly you could connect to an IPv6 server with an IPv4 client, Looking at one of my old (c++) projects, i got this line, that might help you: ```c++ int no = 0; setsockopt(handle,IPPROTO_IPV6,IPV6_V6ONLY,(const char*)&no,sizeof(no)); ``` (same code for windows and for linux) I tested it with an IPv4 client: ``netcat 127.0.0.1 `` , connecting to my IPv6 server and the server interpreted the client's ipv4 as ``::ffff:127.0.0.1`` , but haven't tested it with two different machines yet (i.e. not localhost). // ISSUE TITLE: (0.0.1) Torch light is too dark
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Steps to Reproduce and Observed Behavior

This is not just "git gud"; it's an accessibility issue. The first night was so dark that I can't do anything, even with my screen brightness turned all the way up. Even when planting torches on the second night, it's hard to see things, even when they are in close proximity to the torches.

**Expected behavior:**

At minimum, I would like to be able to place down torches and not strain my eyes/get a headache when trying to see around them. The Hades game for Luanti has the same problem: torches are way too dark. IMO brightness is the wrong lever for lighting tiers; it's much better to do lighting tiers by distance instead.

It would be nice to at least see at night without needing torches.

Longer term, I would also expect a brightness slider, since there's no one-size-fits-all solution here. [Gamma correction](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/116) has been mentioned as one possible way to implement this.

But it's also possible there's some bugs in the graphics engine too, which are rounding down colors to zero at night. Or brightness is on a linear scale when it should be on some order-of-magnitude scale. Given how unhelpful increasing screen brightness was, I wouldn't rule that out.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
If I'm understanding this correctly, this can be solved with a combination of #225, #116, and #2134.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> which are rounding down colors to zero at night. 

This is intentional, although not necessarily on the surface. Only darkness that is meaningful is true 0 black darkness that cannot be lit up with raising gamma. If you want to see all the time just disable daylight cycle, why bother if nothing changes with the night?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
night brightness was addressed in #2444
The remaining issue here, as far as I understand, is the brightness of torches, so I adjusted the title accordingly.

// ISSUE TITLE: Parallelizing the server update thread // ISSUE REPORT: The world should be divided into units of 2×2×2 chunks. This allows parallelizing them using 8 independent passes, such that each chunk event can access it's surrounding chunks without blocking. Here visualized in 2d (which requires only 4 passes): There are two disadvantages that I see with this: 1. When a player moves far enough out of a chunk, we need to prevent further actions until the next tick to avoid accessing data currently used by another thread. This will add some delay to certain actions, however I think it should be fine for the most part. 2. World edit is going to be a problem, since the individual commands first need to be split into chunks, and then at the end they need to be recombined to store its undo operation. I think the best way to handle this would be to put a limit of 1 world edit command per player per tick. However I think we can afford to do this, and the advantage, being able to parallelize the server (and this could also be easily extended to multi-processor systems) with little locking, is well worth it. extracted from #1744 // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] So, which systems fall into "update thread"? [MAINTAINER COMMENT] player actions, block updates and other events, worldedit, entity updates, itemdrop updates, projectile updates. Basically anything that runs on the server except for bookkeeping and terrain generation. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] So inventory ops aren't really directly covered by this are they? They are not placed directly in world. Unless? Unless you think about block entities. What about workbenches and chests? They are implemented very differently. Could be completely irrelevant, but I am still struggling to get the big picture. Updates are still done every frame, right? [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > So inventory ops aren't really directly covered by this are they? Yes they are, they fall under "player actions". > They are not placed directly in world. They will be placed at the location of the player who does the interaction, so the radius of interacting with chest inventories would be limited to 32 blocks. > Updates are still done every frame, right? This is all about the server update. The server has no frames.

// ISSUE TITLE: Noise-controlled patches for biome structures // ISSUE REPORT: ### Description Structures can generate in a uniform way, like they do right now, or they can generate with variable density. ### Parameters/fields - .fullDensityThreshold - f32, 0 to 1 (the noise value at which the density of the structure spawning is at full) - .minDensityThreshold - f32, 0 to 1 (the noise value at which the density of the structure spawning is at its minimum) - .minDensity - f32, 0 to 1 (the minimum density multiplier that the structure will spawn at) - .scale - f32 (the scale of the noise) - .seed - u64 (seed for the noise, useful if you want 2 structure patches to line up. if left blank, will be random) - .invert - bool (invert the output of the noise, useful if you want a structure to only spawn where another isnt) It could also have a more complex or simpler behavior, whatever people think would work best. I think it would also make sense to have structures generate uniformly if none of these parameters are specified. ### Examples With minDensity at 0 and fullDensityThreshold == minDensityThreshold, structures either generate or dont. With minDensity == 0, fullDensityThreshold == 1, and minDensityThreshold == 0, structure density goes smoothly from full to to nothing. #### Clearing in a birch forest with flowers #### Limestone boulder patch #### Other ideas - Groves of trees in the grassland biome instead of trees being everywhere - Clusters of multiple types of flowers/mushrooms - Potentially less patchy snow in in snowy biomes Overall, i think this would be a good way to add a lot more variety and make biomes less monotonous. // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I am not sure we need that. For the use cases you have listed, I am pretty sure you can implement them with a creative mix of sub biomes, smaller and bigger patches and maybe sbbs. Use cases you listed are quite vague and it sounds a bit like nice to have rather than solving a concrete problem. Please elaborate on what [problem you are trying to solve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XY_problem). [USER COMMENT] it's not something that's "needed", it's just something that would nake the world feel more interesting to explore since biomes are big. i feel like this is a better system than using a billion sub-biomes and weird uses of sbbs, and much easier for addon creators to learn. it also shouldn't be too hard to implement, shouldn't make generation much slower, and won't cause any breaking changes. basically i just think it'd make it a lot easier to make more varied biomes. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > For the use cases you have listed, I am pretty sure you can implement them with a creative mix of sub biomes, smaller and bigger patches and maybe sbbs. Just because it's possible to implement them that way, doesn't mean that it is the right solution. What I like about the proposed system is that it would allow you get a wider variety of large-scale shapes within a single biomes without having to do much (and, more importantly, without taking as many resources).

// ISSUE TITLE: Better random ticks
// ISSUE REPORT:
## Problem

Random ticks (https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/issues/77) do not simulate when chunks are unloaded. Adventuring far from your base won't progress any of your crops. I think this is a problem since Cubyz is very adventuring focused

## Solution

**Solution A:** Instead of just progressing when randomly ticked, these blocks could store the time they were last ticked and then when ticked again use the delta to compute how much they should progress.
The time could be very large grain (ex, the day counter) to reduce the number of bits required

**Solution B:** Chunks store the time they were last unloaded. When chunks are loaded, they tick each of their blocks and provide the duration of time the chunk has been unloaded.

I prefer **B** since it has a much lower memory requirement and wouldn't(?) be mutually exclusive with other rotation modes. It might negatively impact chunk loading performance though. I would like to try implementing it but wanted to make an issue first to see if I'm on the right track or not

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Chunks store the time they were last unloaded. When chunks are loaded, they tick each of their blocks and provide the duration of time the chunk has been unloaded.

This is how Terraria does it!

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This can already be implemented if necessary using block entities. Although the system might be missing integration between ticking and block entity hooks, not sure. It is definitely missing an more appealing API because a lot of behavior has to be duplicated when implementing data transfer beyond inventory data.

// ISSUE TITLE: [MODS] Mod registry design // ISSUE REPORT: ## Introduction By registry we mean online server like catalogue of trusted mods (mod is a 3rd or 1st party source code that extends Cubyz engine with additional content) that were verified by 3rd or 1st party. The purpose of the registry is to move the responsibility of reviewing moda code from non-tech savvy users to those who are used to working with code. This does mean that players have to trust the 3rd party that verified this code, but in open source it is inevitable to rely on trust at some point in dependency tree, you are not able to verify everything yourself, thus we consider this approach a fair compromise. Because Cubyz is an open source project we won't be investing time in designing the registry or modding interface in a way that gives closed source mods or registries way to restrict access to source code and we will ignore any issues that request such features. ## Assumptions - Players should be able to add online registries they trust in launcher and they should seamlessly merge into one catalogue of mods - Registry should contain information about what Cubyz versions they support, who made them, where to download the source code from and the hash of the source code to verify that it didn't change since review - Registry may contain multiple versions of same modification so we should have version resolution algorithm that picks latest version compatible with Cubyz version you have ATM - Registry should not contain mod source code itself - Registry should be cached locally - Registry should be versioned - Launcher should allow you to view what was added to registry and what was removed - Mod distribution format should allow both assets and source code to be identified and downloaded - Adding Mod with version X should disallow re-publishing that version with different sources code (preferably at all) to ensure stability for mod packs and to reduce risk of supply chain attacks. - Players when joining modded servers must be informed that is is modded, what are the mods and where they come from - Players when joining server should be offered an option to automatically download the mods and add their sources to the registry - For 3rd party registries that are not already added to launcher trusted registry list we should always display a warning message about potential consequences of installing malicious software - We should include 1st party registry by default in launcher and we should maintain a very limited 1st party registry, mostly with features we discussed for core game but were discarded or implemented in significantly changed way - We should ensure that source code linked in registry does not disappear, so registry should have built in tools for managing is on its own, but separately from metadata. - Regardless of the registries launcher should support direct installation of mods from zip archives in predefined format, possiby with dedicated file extension and metadata attached. ## Questions - Do we want to allow proper dependency trees to allow library mods or should people figure that out on their own? - Should we use git or other VCS or design the protocol ground up? Biggest advantage of git at the moment is that there is a fee infrastructure offered by GitLab, GitHub etc, but it may change anytime. - Should we store mod versions as Zip archives next to the registry package metadata? There were cases where people pulled their source code from registries, either out of spite or for righteous reason which was very disruptive. - Should we use Python, Postgres and Containers or alike stack to create proper server infrastructure for uploading, storing and transferring mods? - Should we look for existing package manager infrastructure to rely on? // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > Do we want to allow proper dependency trees to allow library mods or should people figure that out on their own? No, dependencies should be resolved by the modpack creator, I believe this is also how it's handled in minecraft. > Biggest advantage of git at the moment is that there is a fee infrastructure offered by GitLab, GitHub etc, but it may change anytime. Note that all of these services also allow direct source download as tar.gz or zip. > Should we store mod versions as Zip archives next to the registry package metadata? There were cases where people pulled their source code from registries, either out of spite or for righteous reason which was very disruptive. I think this should be in the responsibility of the registry maintainers. Third party maintainers could just fork the mods in question and add a link to the fork in their mod list. This has the advantage of also preserving the entire history and might make easier for other people to take over development of a particular mod. > Should we use Python, Postgres and Containers or alike stack to create proper server infrastructure for uploading, storing and transferring mods? No, it should be possible to host a registry on github. E.g. I hosted a java maven repository for some of Cubyz's java dependencies at one point, and it was very convenient that I could do so without hosting my own dedicated server. [USER COMMENT] > Do we want to allow proper dependency trees to allow library mods or should people figure that out on their own? Yes, but have the launcher figure the dependency tree and download the dependency mod or mods. > No, dependencies should be resolved by the modpack creator, I believe this is also how it's handled in Minecraft. I think mods should specify that it requires a different mod for it to work. As you (_IntegratedQuantum_) mentioned, Minecraft the mod doesn't load if you don't have its dependency installed and shows you that (see attached screenshot). > Should we use git or other VCS or design the protocol ground up? Biggest advantage of git at the moment is that there is a fee infrastructure offered by GitLab, GitHub etc, but it may change anytime. I think GitHub is too big now to go and charge users. I mean they do offer paid tiers, and I think that's all what they're going to do and have enough money from that. > Should we use Python, Postgres and Containers or alike stack to create proper server infrastructure for uploading, storing and transferring mods? We could make a repo that has multiple json files that have all of the information about the mod like where the source code is at and where the launcher should download from and the name of the mod or that can be taken from the name of the json but the json could contain the description of the mod. And if we do this then this could be 1st party mods as we would need to approve a PR to add a mod to the registry.

// ISSUE TITLE: A data transmission and processing system for traps and puzzles // ISSUE REPORT: ## Requirements - Data transmission, this could for example be a wire(or wireless) connection from a lever to a door. - Data validation, this could be a bunch of logic gates, analog circuitry or actual code. - Measurement devices(levers, pressure sensors, buttons) - Output devices (doors, secret doors, lights, arrow traps) - Time utilities (delay, clock, timer) ### Additional ideas: - toggleable blocks: they are only visible and solid if they get a signal. This would be an easy alternative for piston doors and it would allow making time-limited parkours in dungeons ## Possible implementations ### Redstone Pros: - It doesn't require additionaly GUI overlays - It is observable in action - It can be fun to try to figure out how to integrate redstone into a confined space Cons: - It is expensive for the client: The mesh needs to update every time the signal changes - It is expensive for the server: A redstone tick needs to update a lot of blocks - It has high latency: Redstone ticks are rare and complex calculations require many ticks - It can clutter your buildings if you don't know all the tricks, like using slabs to transmit a signal upwards - It would be quite complicated for procedural dungeon generation to generate traps without relying on pre-built structures. ### Integrated Circuits and Wireless Transmission Essentially you could attach a circuit board to each block, like a lever or a door. Each circuit board is a square grid, with inputs(e.g. lever state) and outputs(e.g. door state). The player can place circuit elements(wires, bridges, logic gates) inside a special GUI. These actions would consume items from the player's inventory. There are wireless receiver and transmitter components. These could be paired up using the same oscillator crystals. Additionally there should be pair up a sender with a receiver to avoid limiting the system too much. Pros: - Efficient: no mesh updates, boards could be compiled to simple code and lookup tables. - No extra space required - Easier to debug, each circuit could be tested in the GUI. - You can hack the puzzle if you brute-force the frequency crystal of the door. This fact could be used to make new puzzles as well. Cons: - Less immersive than redstone - Connections are invisible(maybe a tool could make them visible?) - How to introduce it to the player? - Is it as fun to use as redstone? ### String A string has two states: under tension and loose. Two strings attached to the same endpoint can be used as an OR gate. A NOT gate can be created using a small weight. The weight tensions the output string, unless the input string lifts it up. Pros: - String logic could be built in real life - It doesn't require additionaly GUI overlays - It is observable in action, by changing the color of strings that are under tension - Strings can believably go through walls, so it shares the advantages of the wireless approach - There might be some fun interactions when players can break strings inside of dungeons - Doesn't need mesh updates, except from updating the string colors Cons: - Collisions could be a problem: Without collisions it would be less immersive, but collisions on the other hand would be expensive, and would tension the strings which can have unforeseen consequences, maybe even make circuits unusable. - It would still be somewhat expensive for the server, but maybe less expensive than redstone. - It can absolutely clutter your buildings, maybe multi-colored strings could help - How does the string interact with ambient light? - Is it as fun to use as redstone? ### Slime Mold (visually based on Physarum polycephalum) The mold has two states: idle/sleeping and eating, when eating the texture of the entire organism is animated as nutrients are transferred throughout it. When eating it can exert some force on objects, which allows it to e.g. open a door or push away food from another instance (→not gate). Overall this makes it act kind of like redstone, except it can stick on wall and ceiling, and it's alive (be careful not to give it too much food at once, or your circuit breaks). Pros: - same as redstone - more immersive than redstone, it fits better into the world of Cubyz - placing on walls - being alive would allow other fun interactions (be careful where you place it, or it will eat it and expand) Cons: - expensive (same as redstone) // DISCUSSION: [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I would also take into consideration a system similar to Create Mod mechanics with cogs and belts, It had a great success in Minecraft world. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] That's not a direction I want to go in. I don't want Cubyz to turn into an automation game. I just want some simple logic for traps and puzzles in dungeons. [USER COMMENT] Maybe something like portal's wire system? Have no length limit for connections and instead cache all points where the wire connects to a powerable block. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I suggest that you guys check out Allumeria's wiring system. It's very smart. Basically you can bind an activator (such as a button or pressure pad) to an activatee. No wires needed. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Does it have logic though? E.g. can you make 2 switches toggle the same light source? Also I cannot check it out, because it doesn't work on linux. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] Yes, it's a simple A and B connection. Basically wires that are invisible. [USER COMMENT] Portal has OR gates, we could always make AND gates and XOR gates as well. It's a very simple system where you can attach wires to flat surfaces and they glow when powered. The system isn't accessible outside of the level editor in portal, but the basic mechanics would work in cubyz [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I would like to mention that we should also choose based on what fits the world and the lore. I think neither "WLAN cables" nor glowing Portal wires really fit the world we have built so far, which so far is neither hi-technological nor magical, and honestly I think this is the right place to be. [USER COMMENT] Yeah, overall I think string would make a good option. Having actual pulleys to move doors instead of pistons could be cool. Hydraulics are also an option, potentially in the form of pipes embeddable in blocks filled with water or a different liquid. [USER COMMENT] Pulleys could be pretty unique. If we have climbable ropes you could extend or retract them on a pulley to keep others out of your treehouse or something [USER COMMENT] That would be fun! I don't think I've seen a game that does that before, besides the create mod for Minecraft to some extent. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] > That would be fun! I don't think I've seen a game that does that before, besides the create mod for Minecraft to some extent. Actually, Better Than Wolves did this before Create! In fact it was probably inspired by BTW. Anyways pulleys are fun (smile, smile) [USER COMMENT] > > That would be fun! I don't think I've seen a game that does that before, besides the create mod for Minecraft to some extent. > > Actually, Better Than Wolves did this before Create! In fact it was probably inspired by BTW. > > Anyways pulleys are fun (smile, smile) True, I've never gotten very far in BTW so I forgot about it lol [MAINTAINER COMMENT] BTW has peak design honestly, it's a big inspiration for me. What's fun about it is that it starts off as a survival game, then slowly transitions into automation. Takes a lot of genuine hard work to get to that point. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] I have expanded the list with slime mold, which is my new favorite. [USER COMMENT] I really really like that, it feels very Cubyz. I'm curious how it would look in action [MAINTAINER COMMENT] In action, it would go glorp glorp [USER COMMENT] What about a hybrid approach, Redstone fails in a few categories because of the way it's built your slime mold doesn't have to. As a plus honestly the slime mold could be cool, could make little wooden plant stakes, but built for slime that encourages the slime to grow in a direction. The mold could have the affectation of being electrical(Think electric eels), could hurt the player if you wanted making the circuit open or perhaps adding shorting mechanics(Might be a little feature creep-ish but hey) In this instance I will outline a few definitions real quick. Source - Origin block/item of power Transmission - Connecting block/item Trigger - Recipient of power Let's look at the two biggest problems plaguing Minecraft's redstone. - Recursion. - Block Updates Recursion itself I think is infinitely easier to solve than what Minecraft has, it relies on the ideal that each dust updates it's power level based on it's neighbors in a strong armed strength check. It does this on tick as well and completely fails to use a network chain. How we can solve it. Instead of having recursion happening on the individual instance we make both Source and Trigger owners, with them taking on a master/slave role when connected. When the source propagates it goes from the source out, each level of the Transmision Network receives one update from the source being it's Strength, this is now a child of the Source or Trigger if a source is not attached. If a new Transmision is connected it takes from the current strength to determine it's MaxPossibleStrength and CurrentStrength. So for i=Source -=Dead Transmission o=Trigger x(Where x is a value between 0 and 8)=LiveTransmission Lets look at a full sized full power circuit i1234567o Each distance has a value that's held and retained by it's owner only, it will only update that instance if A it receives power from null or B it's at 0 litteraly. We can slow down the updates intentionally to get cool emergent behavior. Now lets say we broke the 3 block in slime mold so it looked like this i12 4567o On a set timer event(Triggered via an update call from the destroyed block)each tick would slowly dwindle the strength I.E. the Transmission is dying because it isn't connected to it's nutrients i.e. the source. Which would continue until death, deactivating the Trigger. You could also reverse this order for program sake and make it easier to track. i12-3456o ->Trigger is notified to take on a master role(Because the trigger is not a source it cannot retain the power so deprecation begins one block at a time all of this is handled only on the trigger though each block is only concerned with on/off state)(Could get really fancy here and utilize bitpack data so 1 byte equals one network chain) i12 2345o i12 1234o i12 x123o i12 xx12o i12 xxx1o i12 xxxxo ->Trigger loses power Block updates, can be tougher. I haven't dug to much into your individual updates but this would have to happen as a do once procedure. [MAINTAINER COMMENT] [USER COMMENT] I don't think the mold itself should push blocks, rather it be an external block like pistons in m word game [USER COMMENT] The problem with mold itself pushing and pulling blocks is that it becomes physically impossible to control and make things with. What happens if mold tries to push or pull the block that it is on?

Formatting as a process is on a different abstraction level than memory acquisition, so are all the mentioned data structures and should not be mixed.

// ISSUE TITLE: Global event/message system
// ISSUE REPORT:
Alright, I hesitated to make this issue, as this idea is not particularly polished, but I guess you can never be fully prepared.

So, I was thinking if we can fix some of our existing issues using [Observer](https://refactoring.guru/design-patterns/observer) pattern or for those who prefer real life examples, Web browser HTML/JS event listeners.

 A general idea goes like this:
- There is a globally accessible event API
- Any code can use the event API to attach a callback that waits for even identified by ID
- Any code can use the event API to trigger an event
- When event is trigger, event system calls all the callbacks previously attached to that event ID
- Rinse and repeat.

In our case, event system would likely have to be a separate thread, events would be moddable structs and callbacks would accept anytype pointers.

What can we solve this way?
- General idea is to be able to communicate between systems without needing to call callbacks from inside a system.
- ~~**If** events can cross world|UI barrier, we can make player movement inform windows that they should close or open.~~
- **If** events can cross server|client barrier, they can be used as high level general purpose communication interface, adding a moddable layer of abstraction on top of it, eg. to spawn particles or play sounds.
- With a little bit of creativity, we can use hierarhical events to get more control over what events are leaking through UI to game actions (eg. as now we crouch when you hold shift in UI)
- Events can be extensively used to give mods heads up about stuff happening in the game, like joining world.
- Moreover, if events are passed through listeners hierarchically and can be marked as handled to stop propagation, then mods can override the default behavior of the game without actually changing any of the code.
- It should be easier to decouple systems from each other, as now there is a widely available mediator that handles the communication
- **This can help with block entity API.**

There are some challenges tho:
- With flexibility comes performance cost, event handlers have to be registered, unregistered and finally called (not even mentioning synchronization) and we are talking about thousands. Great power, great responsibility. That said, event system can be quite easy to monitor, so at least there is some hope.
- Crossing world|UI and server|client barrier is tied together and in some cases it is not as simple as broadcasting the event to everyone, so a server side filtering of listeners must be possible (flags?). We shouldn't just send every event over the network either (god forbid sending keyboard strokes!).
- Spooky action at a distance (jk) - there will be no clear call path to follow, instead one will have to use IDs of events to find where things are used, rely on good code structure etc. Again, great power, great responsibility.
- ... (TBD)

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
I like it! This is also very commonly used in modding APIs for minecraft (forge, spigot, etc.)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> If events can cross world|UI barrier, we can make player movement inform windows that they should close or open.

How would such an event work? Would it need to check every frame for each player if the player has any position events attached to it?

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Let's say that:
- There is a global Event system, one on client side, one on server side.
- All existing events are know at system startup through comptime modding.
- For each event we create a `CircularBufferQueue` containing Events of given type. 
- For each event we create a `ListUnmanaged` containing callbacks for given event type.
- There is a constantly running "event loop" thread, that uses round robin or alike to loop through all the queues and if queue is not empty, it pops first event and loops through all callbacks for this event, calling them with event as parameter.
- When event is triggered, it is assigned to corresponding queue, unless event is marked as distributed through network using some **metadata**, then it is send to client(s), where it ends up in client event system and corresponding queue, to be executed in future.

This implies that callbacks are always called from different thread than the system they will interact with. 
There is more metadata that would probably have to be attached, since some of the events will be critical to execute when world is shut down, others can just be discarded. Also some events have higher priority and should get more CPU time than flat round robin would give them.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Event handling is inherently unordered and delayed, so it cannot be used for security critical stuff. It a solution for stuff that would otherwise make the game look buggy. For security critical stuff, there is Sync in place, at least I think it would be great to use it this way.

This is basically using event system as a search method and that is not a purpose of event system. Event system is an abstract form of asynchronous one-to-many communication (one-to-one in some cases).

[USER COMMENT]
I thought of a good idea for vehicles, if you're aware of the valkyrien skies mod for minecraft, and what people have done with it and the create mod, I think being able to build your own transportation like that is a much better option, and encourages exploration, as players will have the possibility to build mobile bases, transportation networks like trains for example, maybe even flying vehicles, which would make building and accessing  bases high up on mountains much easier.

This would ofc require allowing making blocks that can move smoothly and collide with the world, as well as components like wheels and more to actually allow building moving structures. Something easier to do in the short-term though, might be adding ziplines like previously suggested, requiring 2 zipline poles but also some rope (crafted maybe from leaves or other plants) dependent on how long the line is, and your speed and direction being dependent on the slope, and extra poles could be added to extend a zipline and allow it to change direction.

However, rope could also be used for platforms and pulleys. the player can build a small structure up to 5x5x5, and place 4 "hooks" these hooks are assumed to be placed at the 4 corners of a square/rectangular structure, and any blocks within those bounds (up to 5x5x5, and no floating blocks) are considered to be part of the platform, the player then attaches rope to the hooks, and to a "pully" block that must be positioned above the center block(s) of the platform. the pulley block can be connected to a winch block (placed on the platform) with rope, which must be manually operated, and can raise/lower the platform, the winch block can also store rope, and the amount of rope decides how far the platform can be lowered. A second winch can also be placed off of the platform and connected to the pulley, allowing a player to pull the platform back up from the top. The platform could also function as a test for moving blocks, as it would be built by the player, and would have to smoothly move down and up, and be able to make sure blocks like chests keep their data, but wouldn't require anything more complicated like physics.

Overall I think player-buildable vehicles would work really well in cubyz, encouraging both exploration and creativity, as players can build their own vehicles, and bring their bases with them, making it easier to explore, since players who build a base are probably going to be less likely to explore very far, but having a mobile base fixes this issue, and if multiple types of vehicles are available (land, flying, tunneling, underwater, above water) player will have tons of freedom to explore.

Though for underground vehicles, I think maybe automated mining and mining vehicles should be allowed to facilitate easier underground exploration and creation of long tunnels for transportation, but maybe automated mining solutions yield drastically less resources, destroying most ores and blocks they break completely. Which would also help prevent lag from items if someone tried to mine away a large area for building with vehicles.

// ISSUE TITLE: We must not use vectors in `extern` structs
// ISSUE REPORT:
They don't have a guaranteed memory layout, see https://github.com/ziglang/zig/issues/24179#issuecomment-2985759158

this is a requirement for #1583 

Problematic places:
- [x] `QuadInfo` in `models.zig`
- [ ] `ChunkData` in `chunk_meshing.zig`
- [x] `Particle` in `particles.zig` (this one is even missing the extern declaration)

I think the best solution for now is probably just to use e.g. `align(16) [3]f32` instead of `Vec3f` and coerce them in all use-cases

// DISCUSSION:

could zig make some optimization by altering layout of vector? if so, only vectors passed into pipeline should be changed to aligned fix-width array.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
This is only about extern structs, which are used to communicate with e.g. glsl, where we must, at all cost, have a fixed memory layout.

[USER COMMENT]
It seems like most vector is decomposed like here before passing into glsl:
https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/blob/9c33f8184d50c5e4ff712a11d98e7981a1cf3846/src/renderer.zig#L314-L315

However, vectors could be passed in as a pointer to aligned buffer:
https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/blob/9c33f8184d50c5e4ff712a11d98e7981a1cf3846/src/renderer/chunk_meshing.zig#L213

Therefore only some vector should be changed to given layout, which is passed into APIs ending with a `v` like `glUniformxxv`

// ISSUE TITLE: Can (and should) we avoid the hue shifting that happens at the edge of light sources?
// ISSUE REPORT:
![Screenshot at 2025-01-07 23-55-10](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a0e93646-1226-4e8a-9a95-80f34ab40e7a)
It could be possible to achieve this with 4 color channels instead of 3.
With 3 color channels there are 8 base colors: black, white, red, green, blue, yellow cyan and magenta.
4 color channels would allow having 16 base colors, currently we have 20 colors in the colored block palette, however some of those might be considered darker variants of others, like crimson, indigo, viridian and the grays.
I don't know if it will actually produce a sensible color space, but it is at least mathematically possible to create a linear color space with 4 color channels from the remaining 15 colors.

As for performance, adding another color channel for lighting should be pretty cheap, the only expensive bit would be the conversion before uploading the mesh, but that's tiny compared to the cost of interpolating the lights.

Configuration might be a bigger problem though, as absorption and emission values would need to use an unintuitive 4 color code.

// DISCUSSION:
[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
In my opinion: yes. Lighting should be improved in the future.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
You could probably have RGB plus a darkness scale

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]

Flood fill lighting only works if all channels are treated independently.

We can change this image to adjust the color space.

Here's an example that would make the lights more vibrant and polarizing.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/029b926f-e9af-4f62-aa58-4d72ea8463bf)
Another example that would make colored lights vibrant, but desaturate as they dim.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
The root issue remains: There is no way to distinguish a faint red light from e.g. a far away orange light.
So matter how you tone-map them, you will either end up turning dark red into orange, or orange into dark red, or both of them into a faint gray.

Also the issue post already contains a solution that has a better chance of working.

[USER COMMENT]
Currently the issue is that orange color channels (31, 15, 0) gets attenuated to (16, 0, 0), which is indistinguishable from dark red.

First of all, we could halve the light radius or add an extra bit to make orange into (31, 15, 0) + (32, 32, 32), so that the attenuated orange will be (48, 32, 32) and still distinguishable from red. Then we face the other hue shifting issue which is due to the ratio r/g not being constant, which we can solve with math or tonemapping.

Alternatively, even if we leave the hue shifting, it might be worth offering tonemapping for artistic control over how it happens. As ikabod offered, we could desaturate colors at the fringes. We could also map dark red/fringe red (16, 0, 0) into something like (12, 4, 0) to and explicitly make the tradeoff of supporting red and orange lights.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> First of all, we could halve the light radius or add an extra bit

A light radius of 15 is too small in my opinion, especially given the scale of our caves, even 32 is still too small sometimes.
And adding an extra bit would mean that light data wouldn't fit into a single u32 anymore, making it more expensive to load in the vertex shader (and yes I have measured in the past that there is a significant difference when adding even just a single additional memory load).

> We could also map dark red/fringe red (16, 0, 0) into something like (12, 4, 0) to and explicitly make the tradeoff of supporting red and orange lights.

This does not work as then a red light source (32, 0, 0) would hue shift into orange.

[USER COMMENT]
> This does not work

Well, either we are forced to hue shift orange into red, or we give ikabod options to have it some other way.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Or we implement what I suggested, and avoid any such compromises.

[USER COMMENT]
@codemob-dev strictly speaking storing the data in a different format wouldn't do anything. More generally, it might be possible to modify the floodfill to handle hues better. I explored this on discord but I didn't get very far.

The mathematics of floodfill lighting is very limited. It's required that given a region of cells, recalculating the floodfill function at some block would give the same value again. Otherwise, it might get computationally ugly to find the fixed points, or it might not even converge. Something naive like "average the two colors together" wouldn't work, and you can verify this for yourself with two lights 2 blocks apart from each other.
Averaging hues via HSV wouldn't change the convergence issue. It seems like a fundamental problem.

Standard floodfill works with 3 color channels, the R, G, and B, and essentially makes a voronoi diagram (`self=min(neighbors)-1`) for each channel independently, and then sums the results back together. The only other sane function I could find would be to update the voronoi diagrams in sync with each other, but that prevents color mixing altogether. Though, it would be cool if you could find another one. There might be potential with a function that averages only conditionally on distance, but I can't get that to work consistently.

[USER COMMENT]
The main issue is that the color channels decrease at the same rate. HSV could make the math easier because you could just decrease the value, the only problem is color mixing.

[USER COMMENT]
The trouble is that most interesting ideas make light sources interfere with themselves. You can't make the math easier until it's valid in the first place.

// ISSUE TITLE: Standardize addon metadata file
// ISSUE REPORT:
### Description

Let's standardize addon metadata file. Proposed name is `addon.zig.zon`. Such file would be a good reference point for discovering addons programatically. Additionally it would serve as a place for meta information like:
- author names
- maintainer names
- web page link
- github link
- addon image path
- ...

---

### Why the proposal?

Its awkward to explain how addons have to be unpacked and placed into worlds asset folder. Navigating to Cubyz world directory is not that hard, but its still something that has to be done outside of the game and thus generates some extra friction. What truly can lead to problems is unpacking stage. Depending on how the addon archive was structured and how the extraction software was configured user can end up with asset directories being nested once, twice or even scattered around zip archive. At the same time a trivial importing mechanism could handle all of that trivially. With clear reference point in form of metadata file it becomes trivial to determine root of the addon and hence to programmatically unpack it properly and place in correct place. Super convenient, no friction solution.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
Should these metadata files be used to inform registries (#1545)? If so, I think it would be a good solution since there will likely be many 3rd party registries and it would be tedious to update separate metadata in each one. Additionally, in the future, I think it would also be nice to include more human readable information, like a longer description with images. Maybe a `readme` key that can point to a markdown or plaintext file.

Some extra keys:

- Name (human readable instead of the addon's namespace)
- Short description
- Supported Cubyz versions
- Addon version (+ changelogs?)
- Metadata version

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We should be careful not to rely too much on weblinks.

> Its awkward to explain how addons have to be unpacked and placed into worlds asset folder. Navigating to Cubyz world directory is not that hard, but its still something that has to be done outside of the game and thus generates some extra friction.

All I see here is the need to add a gui option for this, i.e. a drag and drop location where you can drag and drop the addon tar.

> Depending on how the addon archive was structured and how the extraction software was configured user can end up with asset directories being nested once, twice or even scattered around zip archive.

I would say this is even a disadvantage. We should enforce that all addons internally have the same structure, since that makes it easier for other humans to read it when they "take inspiration" for their own addons.

// ISSUE TITLE: Allow addons to disable/block recipes
// ISSUE REPORT:
This would be helpful when an addon wants to remove recipes from the base game.
The motiviation for this was that someone wanted to make an addon with different torch variants per wood type.
But I think this is useful further for addon that want to change the progression path by blocking certain methods of obtaining items.

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
id personally approach this by first granting recipes their own identifiers

{
     .identifier = "cubyz:recipe_marble_tile", // yes u can do .auto here u will get the same thing
     .inputs = .{"cubyz:marble"},
     .output = "cubyz:marble_tile",
}


if a addon does this


{
     .identifier = "cubyz:recipe_marble_tile",
     .inputs = .{"cubyz:marble"},
     .output = "cubyz:marble_tile",
}


there will be a error that the recipe already exists however addon has a few options about this

**1. overwrite**
overwrite basically allows the addon to overwrite what currently is loaded if it is

{
     .mode = .overwrite,
     .identifier = "cubyz:recipe_marble_tile",
     .inputs = .{"cubyz:marble"},
     .output = "cubyz:marble_tile",
}


**2. disable**
disable basically lets an addon disable the recipe no questions asked however this does not account for a different addon overwriting as it will be allowed even if a recipe is disabled


{
     .mode = .disable,
     .identifier = "cubyz:recipe_marble_tile",
}


**3. modify**
cubyz will try to join original recipe with the new one based on modifications without needing to overwrite the whole recipe
however if the recipe is disabled this will not be allowed

{
     .mode = .modify,
     .identifier = "cubyz:recipe_marble_tile",
     .inputs = .{"3 cubyz:marble"},
}

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Recipe ids are going to be a mess, especially with #1824

Recipes can already be uniquely identified by their behavior, so you would just disable it by restating it exactly:
```zig.zon
{
    .disable = true,
    .inputs = .{"cubyz:marble"},
    .output = "cubyz:marble_tile",
}
```
If you want to modify it, you just reinsert the new, modified recipe:

```zig.zon
{
    .inputs = .{"cubyz:marble", "cubyz:chisel"},
    .output = "cubyz:marble_tile",
}
```

// ISSUE TITLE: [ECS] Entity Component System requirements
// ISSUE REPORT:
## Description

This issue is intended to aggregate concrete requirements for Entity Component System implied by existing and planned entity designs.
It will focus on identifying and describing implementation details and on driving the discussion about best way to implement each of the features to avoid fragmentation of the thought process across multiple issues.

## Entity Types

- Muffalo #1431 

## Entity Type Static Data

- tags `Tag`
- model `[]const u8`
- texture `[]const u8`
- collisionModel `[]const u8` (?)
- maxHealth `f32`
- maxEnergy `f32`
- baseSpeed `f32`
- baseBuoyancy `f32`
- baseFriction `f32`
- drops `[]struct{ id: []const u8, count: []const u8, chance: f32 }`
- furlessModel: `[]const u8`
- furlessTexture: `[]const u8`
- furReGrowTime `f32`
- canBeSheared `bool`
- AI configuration, different states, motion flags, interaction models?

## Systems

- [ ] AI, covered more in-depth by #86
- [ ] Rendering
- [ ] Physics
- [ ] Eating
- [ ] Fur re-growing (?)
- [ ] Effects (#624, #651) either separate or all in one system (?)

## Components

- [ ] EntityType `EntityData = struct{ id: u16 }`
- [ ] Health `EntityData = struct{ maxHealth: f32, currentHealth: f32 }` `EntityTypeData = struct{ maxHealth: f32 }` 
- [ ] Energy `EntityData  = struct{ maxEnergy: f32, currentEnergy: f32 }` `EntityTypeData = struct{ maxEnergy: f32 }`
- [ ] Position `EntityData  = struct{ position: Vec3d }`
- [ ] Rotation `EntityData  = struct{ rotation: Vec3f }`
- [ ] Velocity `EntityData  = struct{ velocity: Vec3f }`
- [ ] PhysicalConstants `EntityData  = struct{ friction: f32, buoyancy: f32 }`
- [ ] Inventory?
- [ ] TimeSinceSheared `f32` (?)
- [ ] Ablaze `struct{ timeLeft: f32, damage: f32 }`
- [ ] Poison `struct{ timeLeft: f32, damage: f32 }`
- [ ] Saturation `struct{ timeLeft: f32, damage: f32 }` (?)
- [ ] IsHerdLeader `struct{}` tag component (?)
- [ ] Damaged `source: union(enum){fall, entity, player, effect}` (?)
 
## Comments

- Entity variants for now are planned as separate entities so we don't have to integrate too much logic into entity configuration loading
- Entities could use tag system to recognize entities of same type
- Do we want to separate entity hunger from entity energy? Didn't we just rename hunger to energy?
- Instead of one huge AI system, we could try creating many small systems we could combine, instead of creating basically second layer of ECS inside AI. We could have `PanicWhenAttacked`, `PanicWhenSheared`, `GroupsWithTag`, `FollowsWithTag` and alike and simply have them as separate components, many of the behaviors are boolean based and you will need boolean Components to detect them anyway.
- Should each of the system specify its own set of static parameters so you don't have to duplicate parameter declarations and explicitly pass them around?

## Links

Related to: #86 
Related to: #87
Related to: #88
Related to: #90
Related to: #112
Related to: #242
Related to: #624
Related to: #651 
Related to: #778 
Related to: #884
Related to: #890 
Related to: #1406

// DISCUSSION:
[USER COMMENT]
I think we could do without a furless model by just changing the outside texture to being blank.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
That is very optimistic, it assumes there is outside and inside texture. IMO model should also shrink a bit to give impression of fur being thick.

To make it as extensible as eg Unity ECS it would have to allow specifying multiple models at once and then fur system would add/remove one of them, no idea how it would recognize the correct one, but that is doable.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
We talked about premature optimization yesterday, and I thought about it some more:
I think the most likely bottleneck is going to be the O(n²)/O(n·m) type interactions, such as entity-entity collision (→pushing entities with your body), itemdrop collection, entity projectile collision, path finding/collision detection, networking (→selecting which entities to send to which player, compressing them based on the player position).
The most important thing is that we optimize those, and most of them can be turned into quasi O(n) by storing a list of entities for each chunk and iterating on entities on a chunk by chunk basis for these systems.

The main entity system is less relevant for performance and in my opinion it's best to go with the simplest approach now and optimize things later.
We definitely do want an ECS in the future, simply to allow for some kind of modding, so we should definitely go with that.

I think grouping components together is premature, since we do not know where the bottlenecks are, and there is a good chance that it will actually hurt us in the end if there are too many dffferent archetypes of entities or if there are too many hashmap lookups. This is something to look into in the future, once we know what kinds of components we will need and where the bottlenecks are.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
Ok, so what does that mean? Do we go with SparseSet approach with no grouping? Do we just hard-code the entities without ECS for now?

In general we are quite unlikely to choose most optimal system without knowing all the requirements and those may never be a final set, always something may change. So just pick something, we can do a rewrite later, it's inevitable, just scale of the rewrite will be different (smaller, if we were lucky to guess correct set of requirements)

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> Ok, so what does that mean? Do we go with SparseSet approach with no grouping? Do we just hard-code the entities without ECS for now?

I think the SparseSet approach is the easiest to work with right now, and I think it wouldn't be too difficult to group the performance-critical components together once we've identified them.

Also I think we should set up the system so it allows using any data structure, not just hardcoding everything to use a SparseSet. This would allow us to use different strategies for different components one step at a time, making future rewrites easier.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
External access to all the entities should be done via ECS with getComponent function with storage being completely opaque implementation detail, you should be able to replace SparseSet with any other data structure for storage (eg. More generalized SparseSet) at any time. I would argue to keep SparseSet simple (as described in books), you are already preparing for optimizations, you don't have solid proof, will be needed. 

Manual grouping of components is optimization you can only apply with full control over components, if you rely on it, mods will suffer from terrible performance, you are anticipating, if they have their own performance critical set of components (all-to-all interactions in eg. AI). IMO this invalidates complicating SparseSet now because this optimization does not satisfy requirements we already know about.

How many components are you willing to manually group and how generic will be the group name? If you just group all the components together, then it no longer makes sense to make ECS.

[MAINTAINER COMMENT]
> with storage being completely opaque implementation detail

Yes, exactly. It should be an implementation detail of the individual component.

> I would argue to keep SparseSet simple

Yes, I absolutely agree, we shouldn't make the SparseSet more complicated. All I'm asking for is to allow components to use different storage options.

> Manual grouping of components is optimization you can only apply with full control over components

No, it's an optimization you can apply with partial control over the performance critical components.

> if you rely on it, mods will suffer from terrible performance, you are anticipating,

If I don't rely on it everything is slow.
But honestly I don't think that dynamic grouping is going to be much better. Sure for iterating over entities in order when we know what pool they are in, it is going to be a lot more efficient, but for all-to-all interactions, where we want to check entities based on proximity, we would still need to jump around between different pools all the time.

> IMO this invalidates complicating SparseSet now because this optimization does not satisfy requirements we already know about.

Yes I agree, we shouldn't do this now, and we shouldn't ever do that, we should just allow choosing different data structures.

I think the memory should be managed by the component, not by some complicated generic system on top of it.

Important discussion about attachable detachable components implementation

[USER COMMENT]
I might recommend using [flecs](https://github.com/SanderMertens/flecs), potentially through [zflecs](https://github.com/zig-gamedev/zflecs). I have some experience writing my own wrapper in both Zig and C#, but it's been a while. Rolling your own ECS will become difficult as you realize you need more features, and there's many common pitfalls that you really only understand once you get there. (The basics are surprisingly simple to implement, and can be enough for simpler games.)

Here's some reasons why flecs is good:
- I believe it to be professional-grade.
- It's been around for a long time.
- It's seeing continued active development.
- The author is very friendly, and is actively working with other ECS projects.
- It has built-in REST API and a webserver you can enable for inspecting and monitoring.
- It has a great C API that from my experience is very easy to integrate with Zig.
- It has support for relationships between entities that can be queried easily.
- It has support for parent/child hierarchies but also custom ones, through relationships.
- Multi-threaded systems without much effort, because their definitions keep track of what they access/modify.
- The query language is very powerful, reminds me of database queries. (Well, an ECS is part database.)
